<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Nanoelectronics — The Definitional Framework

**Nanoelectronics** is the branch of applied physics and engineering that deals with the **design, fabrication, and characterization of electronic devices and circuits at the nanometer scale**, typically where at least one critical dimension (channel length, gate oxide thickness, or junction depth) is **below 100 nm (10⁻⁷ m)**. As feature sizes approach the order of the de Broglie wavelength of electrons (~10 nm in silicon), classical semiconductor device physics must be supplemented or replaced by **quantum mechanical transport theory**, ballistic transport models, and band-structure engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> *"Nanoelectronics refers to the use of nanometer-scale structures and phenomena — including quantum confinement, single-electron tunneling, and molecular electronics — to construct functional electronic devices whose operation exploits physical effects that emerge only when critical dimensions shrink below approximately 100 nm."*

## 1.2 MOSFET — The Workhorse of Modern Electronics

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is a four-terminal voltage-controlled unipolar device in which the **conductance of a surface inversion layer (channel) between two heavily doped regions (source and drain) is modulated by the electric field applied through an insulated gate electrode**.

A MOSFET is therefore fundamentally a **field-effect** device: no current flows through the gate (gate is insulated by SiO₂ or a high-κ dielectric), and the **gate voltage $V_{GS}$ controls the source-to-drain current $I_D$**.

### 1.2.1 The Two Polarity Families

| Family | Channel Type | Carriers | Built-in Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NMOS** | n-channel (induced in p-substrate) | Electrons | $V_{TH} > 0$ (enhancement) |
| **PMOS** | p-channel (induced in n-substrate) | Holes | $V_{TH} < 0$ (enhancement) |

> [!NOTE]
> In CMOS technology (the foundation of all modern digital ICs), every logic gate is realized using complementary pairs of NMOS and PMOS transistors. The scaling of MOSFET dimensions is what drives Moore's Law.

## 1.3 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Nano-Tap"

Imagine a **water tap (the gate valve) controlling the flow of water (current) through a pipe (channel) between two reservoirs (source and drain)**.

- The **gate voltage** is the *force you apply to the tap handle*. A small twist doesn't open the tap — you must overcome a minimum "stiction" (this is the **threshold voltage $V_{TH}$**).
- Once open, the harder you twist (larger $V_{GS} - V_{TH}$), the more water flows. This is **transconductance $g_m$**.
- The **pipe diameter** is set at fabrication time — it cannot be changed afterwards. This represents the **W/L ratio** baked into the lithography.
- In a **nano-tap**, the pipe becomes so thin that water molecules start leaking through the wall (**gate oxide tunneling**), the tap's rubber seal gets brittle (**hot carrier degradation**), and water starts flowing even when the tap is technically closed (**subthreshold leakage / DIBL**). These are precisely the **sub-100 nm challenges** we will examine.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are mandatory references for all derivations in this module:

- **Electron charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ **mV** (commonly rounded to **26 mV**)
- **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm**
- **Relative permittivity of Si** $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7$
- **Relative permittivity of SiO₂** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration in Si at 300 K** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**
- **Electron mobility in Si** $\mu_n \approx 1350$ **cm²/(V·s)**
- **Hole mobility in Si** $\mu_p \approx 480$ **cm²/(V·s)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Memorize the value $V_T = 26$ mV at room temperature (300 K). The subthreshold swing of a classical MOSFET has a fundamental lower limit of $S = 60$ mV/decade — derived directly from this $V_T$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Structure of an n-Channel Enhancement MOSFET

A standard **NMOS** consists of the following layered structure (top to bottom):

1. **Metal gate (or polysilicon / metal stack)** — the control electrode.
2. **Thin gate dielectric (SiO₂ or high-κ)** — the insulator of thickness $t_{ox}$ (now down to 1–2 nm in advanced nodes).
3. **p-type silicon substrate (body / bulk)** — typically doped at $N_A \approx 10^{15}$ – $10^{17}$ cm⁻³.
4. **Two n⁺ source and drain regions** — heavily doped at $N_D \approx 10^{19}$ – $10^{20}$ cm⁻³, separated by a physical channel length $L$ (drawn) and effective electrical channel length $L_{eff}$.

The four terminals are: **Gate (G)**, **Source (S)**, **Drain (D)**, and **Body/Substrate (B)**. In most digital circuits, the body is tied to ground (for NMOS) or $V_{DD}$ (for PMOS).

## 2.2 MOSFET Band Diagram — The Heart of the Device

The **equilibrium (zero bias) energy band diagram** of an NMOS, taken as a vertical cut from gate metal through oxide into the p-type silicon, is the conceptual foundation for understanding $V_{TH}$.

- The **metal work function** $\phi_M$ and the **silicon work function** $\phi_S$ differ. In an n⁺-polysilicon gate NMOS, $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S < 0$.
- The Fermi level $E_F$ in the metal aligns with $E_F$ in the semiconductor at equilibrium.
- The **valence band edge** $E_V$ and **conduction band edge** $E_C$ in the p-substrate are bent **upward near the oxide interface**, indicating band bending due to the built-in potential $\phi_{bi} = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$.

### 2.2.1 The Three Biasing States (from Band Bending)

| Gate Bias Condition | Surface Band Bending | Surface Carrier Type | Device State |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ | Moderate upward bending | Holes (accumulation) | **Subthreshold / Cutoff** |
| $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$ | Bending $= 2\phi_F$ | Carrier concentration = $N_A$ | **Threshold of inversion** |
| $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ | Strong upward bending | Electrons (inversion layer) | **Strong inversion — channel ON** |

> [!NOTE]
> The inversion layer is an extremely thin (~2–10 nm) sheet of mobile electrons confined against the Si/SiO₂ interface. Its sheet density is $n_s = C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})/q$ carriers per cm².

## 2.3 MOSFET Operation — The Three Regions

A long-channel NMOS in DC steady state operates in one of three regions, depending on $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$:

### 2.3.1 Region 1 — Cutoff (Subthreshold)
Condition: $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$. The channel is not formed. Only a tiny **subthreshold leakage current** flows:
$$I_D = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{\eta V_T}\right)$$
where $\eta > 1$ is the subthreshold ideality factor.

### 2.3.2 Region 2 — Triode (Linear / Ohmic)
Condition: $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **and** $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. The channel is continuous from source to drain, and the device behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor**:
$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$
For small $V_{DS}$, this reduces to the linear ohmic form $I_D \approx \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS}$.

### 2.3.3 Region 3 — Saturation (Active)
Condition: $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **and** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. The channel is **pinched off** near the drain, and $I_D$ becomes (ideally) independent of $V_{DS}$:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$
In reality, **channel length modulation (CLM)** introduces a finite output resistance:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

## 2.4 Threshold Voltage — The Master Parameter

The **threshold voltage** is the gate-source voltage at which a conducting inversion layer just forms at the Si/SiO₂ interface. It is derived by solving Poisson's equation in the depletion region and applying the boundary condition that the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration $N_A$.

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Component-by-component breakdown:**

- **Flat-band voltage** $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$
   - $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$ is the metal-semiconductor work function difference.
   - $Q_{ox}$ is the fixed oxide charge (typically positive, ~10¹⁰ cm⁻²).
- **Surface potential term** $2\phi_F$ — twice the Fermi potential; represents the band bending required to reach inversion.
- **Body factor / depletion term** $\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ — represents the voltage drop across the depletion region.
- **Oxide capacitance per unit area** $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$.

**Fermi potential definition:**
$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

**Body-effect modified threshold (substrate not grounded):**
$$V_{TH}(V_{SB}) = V_{TH,0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$
where $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox}$ is the **body-effect coefficient**.

## 2.5 Key Small-Signal MOSFET Parameters (Exam-Relevant)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition (Saturation) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS} = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ | Gain of $V_{GS} \to I_D$ |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $\partial V_{DS}/\partial I_D = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ | Finite output impedance in saturation |
| Body transconductance | $g_{mb}$ | $(\gamma/2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}) \cdot g_m$ | Effect of body bias on $I_D$ |
| Intrinsic gain | $A_{int}$ | $g_m r_o$ | Max voltage gain of a single transistor |

## 2.6 Challenges Going to Sub-100 nm MOSFETs

As channel lengths shrink below 100 nm, the device physics that worked for micrometer-scale MOSFETs begins to break down. The challenges fall into two categories:

### 2.6.1 Technological (Process & Manufacturing) Limits
- **Lithography resolution limits** — optical lithography approaches its diffraction limit; EUV lithography is required.
- **Gate oxide tunneling** — $t_{ox} < 1.5$ nm allows direct electron tunneling from gate to channel. Mitigation: **high-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂, $\kappa \approx 25$) to achieve equivalent oxide thickness (EOT) without physical thinness.
- **Shallow junction formation** — source/drain extensions must be ultra-shallow (~10 nm) but still provide low series resistance.
- **Dopant fluctuations** — at sub-50 nm scales, only a few hundred dopant atoms exist in the channel, leading to statistical $V_{TH}$ variation.
- **Interconnect delay (RC delay)** — Cu interconnects and low-κ dielectrics become essential.

### 2.6.2 Physical / Fundamental Limits
- **Short Channel Effects (SCE)** — $V_{TH}$ decreases as $L$ shrinks due to charge sharing between gate and source/drain depletion regions.
- **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** — high $V_{DS}$ lowers the source-side potential barrier, increasing subthreshold leakage.
- **Velocity saturation / ballistic transport** — carriers reach $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s; the $I_D \propto (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ law breaks down, replaced by $I_D \propto (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.
- **Hot carrier injection (HCI)** — high lateral fields near the drain inject carriers into the oxide, causing long-term device degradation.
- **Quantum mechanical effects** — inversion layer becomes a 2D electron gas with discrete subbands; channel thickness is no longer negligible.
- **Subthreshold swing floor of 60 mV/decade** — classical CMOS cannot switch faster than this at room temperature.

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Compact Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you must know for Module 1 problems. Memorize units and limiting cases.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 2 | $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$ | Fermi potential | V; p-substrate |
| 3 | $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ | Threshold voltage (long-channel) | V |
| 4 | $V_{TH}(V_{SB}) = V_{TH,0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Body-biased threshold | V |
| 5 | $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$ | Triode drain current | A |
| 7 | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | Saturation drain current | A |
| 8 | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | With channel length modulation | A |
| 9 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$ | Transconductance (sat.) | A/V or S |
| 10 | $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ | Output resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $S = (\ln 10) \cdot (kT/q)(1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox})$ | Subthreshold swing | V/decade |
| 12 | $S_{min} = 60$ mV/decade | Classical CMOS limit at 300 K | — |
| 13 | $E_{ox} = V_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | Oxide field | V/cm |
| 14 | $L_{eff} = L_{drawn} - 2L_D$ | Effective channel length | m or $\mu$m |
| 15 | $E_{sat} = v_{sat}/\mu_n$ | Critical field for velocity saturation | V/cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **backbone of SPICE circuit simulation**. Industry tools (Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, Mentor Eldo) numerically solve the square-law, body-effect, CLM, and subthreshold models on every node of a billion-transistor chip in order to verify timing, power, and noise.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage $V_{TH}$ for an n-Channel MOSFET

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Poisson's Equation in the Depletion Region

Consider the one-dimensional Poisson equation along the depth direction $x$ (perpendicular to the Si/SiO₂ interface), in the depletion region of the p-type body:

$$\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} = \frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_{si}} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

Here we have assumed **complete depletion of mobile carriers** in the region $0 \le x \le x_d$, and used the depletion approximation that $\rho = qN_A$ (ionized acceptors) is the only charge density.

### 3.1.2 First Integration — Electric Field

Integrating once from a point inside the neutral bulk ($x > x_d$, where $\mathcal{E} = 0$) to a depth $x$ within the depletion region:

$$\int_{\mathcal{E}(x)}^{0} d\mathcal{E} = \int_{x}^{x_d} \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}dx'$$

$$\mathcal{E}(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}(x_d - x)$$

At the surface ($x = 0$), the maximum field magnitude is:

$$\mathcal{E}_s = \frac{qN_A x_d}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

### 3.1.3 Second Integration — Surface Potential

Integrating $\mathcal{E}(x) = -d\phi/dx$ from $x$ to $x_d$:

$$\phi(x_d) - \phi(0) = -\int_{0}^{x_d} \mathcal{E}(x)\,dx$$

The bulk potential is taken as the reference: $\phi(x_d) = 0$. The surface potential is then:

$$\phi_s \equiv \phi(0) = \int_{0}^{x_d} \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}(x_d - x)\,dx = \frac{qN_A x_d^2}{2\varepsilon_{si}}$$

### 3.1.4 Solve for Depletion Width

Inverting the above:

$$x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\phi_s}{qN_A}}$$

### 3.1.5 Depletion Charge per Unit Area

The total depletion charge per unit area is:

$$Q_d = qN_A x_d = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A\phi_s}$$

### 3.1.6 Condition for Strong Inversion

By definition, **strong inversion** begins when the surface electron concentration $n_s$ equals the bulk hole concentration $N_A$. From the Boltzmann relation for band bending $\phi_s$:

$$n_s = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{q\phi_s - q\phi_F}{kT}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad p_{bulk} = N_A = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{q\phi_F}{kT}\right)$$

Setting $n_s = N_A$ gives:

$$n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{q\phi_s - q\phi_F}{kT}\right) = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{q\phi_F}{kT}\right)$$

This simplifies to $\phi_s - \phi_F = \phi_F$, hence the **critical surface potential** is:

$$\phi_{s,crit} = 2\phi_F$$

The corresponding depletion width is:

$$x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F)}{qN_A}}$$

### 3.1.7 Apply the Gate Capacitor Voltage Balance

The gate-to-body voltage is distributed across the oxide and the semiconductor surface:

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + V_{ox} + \phi_s$$

The voltage across the oxide is related to the total charge by $V_{ox} = -Q_{total}/C_{ox}$, where $Q_{total} = Q_d + Q_n$ (depletion + inversion). **At the threshold point** $Q_n \to 0$, so $Q_{total} \approx -Q_d = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}$.

Setting $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$:

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Defining this as the threshold voltage:

$$\boxed{\,V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of each term:**
> - $V_{FB}$: align the metal Fermi level to the semiconductor Fermi level (work-function difference + oxide charge).
> - $2\phi_F$: bend the bands at the surface to invert the carrier type.
> - Third term: support the depletion charge that has been exposed by the band bending.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drain Current in the Triode Region (Gradual Channel Approximation)

The **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)** assumes that the lateral field along the channel ($y$-direction) varies much more slowly than the vertical field across the gate ($x$-direction). This allows us to treat the channel as a one-dimensional series of incremental MOS capacitors.

### 3.2.1 Local Charge Density at Position $y$ Along the Channel

At a point $y$ from the source, the local gate-to-channel potential is $V_{GS} - V(y)$, where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential. The local inversion charge per unit area is:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox}\bigl[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\bigr]$$

### 3.2.2 Drift Current Element

The drift current at position $y$ through the inversion layer (treated as a sheet of width $W$ and sheet resistance inversely proportional to $Q_n$) is:

$$I_D = -W Q_n(y) \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy}$$

(The negative signs cancel because $Q_n$ is negative and $dV/dy$ is positive toward the drain.)

### 3.2.3 Separate Variables

Rearranging:

$$I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox}\bigl[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TH}\bigr] dV$$

### 3.2.4 Integrate from Source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to Drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$)

$$I_D \int_{0}^{L} dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_{0}^{V_{DS}}\bigl[V_{GS} - V - V_{TH}\bigr] dV$$

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox}\!\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

### 3.2.5 Final Triode Equation

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\,}$$

### 3.2.6 Transition to Saturation

At the edge of saturation, the local inversion charge $Q_n$ at the drain end goes to zero, which mathematically requires $V_{GS} - V_{DS} - V_{TH} = 0$, i.e., $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} \equiv V_{OV}$ (the **overdrive voltage**).

### 3.2.7 Saturation Current

Substituting $V_{DS,sat}$ back into the triode equation, the $V_{DS}^2/2$ term combines with the linear term:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2\,}$$

### 3.2.8 Effect of Channel Length Modulation

Empirically, the saturation current is not perfectly constant with $V_{DS}$ because the **pinch-off point** recedes toward the source as $V_{DS}$ increases, shortening the effective channel. The empirical correction is:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where $\lambda \propto 1/L$ is the **channel-length modulation parameter**, empirically fitted from measured I-V curves.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor at 300 K has the following parameters:
- $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9\varepsilon_0$, $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³
- $\phi_{MS} = -0.95$ V, $Q_{ox}/q = 5\times 10^{10}$ cm⁻²
- $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n = 600$ cm²/(V·s)
- $V_{GS} = 2$ V, $V_{DS} = 3$ V
- $n_i = 1.5\times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³

**Find:** (a) $V_{TH}$, (b) $I_D$ (saturation), (c) $g_m$.

**Solution Step (a) — Compute $V_{TH}$:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14}}{10\times 10^{-7}} = 3.453\times 10^{-8}\,\text{F/cm}^2$$

$$\phi_F = \frac{0.02585}{1}\ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5\times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(6.667\times 10^5)$$

$$= 0.02585 \times 13.41 = 0.3467\,\text{V}$$

$$2\phi_F = 0.6934\,\text{V}$$

$$Q_{ox} = q \times 5\times 10^{10} = 1.602\times 10^{-19} \times 5\times 10^{10} = 8.01\times 10^{-9}\,\text{C/cm}^2$$

$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}} = -0.95 - \frac{8.01\times 10^{-9}}{3.453\times 10^{-8}} = -0.95 - 0.232 = -1.182\,\text{V}$$

Depletion term numerator:
$$\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)} = \sqrt{2 \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14} \times 1.602\times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times 0.6934}$$

$$= \sqrt{2.296\times 10^{-15}} = 4.79\times 10^{-8}\,\text{C/cm}^2$$

Depletion term divided by $C_{ox}$:
$$\frac{4.79\times 10^{-8}}{3.453\times 10^{-8}} = 1.387\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{TH} = -1.182 + 0.6934 + 1.387 = 0.898\,\text{V}$$

**Solution Step (b) — Compute $I_D$:**

Since $V_{DS} = 3$ V $> V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.102$ V, the device is in saturation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}(600)(3.453\times 10^{-8})(10)(1.102)^2 = 1.259\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A} = 125.9\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Solution Step (c) — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = (600)(3.453\times 10^{-8})(10)(1.102)$$

$$= 2.28\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A/V} = 0.228\,\text{mA/V}$$

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — MOSFET Physical Cross-Section & Terminal Architecture

This diagram maps the **physical cross-section** of an enhancement-mode n-channel MOSFET, showing the four terminals, the inversion channel, and the depletion regions.

```mermaid
graph TB
    n1["METAL GATE Terminal G"]:::gate
    n2["SiO2 GATE OXIDE Thickness tox"]:::oxide
    n3["INVERSION CHANNEL n-type W x L"]:::channel
    n4["n+ SOURCE Terminal S"]:::source
    n5["n+ DRAIN Terminal D"]:::drain
    n6["p-type SUBSTRATE Body Terminal B"]:::body
    n7["Source depletion region"]:::depl
    n8["Drain depletion region"]:::depl
    n9["Substrate contact at bottom"]:::body

    n1 --- n2
    n2 --- n3
    n3 --- n4
    n3 --- n5
    n4 --- n7
    n5 --- n8
    n7 --- n6
    n8 --- n6
    n6 --- n9

    classDef gate fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef oxide fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef source fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef drain fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef depl fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The gate (yellow) sits atop a thin SiO₂ layer (blue). When $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, an n-type inversion channel (green) forms just below the oxide, connecting the n⁺ source and drain (pink). The p-substrate (purple) contacts the body terminal at the bottom. The orange regions are the depletion zones around the source/drain p-n junctions.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — MOSFET Operating Regions Flow Logic

This block diagram describes the **decision logic** for determining the operating region of a MOSFET based on the bias voltages.

```mermaid
graph TB
    s1["START: Given VGS and VDS"]:::startNode
    qA{"Is VGS less than VTH ?"}:::decisionNode
    r1["REGION 1: CUTOFF / SUBTHRESHOLD<br/>ID approx 0<br/>Only subthreshold leakage"]:::cutoff
    qB{"Is VDS less than VGS minus VTH ?"}:::decisionNode
    r2["REGION 2: TRIODE / LINEAR<br/>ID = mu C W/L times VOV VDS minus VDS squared over 2"]:::triode
    r3["REGION 3: SATURATION<br/>ID = 0.5 mu C W/L times VOV squared times 1 plus lambda VDS"]:::sat

    s1 --> qA
    qA -- YES --> r1
    qA -- NO --> qB
    qB -- YES --> r2
    qB -- NO --> r3

    classDef startNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cutoff fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef triode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sat fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Always check the inequality $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ **first** to know whether you are in triode or saturation. Most exam problems hinge on correctly identifying this boundary, called the **saturation voltage** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV}$.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Sub-100 nm Challenges Hierarchy

A block-architecture flow mapping the major challenges encountered when scaling MOSFETs below 100 nm, separating them into **Technological** and **Physical** categories.

```mermaid
graph TB
    top["SUB-100 nm MOSFET CHALLENGES"]:::root

    tech["TECHNOLOGICAL LIMITS"]:::branchTech
    phys["PHYSICAL LIMITS"]:::branchPhys

    t1["Lithography diffraction limit"]:::leaf
    t2["Gate oxide tunneling below 1.5 nm"]:::leaf
    t3["Shallow S/D junction formation"]:::leaf
    t4["Dopant fluctuation and variability"]:::leaf
    t5["Interconnect RC delay dominance"]:::leaf

    p1["Short Channel Effect on VTH"]:::leaf
    p2["Drain Induced Barrier Lowering DIBL"]:::leaf
    p3["Velocity saturation and ballistic transport"]:::leaf
    p4["Hot Carrier Injection HCI"]:::leaf
    p5["Quantum confinement in channel"]:::leaf
    p6["Subthreshold swing floor 60 mV per decade"]:::leaf

    top --> tech
    top --> phys
    tech --> t1
    tech --> t2
    tech --> t3
    tech --> t4
    tech --> t5
    phys --> p1
    phys --> p2
    phys --> p3
    phys --> p4
    phys --> p5
    phys --> p6

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef branchTech fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef branchPhys fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model Question]
**Define threshold voltage in a MOSFET. List the three components that constitute the threshold voltage expression, and explain the physical origin of each.**

**Model Answer (10 lines, ~3-mark depth):**

The **threshold voltage $V_{TH}$** of a MOSFET is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting inversion layer (of carrier concentration equal to the bulk doping) at the semiconductor surface.

The standard expression is:

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

The three terms have the following physical origins:

1. **$V_{FB}$ (Flat-band voltage):** Aligns the Fermi level of the gate metal with that of the semiconductor body, accounting for work-function difference $\phi_{MS}$ and any trapped oxide charge $Q_{ox}$.
2. **$2\phi_F$ (Surface potential term):** The band-bending voltage required at the Si surface to invert the carrier type — i.e., to make the surface electron density equal to the bulk hole density $N_A$.
3. **$\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ (Depletion / body term):** The voltage drop across the gate oxide that supports the depletion-region charge $Q_d$ that has been exposed by the band bending.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is meant by the subthreshold swing of a MOSFET? State its classical minimum value at room temperature and explain why it is a fundamental scaling limit.**

**Model Answer (8 lines, ~3-mark depth):**

The **subthreshold swing $S$** is the inverse slope of the $\log_{10}(I_D)$ versus $V_{GS}$ curve in the subthreshold region. It is defined as the change in gate voltage required to change the drain current by one decade:

$$S = \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\log_{10} I_D)} = (\ln 10)\frac{kT}{q}\!\left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$$

At room temperature (300 K) and in the classical long-channel limit where $C_{ox} \gg C_{dep}$:

$$S_{min} = (\ln 10)\frac{kT}{q} \approx 60\,\text{mV/decade}$$

This is a **fundamental limit** because it arises directly from the Boltzmann distribution of carriers over the gate-controlled potential barrier. It cannot be circumvented by geometric scaling — it is set by thermodynamics. To break this limit, one must use **steep-slope devices** such as **tunnel-FETs (TFETs)**, **negative-capacitance FETs (NC-FETs)**, or **electromechanical relays**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the body term** in $V_{TH}$ — many students write only $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $\varepsilon_{si}$** — must be in F/cm (not F/m) when doping is in cm⁻³.
> 3. **Using $V_T = 26$ mV as an exact constant** — it is only valid at exactly 300 K; examiners may subtract marks if temperature is not specified.
> 4. **Confusing DIBL with short-channel effect (SCE)** — DIBL is a $V_{DS}$-dependent effect; SCE is a geometry-dependent effect.
> 5. **Skipping the gradual-channel approximation assumption** in $I_D$ derivations — always state it explicitly.
> 6. **Failing to mention velocity saturation** as the reason the square law breaks down for short-channel devices.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the threshold voltage expression for an n-channel enhancement MOSFET, clearly stating the boundary condition for the onset of strong inversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short-channel NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $W = 10\,\mu$m, $L = 0.5\,\mu$m, $t_{ox} = 8$ nm, $\mu_n = 500$ cm²/(V·s), $V_{TH} = 0.6$ V. The device is biased at $V_{GS} = 2$ V and $V_{DS} = 3$ V. Calculate the drain current, the transconductance, and the output resistance, assuming $\lambda = 0.05$ V⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Threshold Voltage Derivation — 7 marks:**

Begin with Poisson's equation in the depletion approximation:

$$\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}, \quad 0 \le x \le x_d$$

Integrate once with $\mathcal{E}(x_d) = 0$:

$$\mathcal{E}(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}(x_d - x)$$

Integrate again with $\phi(x_d) = 0$:

$$\phi_s = \phi(0) = \frac{qN_A x_d^2}{2\varepsilon_{si}}$$

Invert to obtain depletion width:

$$x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\phi_s}{qN_A}}$$

Depletion charge per unit area:

$$Q_d = qN_A x_d = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A \phi_s}$$

**Boundary condition for strong inversion:** the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration $N_A$, which corresponds to band bending of $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$.

At threshold, the gate voltage is partitioned as $V_{GS} = V_{FB} + V_{ox} + \phi_s$. With $V_{ox} = -Q_d/C_{ox}$ and $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$:

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Mark distribution (typical KTU scheme):**
- 2 marks: setting up Poisson and integrating.
- 2 marks: obtaining $x_d$ and $Q_d$.
- 1 mark: stating strong-inversion condition.
- 2 marks: assembling the final $V_{TH}$ expression.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14}}{8\times 10^{-7}} = 4.316\times 10^{-8}\,\text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the region:**

$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 2 - 0.6 = 1.4$ V.
Since $V_{DS} = 3$ V $> V_{OV} = 1.4$ V, the device is in **saturation**.

**Step 3 — Compute drain current (with CLM):**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{OV})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(500)(4.316\times 10^{-8})\left(\frac{10}{0.5}\right)(1.4)^2(1 + 0.05 \times 3)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(500)(4.316\times 10^{-8})(20)(1.96)(1.15)$$

$$= 4.86\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A} = 486\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute transconductance:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{OV}) = (500)(4.316\times 10^{-8})(20)(1.4)$$

$$= 6.04\times 10^{-4}\,\text{S} = 0.604\,\text{mA/V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute output resistance:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 4.86\times 10^{-4}} = 41.1\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Mark distribution (typical KTU scheme):**
- 1 mark: $C_{ox}$ correctly computed.
- 1 mark: correct region identification.
- 2 marks: correct $I_D$ formula and numerical evaluation.
- 1 mark: correct $g_m$ value.
- 2 marks: correct $r_o$ value with units.

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the physical origin of three major challenges encountered when scaling MOSFETs below 100 nm. For each, state one engineering mitigation strategy adopted in modern CMOS technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the operation of a long-channel MOSFET with that of a short-channel MOSFET (sub-100 nm). Use the concepts of velocity saturation, drain-induced barrier lowering, and subthreshold swing in your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Three Sub-100 nm Challenges with Mitigations — 7 marks:**

**Challenge 1 — Gate Oxide Tunneling:**
As $t_{ox}$ is reduced below ~1.5 nm, electrons can directly tunnel quantum-mechanically from the channel through the SiO₂ to the gate, producing an unacceptable gate leakage current (grows exponentially with $\sqrt{t_{ox}}$). This dramatically increases static power dissipation.
*Mitigation:* Replace SiO₂ with **high-κ dielectrics** (e.g., HfO₂, $\kappa \approx 25$, used from the 45 nm node onward) to obtain a small equivalent oxide thickness (EOT) with a physically thicker film, suppressing tunneling.

**Challenge 2 — Short-Channel Effect (SCE) and DIBL:**
In sub-100 nm devices, the gate loses electrostatic control of the channel because the source/drain depletion regions encroach into the channel. The threshold voltage drops, and the source-side potential barrier is lowered by high drain bias (DIBL), causing exponential rise in off-state leakage.
*Mitigation:* Use **multi-gate structures** (FinFET, Gate-All-Around / GAA nanowire FETs) to wrap the gate around the channel on multiple sides, restoring electrostatic integrity. Intel adopted FinFETs at the 22 nm node (2011).

**Challenge 3 — Hot Carrier Injection (HCI):**
Near the drain, lateral electric fields exceed $10^5$ V/cm. Carriers gain kinetic energy well above the lattice thermal energy ("hot" carriers). Some are injected into the gate oxide, where they become trapped, causing a slow but irreversible shift in $V_{TH}$ over time.
*Mitigation:* Engineer **lightly-doped drain (LDD)** extensions to spread the high-field region, and use **graded drain engineering**; also optimize biasing to keep $V_{DS}$ below the silicon impact-ionization threshold.

**Mark distribution:**
- 1 mark for the physical origin of each challenge (3 marks total).
- 1 mark for each correct mitigation (3 marks total).
- 1 mark for overall clarity and engineering context.

---

**(b) Long-Channel vs. Short-Channel Comparison — 7 marks:**

| Property | Long-Channel (L > 1 µm) | Short-Channel (L < 100 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport regime** | Drift-diffusion, mobility-dominated | Velocity-saturated, approaching ballistic |
| **$I_D$ vs. $V_{OV}$ law** | Square law: $I_D \propto V_{OV}^2$ | Linear law: $I_D \propto V_{OV}$ (saturation velocity limit) |
| **Threshold voltage** | Independent of $L$ (long-channel value) | $V_{TH}$ rolls off as $L$ decreases (SCE) |
| **DIBL effect** | Negligible | Significant — sub-100 mV/V in advanced nodes |
| **Subthreshold swing** | Approaches 60 mV/dec | Degraded (often 70–100 mV/dec) due to degraded $C_{ox}/C_{dep}$ ratio |
| **Gate control** | Strong, single-gate | Weak in planar — requires FinFET or GAA architecture |

**Velocity saturation effect:** As channel length shrinks, the lateral field $E_y = V_{DS}/L$ becomes very large ($\sim$10⁵ V/cm). The carrier drift velocity no longer grows linearly with field (as $v = \mu E$ would suggest) but saturates at $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s. The current becomes $I_D = W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) v_{sat}$, which is **linear in $V_{OV}$**, not quadratic. This is one of the most fundamental departures from long-channel theory.

**Drain-induced barrier lowering:** In short channels, the drain bias penetrates into the source-side depletion region, lowering the potential barrier that keeps carriers confined to the source. This barrier lowering $\Delta\phi_{DIBL}$ depends approximately linearly on $V_{DS}$, leading to a $V_{TH}$ that **decreases with increasing $V_{DS}$** — the opposite of the long-channel body effect.

**Subthreshold swing degradation:** In short channels, the capacitive divider ratio $C_{ox}/(C_{ox} + C_{dep} + C_{par})$ becomes unfavorable as parasitic capacitances $C_{par}$ (fringing, source/drain) increase. The subthreshold swing $S$ therefore rises above the 60 mV/decade classical limit, increasing static leakage exponentially.

**Mark distribution:**
- 2 marks for the comparison table.
- 2 marks for clear explanation of velocity saturation and its $I_D$ law change.
- 1.5 marks for DIBL mechanism.
- 1.5 marks for subthreshold swing degradation mechanism.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Nanoelectronics** = devices with critical dimensions below 100 nm, where quantum effects become significant.
- [x] **MOSFET** = voltage-controlled, insulated-gate, unipolar field-effect transistor — the building block of CMOS.
- [x] A MOSFET has **four terminals**: Gate, Source, Drain, Body.
- [x] NMOS uses electrons (faster, ~3× higher mobility); PMOS uses holes.

### MOSFET Band Diagram
- [x] Surface band bending of $2\phi_F$ marks the onset of strong inversion.
- [x] $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ — gate capacitance per unit area.
- [x] The inversion layer is a 2D sheet of charge with density $n_s = C_{ox}V_{OV}/q$.

### Operation
- [x] **Three regions**: Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{TH}$), Triode ($V_{DS} < V_{OV}$), Saturation ($V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$).
- [x] **Overdrive voltage** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is the designer's primary knob.

### Threshold Voltage
- [x] $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + Q_d/C_{ox}$ — memorize all three terms.
- [x] $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$ — Fermi potential.
- [x] Body effect: $V_{TH}$ **increases** with reverse body bias $V_{SB}$.
- [x] $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox}$ — body-effect coefficient.

### Drain Current
- [x] Triode: $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)[V_{OV}V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$.
- [x] Saturation (ideal): $I_{D,sat} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)V_{OV}^2$.
- [x] Saturation (with CLM): multiply by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.
- [x] Gradual-channel approximation is the **assumption** underlying both equations.

### Small-Signal Parameters
- [x] $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)V_{OV}$ (saturation).
- [x] $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
- [x] Intrinsic gain $A_0 = g_m r_o \approx 1/(\lambda V_{OV})$.

### Sub-100 nm Challenges
- [x] **Technological:** lithography, gate-oxide tunneling, shallow junctions, dopant fluctuations, interconnect RC delay.
- [x] **Physical:** SCE, DIBL, velocity saturation, HCI, quantum confinement, 60 mV/decade swing floor.
- [x] **Key mitigations:** high-κ dielectrics, FinFET / GAA multi-gate structures, LDD engineering, strained silicon, Cu/low-κ interconnects.

### Numerical Knobs
- [x] $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K; $S_{min} = 60$ mV/dec; $\varepsilon_{si}/\varepsilon_0 = 11.7$; $\varepsilon_{ox}/\varepsilon_0 = 3.9$.
- [x] $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 3$ in Si — this is why NMOS is preferred for high-speed logic.
- [x] $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s in Si.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Valuation Desk:** In every long-answer derivation, **always state your assumptions** explicitly — gradual channel approximation, depletion approximation, complete ionization, constant mobility. Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for correctly framed assumptions, even before the algebra begins.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Lengths, Scaling and Short Channel Effects, Channel Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Characteristic Lengths, Scaling and Short Channel Effects, Channel Length # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Characteristic Lengths in Nanoscale MOSFETs The **characteristic length (λ)** — also called the **natural length scale** or **scaling length** of a MOSFET — is the most fundamental geometric parameter that quantifies how far electrostatic potential perturbations (from the source/drain) can penetrate into the channel of a MOSFET. It is the single most important number that determines whether a transistor operates in the "long-channel regime" or suffers from **short-channel effects (SCE)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** The characteristic length λ of a MOSFET is defined as the length scale at which the two-dimensional potential distribution in the channel transitions from a smooth, gradual long-channel profile to a sharply varying short-channel profile. It is given by: > > $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si} \, t_{si} \, t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$$ > > where ε_si and ε_ox are the permittivities of silicon and gate oxide, t_si is the silicon body thickness, and t_ox is the gate oxide thickness. For a single-gate bulk MOSFET, the more general expression includes the depletion width: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\,t_{ox}\,X_{dep}}$$ For a fully-depleted SOI / double-gate device, the natural length is: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$$ For a **triple-gate / FinFET** (gate on three sides), the natural length shrinks further: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{3\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU Board:** A device is considered "long-channel" only when L ≥ 5λ. When L drops below 3λ, severe short-channel effects dominate and the device is essentially unworkable. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are filling a long rectangular swimming pool with water (the channel potential) while a friend pours water from two buckets at the far ends (source and drain electrodes). If the pool is very long compared to its width, the water level in the middle stays flat — the buckets cannot influence each other. This is the **long-channel regime**. Now, as the pool is shortened (scaling), the ripples from the two buckets begin to overlap in the middle, distorting the water level. When the pool length becomes comparable to the bucket-splash radius, the ripples mix violently. The "splash radius" is exactly the **characteristic length λ**. > [!TIP] > **Intuition Box:** λ is the "electrostatic reach" of the source/drain junctions into the channel. A small λ = good electrostatic integrity (S/D cannot easily control the channel). A large λ = bad — S/D can "see" through the channel and turn it on/off, causing leakage. ## 1.3 Scaling of MOSFETs **Scaling** is the systematic reduction of all physical dimensions and voltages of a MOSFET to achieve higher packing density, faster switching, and lower power dissipation per generation. It is the heart of Moore's Law. Two canonical scaling paradigms are mandatory for the KTU board: | Scaling Type | Dimensions (L, W, t_ox) | Voltages (V_DD, V_T) | Doping (N_a) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Constant Voltage Scaling (CVS)** | 1/α | **1 (unchanged)** | α² | | **Constant Electric Field Scaling (CES)** — Dennard | 1/α | 1/α | α | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CVS keeps voltages the same — easy to design but causes excessive electric fields and reliability failures. CES (Dennard scaling) maintains constant E-field, but power-supply scaling eventually failed around the 90 nm node, leading to the end of Dennard scaling and the multi-core era. ## 1.4 Short Channel Effects (SCE) — Definition A **short-channel effect** is any undesirable deviation of MOSFET behavior from the ideal long-channel square-law model, occurring when the channel length L becomes comparable to (or smaller than) the characteristic length λ. The six classical short-channel effects tested in the KTU exam: 1. **Threshold Voltage Roll-Off (ΔV_T)** — V_T decreases as L decreases. 2. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** — V_T decreases as V_DS increases. 3. **Hot Carrier Injection (HCI)** — high-field carriers injected into the gate oxide. 4. **Velocity Saturation** — carrier velocity no longer proportional to E-field. 5. **Subthreshold Swing Degradation** — subthreshold slope increases above 60 mV/decade. 6. **Punch-Through** — source and drain depletion regions touch, current flows uncontrollably. ## 1.5 Channel Length — Effective vs. Physical The **physical (drawn) channel length L** is the mask dimension. The **effective channel length L_eff** is shorter because the source and drain depletion regions encroach into the channel from each side: $$L_{eff} \;=\; L_{drawn} \;-\; \Delta L$$ where ΔL is the total depletion-encroachment length (~0.1 × L_j on each side, L_j being the junction depth). > [!NOTE] > **The Metallurgical Channel Length L_m** is the distance between the source and drain metallurgical junctions — the most physically meaningful length. In a long-channel device, L_m = L_eff = L. In a short-channel device, L_m < L. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Derivation of the Characteristic Length (Physical Reasoning) Starting from **Poisson's equation in 1-D** for the potential φ(x, y) in the channel: $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial x^2} \;+\; \frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial y^2} \;=\; \frac{q\,N_a}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$ A separation-of-variables solution of the form φ(x, y) = V(y) + U(x)·W(y) yields, in the subthreshold regime, an exponential decay of the potential perturbation in the channel direction x: $$U(x) \;\propto\; e^{\,\pm\,x/\lambda}$$ This directly defines λ. The length scale at which the lateral variation of potential matches the vertical variation gives the natural length of the device. ## 2.2 The Six Steps of How SCE Onset (Logical Chain) 1. **Channel Length Shrinks** → L/λ ratio falls below 5. 2. **2-D Electrostatics Take Over** → vertical field from gate is no longer dominant; lateral field from S/D becomes comparable. 3. **Potential Barrier at the Source Lowers** → source-to-channel barrier is shared between gate, source, and drain. 4. **Threshold Voltage Drops (V_T roll-off)** → channel turns on at lower gate voltage. 5. **Drain Bias Influences Barrier (DIBL)** → higher V_DS further lowers the barrier; subthreshold swing worsens. 6. **Loss of Gate Control** → gate cannot turn the device off completely → static leakage. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in industry:** At the 22 nm node (Intel, 2011), the characteristic length λ of a planar bulk MOSFET was ~10 nm, meaning L = 22 nm was only ≈ 2λ. This forced the industry to abandon planar bulk silicon and adopt **FinFETs** (3-D tri-gate), which intrinsically have a 35% smaller λ. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Natural length, **bulk single-gate** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\,t_{ox}\,X_{dep}}$ | X_dep in cm | | 2 | Natural length, **double-gate / FD-SOI** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$ | Most-quoted form | | 3 | Natural length, **tri-gate / FinFET** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{3\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$ | Gate on 3 sides | | 4 | Natural length, **gate-all-around (GAA)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{4\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$ | Best scalability | | 5 | Effective channel length | $L_{eff} = L_{drawn} - \Delta L$ | ΔL = 2 × (0.1·L_j) | | 6 | Threshold roll-off (SCE) | $\Delta V_T = -\dfrac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\cosh(L_{eff}/2\lambda)-1}\,\sqrt{1+\dfrac{2\,V_{DS}}{V_{bi}-\phi_s}}$ | Most-tested formula | | 7 | DIBL factor | $\Delta V_T^{DIBL} = -\sigma\,V_{DS}$ | σ = DIBL coefficient (mV/V) | | 8 | Subthreshold swing | $S = \ln(10)\,\dfrac{kT}{q}\,\left(1+\dfrac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | Minimum 60 mV/dec at 300 K | | 9 | Dennard scaling factor | Power per chip = **constant** (for CES) | Broke down at 90 nm | | 10 | Body factor | $m = 1 + \dfrac{C_{si}}{C_{ox}} = 1 + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}/t_{si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}}$ | Determines S | | 11 | Saturation current scaling | $I_{D,sat} \propto \dfrac{1}{L_{eff}}$ | Linear in 1/L | | 12 | Intrinsic delay | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{ox}\,V_{DD}}{I_{on}} \propto \dfrac{L_{eff}^{2}}{V_{DD}}$ | L² dependence | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** In any exam, the symbol **L** in the roll-off formula ALWAYS means the **effective** channel length L_eff. The metallic junction length L_m is used only when defining the metallurgical channel. Substituting the drawn L will cost you 1 mark. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Characteristic Lengths - **Process Design Rule:** The technology node target length L_node ≈ 5λ to 7λ (gate-all-around allows L ≈ 3λ). - **Lithography Budget:** Knowing λ sets the maximum allowable random dopant fluctuation and line-edge roughness. - **Multi-Gate Roadmap:** Every additional gate wrapping the channel divides λ by √n, allowing further L_scaling — the precise reason behind the FinFET → GAA transition at 3 nm (TSMC 2022). - **Leakage Power Estimation:** Subthreshold leakage I_off = I_0 · 10^((V_GS - V_T)/S); both ΔV_T and ΔS depend directly on L/λ. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Characteristic Length of a Double-Gate MOSFET (Full Working) This is the **most frequently asked derivation in the KTU ESE paper** for Module 1. ### Step 1: Set up Poisson's equation in the silicon body Consider a thin silicon film of thickness t_si sandwiched between two gates. The y-direction is vertical (gate to body), x-direction is along the channel. There is no doping (fully depleted, intrinsic body). Poisson's equation simplifies to Laplace's equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial x^2} \;+\; \frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial y^2} \;=\; 0$$ ### Step 2: Separation of variables Assume a solution of the form: $$\phi(x,y) \;=\; A(y) \;+\; B(x)\,C(y)$$ where the first term is the long-channel 1-D solution and the second is the short-channel perturbation. Substituting and separating: $$B''(x)\,C(y) \;+\; B(x)\,C''(y) \;=\; 0$$ Divide by B(x)·C(y): $$\frac{B''(x)}{B(x)} \;=\; -\frac{C''(y)}{C(y)} \;=\; -k^2$$ ### Step 3: Solve the y-equation (gate-controlled profile) The boundary conditions on the y-equation (gates at y = ±t_si/2 with surface potential V_s): $$C''(y) \;=\; k^2\,C(y) \quad\Rightarrow\quad C(y) = \alpha\,\cosh\!\left(\frac{y}{\lambda}\right) + \beta\,\sinh\!\left(\frac{y}{\lambda}\right)$$ where we identify: $$\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{k}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the boundary conditions on the y-equation The vertical electric field at the gate-oxide interface must equal the oxide field (Gauss's law): $$\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right|_{y=\pm t_{si}/2} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,\frac{V_g - V_{fb} - \phi(\pm t_{si}/2)}{t_{ox}}$$ Solving these two coupled boundary conditions yields: $$k\,t_{si}\,\sinh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \;+\; 2\,\frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,\frac{t_{si}}{t_{ox}}\,\cosh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \;=\; 0$$ ### Step 5: Take the thin-film limit t_si / λ << 1 In modern devices, t_si is so thin (≤ 10 nm) that t_si/λ << 1, so we can Taylor expand: $$\sinh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \approx \frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}, \qquad \cosh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \approx 1$$ Substituting into the boundary equation: $$\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda} \cdot \frac{t_{si}}{2} \;+\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,\frac{t_{si}}{t_{ox}} \cdot 2 \;\approx\; 0$$ (The minus sign on cosh cancels with the +2 in the second term from symmetric double-gate). $$\frac{t_{si}^{\,2}}{4\lambda} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,\frac{t_{si}}{t_{ox}} \cdot 1$$ Solve for λ: $$\lambda^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}^{\,2}\,t_{ox}}{4\,\varepsilon_{ox}\,t_{si}} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{4\,\varepsilon_{ox}}$$ Wait — for **symmetric double gate**, the boundary condition gives a factor of 2, yielding: $$\boxed{\;\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **canonical double-gate natural length** quoted in KTU textbooks (e.g., Taur & Ning, "Fundamentals of Modern VLSI Devices", Cambridge). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Voltage Roll-Off (ΔV_T) from 2-D Electrostatics ### Step 1: Long-channel surface potential The long-channel surface potential at the source end (x = 0) is: $$\phi_{s,LC}(0) \;=\; V_{bi} \;-\; \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{T0,LC})\,\gamma}{\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} + \gamma}$$ In subthreshold, with V_GS = 0: $$\phi_{s,LC} \;\approx\; V_{bi}$$ ### Step 2: Add the short-channel perturbation (separation of variables) In subthreshold, the 2-D solution to Laplace's equation with the source/drain as boundary conditions gives: $$\phi_s(x) \;=\; \phi_{s,LC} \;+\; \frac{(V_{bi} - \phi_{s,LC})\,\sinh(x/\lambda) \;+\; (V_{bi} + V_{DS} - \phi_{s,LC})\,\sinh((L-x)/\lambda)}{\sinh(L/\lambda)}$$ ### Step 3: Find the minimum surface potential (the source-to-channel barrier) The minimum occurs where dφ_s/dx = 0, i.e., at the point x_min where: $$\cosh(x_{min}/\lambda) \;=\; \sqrt{1 \;+\; \left(\frac{V_{DS}}{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_{s,LC})}\right)^2} \;-\; \frac{V_{DS}}{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_{s,LC})}$$ Substituting back, the **minimum surface potential** becomes: $$\phi_{s,min} \;=\; \phi_{s,LC} \;-\; \frac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_{s,LC})}{\dfrac{L_{eff}}{\lambda}} \cdot \text{(correction term)}$$ ### Step 4: Define V_T roll-off V_T is reached when φ_s,min = 2φ_F (the onset of strong inversion). Reducing φ_s,min means a smaller V_GS is needed, i.e., V_T has dropped. After algebraic manipulation: $$\Delta V_T \;=\; V_T - V_{T,LC} \;=\; -\,\frac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\cosh(L_{eff}/2\lambda) - 1}\,\sqrt{1 \,+\, \frac{2\,V_{DS}}{V_{bi}-\phi_s}}$$ where φ_s = 2φ_F in strong inversion, and (V_bi - φ_s) is the built-in potential relative to the surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign convention:** ΔV_T is **negative** for short-channel devices — V_T reduces. When L_eff → ∞, cosh(L_eff/2λ) → ∞, so ΔV_T → 0, recovering the long-channel result. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Length Modulation Output Resistance In saturation, the pinch-off point is not exactly at the drain but moves slightly toward the source. The effective length reduces: $$L_{eff} \;=\; L \;-\; \Delta L \;=\; L \;-\; l_{d}\,\sqrt{\frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l_{d}\,E_c}}$$ The drain current becomes: $$I_D \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L - \Delta L}\,(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$$ The output resistance is therefore finite: $$r_{o} \;=\; \left(\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}}\right)^{-1} \;\approx\; \frac{L \cdot E_c}{(V_{GS}-V_T)^2 \cdot \mu_n C_{ox} W / (2L)}$$ This shows that as L shrinks, r_o falls — another short-channel penalty. # 4.
