KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the characteristic length (natural length) of a MOSFET. Why is it called the "scaling length"?** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Model Answer:** The **characteristic length λ** of a MOSFET is the length scale that governs the two-dimensional electrostatic potential distribution in the channel. For a double-gate MOSFET, it is defined as: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}$$ (3 marks) It is called the **scaling length** because the onset of short-channel effects is determined solely by the ratio L/λ. The **KTU-cited benchmark** is: - **L ≥ 5λ** → long-channel behaviour; classical square-law I-V applies. - **3λ ≤ L < 5λ** → mild SCE (subthreshold swing worsens, small ΔV_T). - **L < 3λ** → severe SCE (DIBL, punch-through, gate loses control). A smaller λ allows scaling to a smaller L without SCE, hence its name. **(3 marks — including 1 for benchmark ratios)** --- ### **Q2. List and briefly explain any three short-channel effects in a nanoscale MOSFET.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **(a) Threshold Voltage Roll-Off:** As L decreases below 5λ, the source/drain depletion regions encroach into the channel. The gate no longer fully controls the channel potential, so a smaller gate voltage is required to form the inversion layer. Hence V_T decreases. **(1 mark)** **(b) Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL):** With the channel short, a high drain voltage V_DS lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier, effectively reducing V_T. This is measured by the DIBL coefficient σ = ΔV_T / ΔV_DS in mV/V. **(1 mark)** **(c) Hot Carrier Injection (HCI):** When L is short, V_DS is dropped over a tiny distance, creating huge lateral electric fields (> 10⁵ V/cm). Carriers gain kinetic energy > 3.2 eV (Si band gap) and are injected into the gate oxide, causing permanent device degradation. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark ESE Questions (with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **characteristic length λ of a symmetric double-gate MOSFET** in the thin-film limit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A symmetric double-gate MOSFET has t_si = 10 nm, t_ox = 1.5 nm (SiO₂). Compute its characteristic length and the minimum L that can be considered "long-channel". Given that the same device is built with HfO₂ (ε_r = 22) of equivalent t_ox in SiO₂ = 1.5 nm, find the new characteristic length and comment. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [Step 1: 1 mark] Poisson's equation reduces to Laplace's equation (fully depleted, undoped body): $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial x^2} \;+\; \frac{\partial^2 \phi(x,y)}{\partial y^2} \;=\; 0$$ [Step 2: 1 mark] Separation of variables: $$\phi(x,y) \;=\; A(y) \;+\; B(x)\,C(y)$$ [Step 3: 1 mark] The y-equation with boundary conditions gives: $$C(y) = \alpha\,\cosh\!\left(\frac{y}{\lambda}\right) + \beta\,\sinh\!\left(\frac{y}{\lambda}\right)$$ [Step 4: 1 mark] Apply Gauss's law at the gate interfaces y = ±t_si/2. The lateral electric field in the oxide equals the lateral field in Si at the interface. [Step 5: 1 mark] Solve the transcendental equation for λ: $$k\,t_{si}\,\sinh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \;+\; 2\,\frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,\frac{t_{si}}{t_{ox}}\,\cosh\!\left(\frac{t_{si}}{2\lambda}\right) \;=\; 0$$ [Step 6: 1 mark] Thin-film limit t_si/λ << 1: $$\boxed{\;\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}}{2\,\varepsilon_{ox}}}\;}$$ [Step 7: 1 mark] **Physical interpretation:** The factor 2 in the denominator comes from the **two gates** sharing the electrostatic control of the thin body. Each gate is half as effective as a single gate on a thick body. This is the fundamental reason multi-gate structures scale to shorter L. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** [Step 1: 1 mark] Given constants: ε_si = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm = 1.036 × 10⁻¹² F/cm; ε_ox (SiO₂) = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ = 3.453 × 10⁻¹³ F/cm. t_si = 10 nm = 1 × 10⁻⁶ cm; t_ox = 1.5 nm = 1.5 × 10⁻⁷ cm. [Step 2: 2 marks] Compute λ for SiO₂: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 3.453 \times 10^{-13}}}$$ $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1.554 \times 10^{-25}}{6.906 \times 10^{-13}}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.250 \times 10^{-13}} \;\text{cm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \;\approx\; 4.74 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm} \;=\; 4.74\,\text{nm}}$$ [Step 3: 1 mark] Minimum long-channel L: L_min = 5λ = **23.7 nm**. [Step 4: 2 marks] For HfO₂, the equivalent SiO₂ thickness is t_ox(eq) = 1.5 nm. The actual physical HfO₂ thickness is: $$t_{ox,HfO_2} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{HfO_2}}{\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}\,t_{ox,eq} \;=\; \frac{22}{3.9}\times 1.5 \;\text{nm} \;\approx\; 8.46\,\text{nm}$$ Now, in the λ formula, we use the **physical** HfO₂ thickness and its permittivity: $$\lambda_{HfO_2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox,HfO_2}}{2\,\varepsilon_{HfO_2}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox,eq}}{2\,\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}}$$ = **the same 4.74 nm** as the SiO₂ case (EOT-based equivalence). **(1 mark)** [Step 5: 1 mark] **Comment:** Using a high-κ dielectric does NOT reduce λ (when EOT is the same). The only way to reduce λ is to **thin t_si** or use **multi-gate geometry** (factor 2 → 3 → 4 in denominator). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** With the aid of a suitable expression, explain **threshold voltage roll-off** due to short-channel effects. Show that ΔV_T → 0 as L → ∞. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A nanoscale NMOS has the following parameters: V_bi = 0.9 V, φ_s = 2φ_F = 0.7 V, L_eff = 60 nm, λ = 12 nm, V_DS = 0.5 V. Compute the threshold voltage shift ΔV_T. Comment on whether this is "mild" or "severe" SCE. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution **(a) Conceptual and Mathematical Explanation (7 marks):** [Step 1: 1 mark] In a long-channel device, V_T is set entirely by the gate-induced band bending and is **independent of L** and V_DS. [Step 2: 1 mark] When L is comparable to λ, the source and drain depletion regions couple electrostatically to the channel centre. The surface potential φ_s(x) is no longer parabolic but has a 2-D solution: $$\phi_s(x) = \phi_{s,LC} - \frac{2(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\cosh(L_{eff}/2\lambda)-1} \cdot \left[1 - \cosh\!\left(\frac{x-L/2}{\lambda}\right)\right] + \ldots$$ [Step 3: 1 mark] The minimum surface potential φ_s,min (which determines V_T) is reduced. The corresponding threshold shift is: $$\Delta V_T \;=\; -\,\frac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\cosh(L_{eff}/2\lambda) - 1}\,\sqrt{1 \,+\, \frac{2\,V_{DS}}{V_{bi}-\phi_s}}$$ [Step 4: 2 marks] Taking the limit L → ∞: cosh(x) ≈ (e^x)/2 → ∞. Therefore: $$\lim_{L_{eff}\to\infty}\Delta V_T \;=\; -\,\frac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\infty} \;=\; 0$$ [Step 5: 1 mark] Also as V_DS → 0, the square-root term → 1, and the roll-off still vanishes as L → ∞, showing the formula is self-consistent. [Step 6: 1 mark] **Physical picture:** As L increases, the S/D junctions become "infinitely far" from the channel centre. Their potential perturbations decay exponentially as e^(−x/λ) and never reach the channel. Thus the gate alone controls V_T, and we recover the long-channel result. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** [Step 1: 1 mark] Identify the parameters: V_bi = 0.9 V, φ_s = 0.7 V, so (V_bi − φ_s) = 0.2 V. L_eff = 60 nm, λ = 12 nm, V_DS = 0.5 V. [Step 2: 1 mark] Compute L_eff / (2λ) = 60 / 24 = 2.5. [Step 3: 1 mark] cosh(2.5) = (e^2.5 + e^−2.5)/2 = (12.182 + 0.0821)/2 = 6.132. [Step 4: 1 mark] cosh(L_eff / 2λ) − 1 = 6.132 − 1 = 5.132. [Step 5: 1 mark] Compute the DIBL square-root correction: $$\sqrt{1 \,+\, \frac{2 V_{DS}}{V_{bi}-\phi_s}} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 0.5}{0.2}} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + 5} \;=\; \sqrt{6} \;\approx\; 2.449$$ [Step 6: 1 mark] Compute ΔV_T: $$\Delta V_T \;=\; -\,\frac{2 \times 0.2}{5.132} \times 2.449 \;=\; -\,\frac{0.4 \times 2.449}{5.132} \;\approx\; -0.191\,\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V_T \;\approx\; -191\,\text{mV}}$$ [Step 7: 1 mark] **Comment:** L_eff/λ = 60/12 = 5, which is exactly at the long-channel boundary. A shift of ~190 mV is **moderate-to-severe** SCE. For a long-channel reference V_T0 = 0.4 V, the actual V_T has dropped to ≈ 0.21 V — almost halved — and subthreshold leakage will be exponentially higher. This explains why modern 5 nm logic nodes use **multi-gate (GAA)** structures with λ < 5 nm. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong sign in ΔV_T:** Forgetting the negative sign — V_T DECREASES in short-channel devices. Loss: 1 mark. > 2. **Substituting L_drawn instead of L_eff:** L_eff = L_drawn − ΔL. Loss: 1 mark. > 3. **Using ε_0 (vacuum) instead of ε = ε_r × ε_0:** Loss: 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting the factor 2 in the double-gate λ:** Using single-gate formula for double-gate. Loss: 2 marks. > 5. **Confusing λ and L_m:** λ is a *scaling length* (a property of geometry/materials), L_m is a *physical channel length*. They have different units and meanings. Loss: 1 mark. > 6. **Forgetting the DIBL √-term when V_DS ≠ 0:** The roll-off formula is V_DS-dependent. Loss: 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Characteristic length λ** is the "electrostatic reach" of the S/D junctions. Smaller λ = better scaling headroom. - **Canonical λ formulas (MEMORIZE):** - Bulk SG: $\lambda = \sqrt{(\varepsilon_{si}/\varepsilon_{ox})\,t_{ox}\,X_{dep}}$ - Double Gate: $\lambda = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}/(2\,\varepsilon_{ox})}$ - Tri-Gate: $\lambda = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}/(3\,\varepsilon_{ox})}$ - GAA: $\lambda = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{si}\,t_{si}\,t_{ox}/(4\,\varepsilon_{ox})}$ - **Long-channel rule:** L ≥ 5λ (mandatory for KTU long-channel analysis). - **Threshold roll-off formula** (negative sign, with DIBL correction): $\Delta V_T = -\dfrac{2\,(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}{\cosh(L_{eff}/2\lambda)-1}\,\sqrt{1 + 2V_{DS}/(V_{bi}-\phi_s)}$ - **DIBL** is captured by the √-term in ΔV_T. It vanishes when V_DS = 0. - **Effective vs drawn length:** L_eff = L_drawn − ΔL; use L_eff in all SCE formulas. - **Two scaling paradigms:** CVS (voltages constant, E-field rises) and CES (Dennard, E-field constant). CES broke down at 90 nm. - **Multi-gate advantage:** Each additional gate divides λ by √n → GAA has the smallest λ → enables sub-5 nm channel lengths. - **Hot carrier limit:** E_lat > 10⁵ V/cm causes HCI. High-κ dielectrics do **not** reduce λ; only thin t_si and multi-gating do. - **Subthreshold swing minimum** is 60 mV/dec at 300 K (Boltzmann tyranny). Real devices: 70–100 mV/dec. - **Punch-through:** when L_eff → 0 (source and drain depletion regions touch). Different from DIBL: punch-through is the source-drain barrier going to zero, DIBL is a smaller barrier reduction. - **Units reminder:** ε_si = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm, ε_ox (SiO₂) = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm. Always convert nm to cm (× 10⁻⁷). - **Hallmark of KTU ESE answers:** Always show the **limiting case** (L → ∞ → ΔV_T → 0). It demonstrates command over the physics.

topics.title: Oxide Layer Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oxide Layer Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

The **Oxide Layer Thickness** (denoted as $t_{ox}$ or $d_{ox}$) is the physical dimension of the insulating dielectric film—historically thermally grown **SiO₂** (silicon dioxide) and, in modern nanoscale devices, a **high-k dielectric** material—sandwiched between the **gate electrode** and the **semiconductor channel** in a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET). In nanoelectronics, $t_{ox}$ has been aggressively miniaturized from micrometers down to sub-**1.5 nm** regimes, making it the most aggressively scaled parameter in any integrated circuit technology.

The **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)** is the thickness of pure SiO₂ that would yield the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual dielectric stack in use, allowing direct electrical comparison across technology nodes.

## Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaky Dam"

Imagine a massive hydroelectric dam (the **gate electrode**) that controls the flow of water (electrons) into a downstream irrigation canal (the **channel**). The dam wall (the **oxide**) must be thick enough to prevent leakage but thin enough to allow precise water-level control.

- In the 1990s, the dam wall was a comfortable 10 meters thick (10 nm of SiO₂) — control was excellent, and leakage was negligible.
- In the 2010s, engineers shrank the wall to just 1.5 meters thick (1.5 nm) — control became superb, but water (electrons) started **quantum-mechanically seeping** through the wall via a phenomenon called *direct tunneling*.
- The modern solution (post-2007) is to build the dam wall from a special "spongy concrete" (a **high-k dielectric** like HfO₂) that is physically thick (4–5 meters) to prevent leakage, but electrically *appears* as thin as 0.7 meters to the water because of its higher dielectric constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Physical Thickness ($t_{phys}$):** the actual measured geometric dimension.
> - **Electrical/EOT Thickness:** the SiO₂ equivalent that controls capacitance and short-channel effects.
> - **Two different dielectrics can share the same EOT but have vastly different $t_{phys}$** — this is the central engineering trade-off in nanometer MOSFET design.

## Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Aligned)

- **Physical thickness ($t_{ox}$):** measured in **nm** (1 nm = **10 Å**)
- **Dielectric constant of SiO₂:** $\kappa_{ox} = 3.9$
- **Typical scaling limit of pure SiO₂:** $\sim 1.0$–$1.2$ nm (set by tunneling leakage)
- **Modern EOT (45 nm and below):** $\sim 0.5$–$1.0$ nm
- **Barrier height for electrons at Si/SiO₂ interface:** $\phi_B \approx 3.1$ eV
- **Effective electron mass in SiO₂:** $m^* \approx 0.5\,m_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Oxide layer thickness directly controls **gate capacitance, threshold voltage, transconductance, subthreshold slope, and gate leakage current**. The transition from SiO₂ to high-k dielectrics (introduced by Intel at the 45 nm node, 2007) is treated as a *mandatory* topic in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin — Why the Oxide Dominates Device Behavior

The gate oxide in a MOSFET acts as the **dielectric of a parallel-plate capacitor** in which:
- **Top plate** = polysilicon or metal gate electrode
- **Bottom plate** = induced inversion-layer charge in the silicon channel
- **Dielectric** = the oxide layer (SiO₂ or high-k)

The **gate capacitance per unit area** $C_{ox}$ is the inverse-linear link between oxide thickness and *every* figure of merit of the transistor. Reducing $t_{ox}$ boosts $C_{ox}$, which in turn raises **transconductance** $g_m$ (faster switching), **drain current** $I_{on}$ (more drive), and **subthreshold slope** (lower OFF-state leakage). However, every nanometer removed also amplifies the **exponential tunneling leakage** through the barrier, setting a hard physical floor on how thin $t_{ox}$ can become.

## 2.2 Analytical Breakdown of the Effects

1. **Capacitive Effect** (Desirable)
   - $C_{ox} \propto 1/t_{ox}$ → larger $C_{ox}$ as $t_{ox}$ shrinks
   - Higher $C_{ox}$ → larger $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_T)$
   - Higher $C_{ox}$ → *reduces* the depletion-charge term in $V_T$
   - Higher $C_{ox}$ → tighter gate control over the channel → **reduced short-channel effects**

2. **Tunneling Effect** (Undesirable)
   - For $t_{ox} > 3$ nm: tunneling is negligible; classical drift-diffusion is valid
   - For $t_{ox} < 3$ nm: **direct tunneling** sets in (electrons traverse the barrier as waves)
   - For $t_{ox} < 1.5$ nm: gate leakage current may *exceed* the ON-state current of the device, making SiO₂ unviable

3. **Poly-Depletion Effect** (Secondary)
   - As $t_{ox}$ approaches 1–2 nm, the finite depletion region in the polysilicon gate becomes comparable to $t_{ox}$ itself, **effectively adding 0.5–1 nm** of "hidden" series capacitance. This is why the industry migrated to **metal gates** alongside high-k dielectrics.

4. **Quantum-Mechanical Inversion-Layer Quantization**
   - In ultra-thin oxides, the channel electrons are confined into a 2-D sub-band ~1 nm below the Si/SiO₂ interface. The centroid of this charge is *physically displaced* from the interface, adding another 0.3–0.5 nm of "effective" series thickness.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0/t_{ox}$ | F/m² | Central MOSFET design parameter |
| 2 | Equivalent Oxide Thickness | $EOT = (\kappa_{SiO_2}/\kappa_{high\text{-}k}) \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$ | nm | Electrical thickness of any dielectric |
| 3 | Direct tunneling current density | $J \propto \exp(-2\kappa \cdot t_{ox})$ | A/m² | Sets SiO₂ scaling floor at $\sim 1.2$ nm |
| 4 | Tunneling decay constant | $\kappa = \sqrt{2 m^* \phi_B}/\hbar$ | m⁻¹ | $\approx 6.4 \times 10^{9}$ m⁻¹ for Si/SiO₂ |
| 5 | Long-channel threshold voltage | $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ | V | Last term scales linearly with $t_{ox}$ |
| 6 | Transconductance (saturation) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)$ | S | Drives switching speed |
| 7 | Subthreshold slope limit | $SS = (kT/q)\ln(10) \cdot (1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox})$ | mV/dec | Approaches 60 mV/dec as $C_{ox} \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Quantum correction to $t_{ox}$ | $t_{ox,eff} = t_{ox} + t_{poly} + t_{QM}$ | nm | $t_{QM} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Avoidance:** Whenever you compute $C_{ox}$ in numerical problems, **always convert $t_{ox}$ from nm to meters**. Forgetting this unit conversion is the single most common 2-mark deduction across KTU board papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The oxide layer thickness concept is the cornerstone of every modern logic technology node — from Intel's 90 nm node (2003, $t_{ox} \approx 1.2$ nm SiO₂) to the 3 nm FinFET era (2022+, EOT $\approx 1.0$ nm using HfO₂-based dielectrics). The same physics governs **DRAM storage capacitors**, **floating-gate Flash memory**, **CMOS image sensors**, and **2-D material transistors** (where hexagonal boron nitride, $\kappa \approx 4$, plays the role of the oxide). Mastering this parameter is the gateway to understanding every later topic in nanoelectronics: quantum confinement, ballistic transport, and single-electron devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gate Oxide Capacitance per Unit Area

**Starting point:** The MOS gate stack is treated as a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$ with the oxide as the dielectric.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot A}{t_{ox}}$$

Dividing both sides by area $A$ to obtain the per-unit-area quantity:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{C}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

Substituting $\varepsilon_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0$ with $\kappa_{ox} = 3.9$ and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{t_{ox}} \text{ F/m}^2 = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{t_{ox}} \text{ F/m}^2$$

**Numerical evaluation for $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.302 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2$$

Converting to the more intuitive $\text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$:

$$C_{ox} = 2.302 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 \times \frac{10^{15} \text{ fF}}{1 \text{ F}} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}^2}{10^{12} \mu\text{m}^2} = 23.0 \text{ fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** $C_{ox} \approx 23$ fF/µm² is the value used in nearly all 90 nm–65 nm MOSFETs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direct Tunneling Decay Constant $\kappa$

The WKB approximation for a rectangular potential barrier of height $\phi_B$ and width $t_{ox}$ gives a transmission probability:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \int_0^{t_{ox}} \sqrt{2m^*(\phi_B - E)}/\hbar \; dx\right)$$

For $E \ll \phi_B$ (typical for gate electrons):

$$T \approx \exp(-2\kappa \cdot t_{ox}), \quad \text{where } \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m^* \phi_B}}{\hbar}$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation for Si/SiO₂ interface:**

Given:
- $m^* = 0.5\,m_0 = 0.5 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 4.555 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $\phi_B = 3.1$ eV $= 3.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 4.96 \times 10^{-19}$ J
- $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

**Step A — Numerator under the square root:**

$$2 m^* \phi_B = 2 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.96 \times 10^{-19} = 4.518 \times 10^{-49} \text{ kg}^2\text{J/m}^2$$

**Step B — Square root:**

$$\sqrt{4.518 \times 10^{-49}} = 6.722 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg}\sqrt{\text{J}}/\text{m}$$

**Step C — Divide by $\hbar$:**

$$\kappa = \frac{6.722 \times 10^{-25}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} = 6.37 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{-1} = 0.637 \text{ Å}^{-1}$$

> **Catastrophic consequence:** A reduction of just **1 Å (0.1 nm)** in $t_{ox}$ multiplies the tunneling current by a factor of $\exp(2 \times 0.637) = \exp(1.274) \approx 3.57$. A 2 Å reduction multiplies it by $\sim 12.8$, and a 5 Å reduction by $\sim 600$. This is why the industry has hit a hard wall on SiO₂ thinning.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) for High-k Dielectric

**Definition (from equal capacitance):**

$$C_{ox,SiO_2} = C_{ox,high\text{-}k} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox,SiO_2}} = \frac{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}k}}$$

Solving for the SiO₂-equivalent thickness:

$$EOT \equiv t_{ox,SiO_2} = \frac{\kappa_{ox}}{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k} = \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$$

**Numerical example — HfO₂ (κ = 25), physical thickness 4 nm:**

$$EOT = \frac{3.9}{25} \times 4 = 0.624 \text{ nm}$$

**Comparison:** This 4 nm HfO₂ layer provides the **same gate capacitance** as 0.624 nm of SiO₂, but its **physical thickness is 6.4× greater**, which suppresses direct tunneling by orders of magnitude (exponential dependence on $t_{phys}$). Since HfO₂ also has a *higher conduction-band offset* with silicon (lower $\phi_B$ for electrons, but the physical thickness increase dominates), the net gate leakage is reduced by approximately $10^4$–$10^5$× at the same EOT.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Threshold Voltage Dependence on $t_{ox}$

The classical long-channel threshold voltage expression (with $N_A$ as channel doping and $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$):

$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Substituting $C_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0/t_{ox}$:

$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{t_{ox}}{\kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0}\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** The depletion-charge term in $V_T$ is *directly proportional* to $t_{ox}$. Therefore, as $t_{ox}$ shrinks, the **body-effect coefficient** shrinks, making $V_T$ less sensitive to substrate bias — a desirable outcome for analog circuit design.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Why the Subthreshold Slope Improves with Thinner Oxide

The subthreshold swing (SS) of a MOSFET is given by:

$$SS = \frac{kT}{q}\ln(10) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$$

Where $C_{dep} = \sqrt{q\varepsilon_{si}N_A/(2\phi_F)}$ is the depletion-layer capacitance per unit area. As $t_{ox} \to 0$, $C_{ox} \to \infty$ and $C_{dep}/C_{ox} \to 0$, so:

$$SS \to \frac{kT}{q}\ln(10) \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec at 300 K}$$

This is the **Boltzmann tyranny limit** that no conventional MOSFET can beat — and is the fundamental reason that purely reducing $t_{ox}$ cannot reduce OFF-state power indefinitely. New device architectures (FinFET, GAA, tunnel FETs) attack this limit from a different angle.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SiO₂ vs. High-k

| Property | SiO₂ (κ=3.9) | HfO₂ (κ=25) | HfSiON (κ≈15) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Physical thickness for EOT = 1 nm | 1.0 nm | 6.4 nm | 3.85 nm |
| Band gap | 9 eV | 5.7 eV | ~6.5 eV |
| Conduction-band offset to Si | 3.1 eV | 1.5 eV | 2.0 eV |
| Gate leakage at 1 nm EOT, 1 V | ~10 A/cm² | ~10⁻³ A/cm² | ~10⁻² A/cm² |
| Thermal stability with Si | Excellent | Poor (needs capping layer) | Good |
| Compatibility with poly-Si gate | Yes | No (needs metal gate) | Limited |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following block-level functional diagrams map the **cause-and-effect relationships** of oxide-layer thickness in nanoscale MOSFETs. (Mermaid compilation safeguards applied: all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.)

## Diagram 1 — Physical Architecture of a MOSFET Gate Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["GATE ELECTRODE METAL OR POLYSILICON"]
    nodeB["GATE OXIDE OR HIGH K DIELECTRIC THICKNESS TOX"]
    nodeC["INTERFACE TRAP LAYER APPROX 0.5 NM"]
    nodeD["SILICON CHANNEL INVERSION LAYER APPROX 1 NM"]
    nodeE["SILICON SUBSTRATE BODY OR P WELL"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

## Diagram 2 — Effect of Reducing $t_{ox}$ on Device Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["REDUCING TOX PHYSICAL THICKNESS"]
    nodeB["INCREASE COX CAPACITANCE PER UNIT AREA"]
    nodeC["HIGHER TRANSCONDUCTANCE GM"]
    nodeD["LOWER THRESHOLD VOLTAGE VT"]
    nodeE["TIGHTER SHORT CHANNEL CONTROL"]
    nodeF["HIGHER ION DRIVE CURRENT"]
    nodeG["STEEPER SUBTHRESHOLD SLOPE SS"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
```

## Diagram 3 — The Tunneling Penalty and the High-k Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["REDUCING TOX BELOW 3 NM"]
    nodeB["DIRECT QUANTUM TUNNELING THROUGH BARRIER"]
    nodeC["EXPONENTIAL LEAKAGE CURRENT RISE"]
    nodeD["STATIC POWER DISSIPATION EXCEEDS BUDGET"]
    nodeE["SIO2 SCALING LIMIT REACHED AT TOX 1.2 NM"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE

    nodeF["ENGINEERING SOLUTION"]
    nodeG["REPLACE SIO2 WITH HIGH K DIELECTRIC"]
    nodeH["USE HFO2 WITH KAPPA 25 OR HFSION"]
    nodeI["LARGER PHYSICAL THICKNESS 4 TO 5 NM"]
    nodeJ["SAME OR LOWER EOT APPROX 0.8 NM"]
    nodeK["TUNNELING SUPPRESSED BY 4 TO 5 ORDERS"]
    nodeL["METAL GATE INTEGRATION TO REMOVE POLY DEPLETION"]

    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeL
```

## Diagram 4 — Process Flow for Modern High-k Gate Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["STEP 1: HF LAST CLEAN OF SI SURFACE"]
    nodeB["STEP 2: FORM INTERFACE LAYER SIO2 0.5 NM"]
    nodeC["STEP 3: ALD DEPOSIT HFO2 3 TO 5 NM"]
    nodeD["STEP 4: DEPOSIT TI OR TIN METAL CAP"]
    nodeE["STEP 5: ANNEAL FOR WORKFUNCTION TUNING"]
    nodeF["STEP 6: DEPOSIT POLY SI FILL"]
    nodeG["STEP 7: PATTERN GATE BY RIE"]
    nodeH["FINAL EOT APPROX 1 NM"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT). Why is EOT preferred over physical oxide thickness in modern nanoscale MOSFETs?**

**Model Answer:**
EOT is the thickness of pure SiO₂ ($\kappa = 3.9$) that would yield the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual dielectric in use:
$$EOT = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$$
EOT is preferred over physical thickness because it **directly captures the electrical behavior** (gate capacitance, threshold voltage, transconductance) regardless of the material employed. Two transistors with different dielectrics (e.g., SiO₂ vs. HfO₂) but identical EOT exhibit **nearly identical electrostatic characteristics**, even though their physical thicknesses differ by a factor of 5–10×. This makes EOT the universal benchmark across technology nodes (90 nm, 65 nm, 45 nm, etc.).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is direct tunneling, and at what oxide thickness does it become the dominant leakage mechanism in a MOSFET?**

**Model Answer:**
Direct tunneling is a **quantum-mechanical phenomenon** in which an electron traverses a thin insulating potential barrier *without* acquiring sufficient energy to surmount it—the electron's de Broglie wavelength becomes comparable to the barrier width, and a non-zero probability of transmission emerges. The tunneling current density is:
$$J \propto \exp(-2\kappa \cdot t_{ox})$$
Direct tunneling becomes dominant when $t_{ox} < 3$ nm. Below $1.5$ nm, the leakage current can exceed 1 A/cm², which is unacceptably high for low-power CMOS logic. This is the **physical reason** why pure SiO₂ cannot be scaled below $\approx 1.2$ nm and why **high-k dielectrics** were introduced at the 45 nm node (2007).

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Choose **either** Question A or Question B.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Capacitance & EOT Focus**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for gate oxide capacitance per unit area in a MOSFET. Compute $C_{ox}$ (in fF/µm²) for $t_{ox} = 2$ nm of SiO₂. State clearly all physical constants used.

**Model Solution:**
The MOS gate forms a **parallel-plate capacitor**: top plate = gate electrode, bottom plate = inversion-layer charge, dielectric = oxide.
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot A}{t_{ox}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$$

With $\kappa_{ox} = 3.9$ and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, and converting $t_{ox} = 2 \text{ nm} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m:
$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.727 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2$$
Converting: $1.727 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 = 17.3 \text{ fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain EOT. A HfO₂ layer ($\kappa = 25$) of physical thickness $4$ nm is used as the gate dielectric. (i) Find the EOT. (ii) If this layer replaces 1 nm of SiO₂, by approximately what factor is the direct tunneling current reduced? Use $\kappa \approx 6.4 \times 10^9$ m⁻¹ for both.

**Model Solution:**
(i) EOT definition:
$$EOT = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{HfO_2}} \cdot t_{HfO_2} = \frac{3.9}{25} \times 4 = 0.624 \text{ nm}$$

(ii) Tunneling reduction factor. Although HfO₂ has a different (lower) barrier height, the problem asks us to use the same $\kappa$ and compare **physical thicknesses**:
$$\frac{J_{SiO_2}}{J_{HfO_2}} = \frac{\exp(-2\kappa \cdot 1\text{ nm})}{\exp(-2\kappa \cdot 4\text{ nm})} = \exp(2\kappa(4-1)\text{ nm})$$
$$= \exp(2 \times 6.4 \times 10^9 \times 3 \times 10^{-9}) = \exp(38.4) \approx 3.3 \times 10^{16}$$

> In practice, additional factors (real barrier heights, interface layers) bring the actual reduction to $10^4$–$10^6\times$, which is what the industry observes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Scaling & High-k Focus**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch of the MOS structure, derive the threshold voltage $V_T$ expression and show explicitly its dependence on $t_{ox}$. What is the engineering significance of this dependence?

**Model Solution:**
The MOS structure consists of (from top to bottom): **metal/poly gate → oxide ($t_{ox}$, $\kappa_{ox} = 3.9$) → silicon substrate (p-type, doping $N_A$)**. At the threshold of strong inversion, the surface potential equals $2\phi_F$, and the depletion-layer charge per unit area is:
$$Q_B = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}$$
Applying charge neutrality at the gate and dividing by $C_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0/t_{ox}$:
$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{t_{ox}}{\kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0}\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}$$
**Engineering significance:** The body-effect term in $V_T$ is *directly proportional* to $t_{ox}$. Thus, thinner oxide ⇒ smaller $V_T$ sensitivity to substrate bias, which is critical for analog and RF circuit stability. It also tightens short-channel control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the scaling limit of SiO₂ as a gate dielectric. Explain with relevant equations how high-k dielectrics overcome this limit, and list two challenges introduced by their adoption.

**Model Solution:**
**SiO₂ Scaling Limit:**
As $t_{ox}$ approaches 1.0–1.2 nm, the tunneling current becomes:
$$J \propto \exp(-2\kappa \cdot t_{ox}) \quad \text{with} \quad \kappa \approx 6.4 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^{-1}$$
At $t_{ox} = 1$ nm, $J \approx 1$–$10$ A/cm² at $V_{ox} = 1$ V, causing unmanageable standby power. This is the *physical floor* for SiO₂.

**How High-k Dielectrics Help:**
Substituting a high-k material in the capacitance equation:
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}k}} \;\Rightarrow\; EOT = \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$$
For HfO₂ ($\kappa = 25$), $t_{phys} = 4$ nm gives EOT $= 0.624$ nm. The tunneling barrier is now *physically 4× thicker*, so:
$$J_{HfO_2} \propto \exp(-2\kappa_{HfO_2} \cdot 4 \text{ nm}) \ll J_{SiO_2} \propto \exp(-2\kappa_{SiO_2} \cdot 1 \text{ nm})$$

**Two Challenges:**
1. **Compatibility with polysilicon gates:** HfO₂ reacts with poly-Si during annealing, forming silicides. Solved by using **metal gates** (TiN, TaN) — the industry moved to a *gate-first* or *replacement-metal-gate* process.
2. **Threshold voltage tuning:** Mid-gap workfunction of HfO₂ is poorly aligned with Si bands. Solved by using **dual-workfunction metal gates** (e.g., TiN for nMOS, TiAlC for pMOS).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Oxide Layer Thickness Questions:**
> - **Forgetting to convert nm to meters** when computing $C_{ox}$: a 2-mark loss that recurs in nearly 60% of scripts.
> - **Conflating physical thickness with EOT**: always state which one you are using. Mixing them in a single equation is a 3-mark deduction.
> - **Citing "Moore's Law" as a physical limit instead of the *tunneling current* $J \propto \exp(-2\kappa t_{ox})$**: examiners want the physics, not a generic statement.
> - **Omitting $\kappa_{ox} = 3.9$**: it must appear explicitly in any capacitance or EOT calculation.
> - **Missing units in the final numerical answer** (e.g., writing $C_{ox} = 17.3$ without "fF/µm²"): ½-mark deduction per missing unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** $t_{ox}$ = physical thickness of the gate dielectric; **EOT** = SiO₂-equivalent electrical thickness.
- **Capacitance:** $C_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0 / t_{ox}$ — the central relationship linking thickness to device behavior.
- **EOT Formula:** $EOT = (3.9/\kappa_{high\text{-}k}) \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$ — applies only to single-layer dielectrics.
- **Tunneling Decay:** $\kappa = \sqrt{2m^*\phi_B}/\hbar \approx 0.637$ Å⁻¹ for Si/SiO₂.
- **Tunneling Current:** $J \propto \exp(-2\kappa \cdot t_{ox})$ — exponential rise below 3 nm.
- **SiO₂ Hard Limit:** $\approx 1.0$–$1.2$ nm physical thickness, set by $\sim 1$ A/cm² leakage budget.
- **Threshold Voltage:** $V_T$ has a *linear* term in $t_{ox}$ — thinner oxide, smaller body-effect coefficient.
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)$ — scales inversely with $t_{ox}$.
- **Subthreshold Slope:** $SS = 60 \text{ mV/dec} \cdot (1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox})$ — improves as $C_{ox} \to \infty$ but never below the 60 mV/dec Boltzmann limit.
- **High-k Solution:** HfO₂ ($\kappa = 25$) gives EOT $< 1$ nm with $t_{phys} > 4$ nm, reducing leakage by $\sim 10^4$–$10^5\times$.
- **Mandatory Co-Technologies:** High-k dielectrics *require* metal gates (no poly depletion) and careful interface-layer engineering ($\sim 0.5$ nm SiO₂ under the HfO₂).
- **Three Quantum Corrections to $t_{ox}$:** poly-depletion, inversion-layer quantization, and interface-layer capacitance — all inflate the *effective* oxide thickness by 0.3–1.0 nm beyond the physical number.
- **Application Domains:** Si MOSFETs, DRAM capacitors, Flash memory, 2-D-material transistors, photonic detectors — the same thickness-vs-leakage trade-off recurs universally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunneling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS > - **Course Code:** PEEVT526 > - **Module:** Module 1 — Fundamentals of Nanoelectronics > - **Topic:** Tunneling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > **Quantum Tunneling** is the quantum mechanical phenomenon in which a particle with total energy $E$ *less than* the potential barrier height $V_0$ has a **non-zero probability** of penetrating and emerging on the other side of the barrier. In classical mechanics, such a particle would be completely reflected; in quantum mechanics, the wavefunction $\psi(x)$ has an exponentially decaying yet *non-zero* amplitude inside the barrier, leading to a finite **transmission coefficient** $T$. ## 1.1 Classical vs Quantum View | Feature | Classical Mechanics | Quantum Mechanics | |---|---|---| | Particle energy $E < V_0$ | Total Reflection ($T = 0$) | Partial Transmission ($T > 0$) | | Wavefunction inside barrier | Not defined (forbidden region) | Exponentially decaying | | Governing equation | Newton's Laws | Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation | | Key constant | — | **Reduced Planck's constant $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ghostly Hill" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine pushing a cricket ball towards a tall, solid concrete hill. > - **Classically:** If the ball does not have enough kinetic energy, it rolls back. It can **never** appear on the other side. > - **Quantum-Mechanically:** The ball is described as a *wave packet*. The wave does not abruptly stop at the hill — it **decays exponentially through the concrete** and a small fraction emerges on the other side, like a "ghost" passing through a wall. This is the essence of **tunneling**. > - The smaller the hill's width and height, the more likely the ball "tunnels" through. This is why tunneling dominates in **nanoscale devices** where barrier widths approach a few nanometers. ## 1.3 Why Tunneling Matters in Nanoelectronics When device dimensions shrink below ~**10 nm**, electron transport is no longer governed by classical drift-diffusion but by the **wave nature of electrons**. Tunneling becomes: - The **operating principle** of devices like Esaki Tunnel Diodes, Resonant Tunneling Diodes (RTD), and Single-Electron Transistors (SET). - A **leakage mechanism** that must be controlled in modern CMOS transistors (gate oxide tunneling below 2 nm). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Square Potential Barrier — Setup Consider a one-dimensional rectangular potential barrier of height $V_0$ and width $a$: $$V(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \quad \text{(Region I)} \\ V_0, & 0 \le x \le a \quad \text{(Region II)} \\ 0, & x > a \quad \text{(Region III)} \end{cases}$$ A free electron of energy $E < V_0$ is incident from the left ($x = -\infty$). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Math — Step Logic 1. **Why use the Schrödinger Equation?** Because at the nanoscale, electrons behave as matter waves; classical mechanics breaks down. 2. **Why three regions?** The potential is piecewise constant, so each region has its own constant-coefficient ODE. 3. **Why decaying exponentials inside the barrier?** When $E < V_0$, the wavenumber becomes **imaginary**, transforming oscillatory solutions $\exp(ikx)$ into decaying solutions $\exp(-\kappa x)$. 4. **Why match at boundaries?** The wavefunction $\psi$ and its first derivative $\frac{d\psi}{dx}$ must be continuous everywhere — this gives the 4 unknowns in terms of 1 incident amplitude. 5. **Why a transmission coefficient?** The probability current density on the right ($x > a$) is non-zero — proving the electron passed through. ## 2.3 Boundary Conditions $$\psi_I(0) = \psi_{II}(0), \quad \frac{d\psi_I}{dx}\bigg|_{0} = \frac{d\psi_{II}}{dx}\bigg|_{0}$$ $$\psi_{II}(a) = \psi_{III}(a), \quad \frac{d\psi_{II}}{dx}\bigg|_{a} = \frac{d\psi_{III}}{dx}\bigg|_{a}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Tunneling | # | Quantity | Formula | Definition / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Free-particle wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | | 2 | Decay constant in barrier | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | | 3 | Wavefunction Region I | $\psi_I = A e^{ikx} + B e^{-ikx}$ | Incident + Reflected | | 4 | Wavefunction Region II | $\psi_{II} = C e^{\kappa x} + D e^{-\kappa x}$ | Inside barrier | | 5 | Wavefunction Region III | $\psi_{III} = F e^{ikx}$ | Transmitted only | | 6 | **Transmission Coefficient** (exact) | $T = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{V_0^{\,2} \sinh^{2}(\kappa a)}{4E(V_0 - E)}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Transmission Coefficient (thick barrier, $\kappa a \gg 1$) | $T \approx \dfrac{16\,E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}}\, e^{-2\kappa a}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Reflection Coefficient | $R = 1 - T$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | Tunneling current density | $J \propto T \cdot (n_{left} - n_{right})$ | $\text{A/m}^{2}$ | | 10 | Resonance condition (RTD) | $E = E_{n} = \dfrac{n^{2} \pi^{2} \hbar^{2}}{2 m a^{2}}$ | $\text{eV}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why the $\sinh$ form?** The exact transmission coefficient uses $\sinh^{2}(\kappa a)$ because it correctly accounts for *any* barrier width — including the thin-barrier limit where the electron bounces multiple times. The exponential approximation is valid only when $\kappa a \gg 1$ (i.e., the barrier is wide compared to the decay length $1/\kappa$). ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Tunnel Diodes (Esaki Diodes):** Exploit inter-band tunneling in heavily doped p-n junctions; exhibit **negative differential resistance (NDR)**. - **Resonant Tunneling Diodes (RTD):** Use a double-barrier quantum well; peak-valley ratio depends critically on $T$. - **Flash Memory (Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling):** Electrons tunnel through a thin SiO₂ layer (~**10 nm**) for program/erase. - **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM):** Measures tunneling current between tip and sample — atomic resolution imaging. - **MOSFET Gate Leakage:** At oxide thickness < **2 nm**, direct tunneling through SiO₂ causes unacceptable leakage — leading to the industry shift to **High-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Transmission Through a Square Barrier (Thick-Barrier Approximation) ### Step 1 — Write the Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE) For a particle of mass $m$ and energy $E$ in potential $V(x)$: $$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m} \frac{d^{2}\psi}{dx^{2}} + V(x)\psi = E\psi$$ ### Step 2 — Solve in Each Region **Region I and III** ($V = 0$, $E > 0$, free particle — oscillatory solutions): $$\frac{d^{2}\psi}{dx^{2}} = -k^{2}\psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = A e^{ikx} + B e^{-ikx}, \quad k = \frac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar}$$ **Region II** ($V = V_0$, $E < V_0$ — barrier region, decaying solutions): $$\frac{d^{2}\psi}{dx^{2}} = +\kappa^{2}\psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = C e^{\kappa x} + D e^{-\kappa x}, \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$ > **Logic note:** The sign of $(E - V)$ flips inside the barrier. The ODE changes from oscillatory (negative coefficient) to exponential (positive coefficient), so $\psi$ *decays* — but it never becomes exactly zero, which is the heart of the tunneling effect. ### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions at $x = 0$ At $x = 0$, $\psi$ and $d\psi/dx$ are continuous: $$A + B = C + D \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$ik(A - B) = \kappa(C - D) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions at $x = a$ $$C e^{\kappa a} + D e^{-\kappa a} = F e^{ika} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ $$\kappa(C e^{\kappa a} - D e^{-\kappa a}) = ik F e^{ika} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify Using the Thick-Barrier Assumption For a wide barrier where $\kappa a \gg 1$, the term $e^{+\kappa a}$ dominates. We can neglect $e^{-\kappa a}$ relative to $e^{+\kappa a}$ in Region II (the "growing" exponential is unphysical when sourced from the right). After algebraic elimination of the four unknown coefficients $B, C, D, F$ in terms of the incident amplitude $A$: $$\left|\frac{F}{A}\right|^{2} = \frac{16\,E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}}\, e^{-2\kappa a}$$ ### Step 6 — Define the Transmission Coefficient The transmission coefficient is the ratio of *transmitted* to *incident* probability current density: $$T = \frac{\vert F \vert^{2}}{\vert A \vert^{2}} = \frac{16\,E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}}\, e^{-2\kappa a}$$ $$\boxed{\,T \approx \frac{16\,E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}}\, \exp\!\left[-\frac{2a}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}\right]\,}$$ ### Step 7 — Physical Interpretation of Each Term | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | $\exp(-2\kappa a)$ | **Exponential decay** through the barrier — the dominant factor | | $16 E (V_0 - E) / V_0^{\,2}$ | **Prefactor** — depends on barrier shape and energy mismatch | | $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$ | Larger $\kappa$ → faster decay → less tunneling | | $a$ | Barrier width — halving $a$ dramatically *increases* $T$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU:** A **1 nm reduction** in barrier width can change $T$ by **orders of magnitude** because of the exponential. This is the fundamental reason tunneling becomes dominant at the nanoscale. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Given:** Electron with $E = 0.5\ \text{eV}$, barrier $V_0 = 1.0\ \text{eV}$, $a = 1\ \text{nm}$, $m = m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$. **Step 1 — Compute $\kappa$:** $$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot (1.0 - 0.5) \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}$$ $$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{1.460 \times 10^{-49}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} = \frac{1.208 \times 10^{-24.5}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} \approx 7.26 \times 10^{9}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $2\kappa a$:** $$2\kappa a = 2 \cdot 7.26 \times 10^{9} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} = 14.52$$ **Step 3 — Compute Transmission:** $$T \approx \frac{16 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(1.0)^{2}} \cdot e^{-14.52} = 4 \cdot e^{-14.52} = 4 \cdot 4.93 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$\boxed{\,T \approx 1.97 \times 10^{-6}\,}$$ > **Interpretation:** Only ~2 in a million electrons tunnel through — but for nanoscale current densities, this is enough to produce **measurable and useful device current**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tunneling Process ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Electron Source
Region I x less than 0] -->|Incident Wave A exp ikx| B[Potential Barrier
Region II 0 to a] B -->|Decaying Wave C exp kappa x| C[Decay Continues
Amplitude shrinks exponentially] C -->|Emergent Wave F exp ikx| D[Transmitted Electron
Region III x greater than a] B -.->|Reflected Wave B exp -ikx| A E[Boundary x equals 0
Continuity of psi and dpsi dx] -.-> A F[Boundary x equals a
Continuity of psi and dpsi dx] -.-> D G[Output T equals F magnitude squared over A magnitude squared] -.-> D ``` ## 4.2 Energy-Band Diagram of Tunneling ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REG1[Region I Incident Side] A1[Free Electron E less than V0] A2[Incident Wave moving right] end subgraph REG2[Region II Barrier] B1[Potential V0 above E] B2[Exponentially decaying psi x] end subgraph REG3[Region III Exit Side] C1[Free Electron E less than V0] C2[Transmitted Wave moving right] end REG1 --> REG2 REG2 --> REG3 ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Chart — Classical vs Quantum Behavior ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Particle Approaches Barrier] --> B{Energy E vs V0} B -->|E greater than V0| C[Classical: Passes Over
Quantum: Passes Through] B -->|E less than V0| D[Classical: Total Reflection T equals 0
Quantum: Partial Tunneling T greater than 0] C --> E[Transmitted Wave Large Amplitude] D --> F[Exponentially Decaying Wave Inside Barrier] F --> G[Small but finite T on far side] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]** **Q1. Define quantum mechanical tunneling. Why is it not observed in macroscopic systems?** **Model Answer:** Quantum tunneling is the phenomenon in which a particle with energy $E$ less than the barrier height $V_0$ has a finite probability of penetrating the barrier and emerging on the other side. The transmission coefficient is: $$T \approx \frac{16 E (V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}} e^{-2\kappa a}$$ In macroscopic systems, the barrier width $a$ is enormous (meters), so the exponential factor $e^{-2\kappa a}$ becomes vanishingly small — effectively zero. Hence tunneling is not observed classically. At the nanoscale ($a \sim$ nm), $\kappa a$ is small, making $T$ measurable. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. State the boundary conditions applied at the interfaces of a square potential barrier during tunneling analysis.** **Model Answer:** The wavefunction $\psi(x)$ and its first derivative $d\psi/dx$ must be continuous at $x = 0$ and $x = a$: $$\psi_I(0) = \psi_{II}(0), \quad \frac{d\psi_I}{dx}\bigg|_{0} = \frac{d\psi_{II}}{dx}\bigg|_{0}$$ $$\psi_{II}(a) = \psi_{III}(a), \quad \frac{d\psi_{II}}{dx}\bigg|_{a} = \frac{d\psi_{III}}{dx}\bigg|_{a}$$ This continuity arises because $\psi$ must be single-valued and $d\psi/dx$ must be finite (so probability current is conserved). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 1, PEEVT526]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — OR — ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) --- #### **Question A** (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expression for the transmission coefficient of an electron incident on a rectangular potential barrier of height $V_0$ and width $a$, when $E < V_0$. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electron of energy $E = 0.3\ \text{eV}$ is incident on a barrier of height $V_0 = 0.8\ \text{eV}$ and width $a = 0.8\ \text{nm}$. Calculate the transmission coefficient $T$. Given: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** 1. Set up TISE in three regions (I: $x<0$, II: $0 \le x \le a$, III: $x > a$). 2. Region I & III: $\psi = A e^{ikx} + B e^{-ikx}$ with $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$. 3. Region II: $\psi = C e^{\kappa x} + D e^{-\kappa x}$ with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$. 4. Apply continuity of $\psi$ and $d\psi/dx$ at $x = 0$ and $x = a$ (4 equations). 5. Assume $\kappa a \gg 1$, neglect $e^{-\kappa a}$ term. 6. Solve for $\vert F/A \vert^2$: $$T = \frac{16 E (V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}} e^{-2\kappa a}, \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$ **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1:** Compute $\kappa$: $$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot (0.8 - 0.3) \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{1.460 \times 10^{-49}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} \approx 7.26 \times 10^{9}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $2\kappa a$: $$2\kappa a = 2 \cdot 7.26 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{-9} = 11.62$$ **Step 3:** Compute $T$: $$T = \frac{16 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.5}{(0.8)^{2}} \cdot e^{-11.62} = 3.75 \cdot 8.97 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{\,T \approx 3.36 \times 10^{-5}\,}$$ --- #### **Question B** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain with neat energy-band diagrams, the principle of operation of a **Tunnel Diode (Esaki Diode)**. Discuss its I-V characteristics and the origin of negative differential resistance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tunnel diode has a peak current $I_P = 10\ \text{mA}$ at $V_P = 50\ \text{mV}$ and valley current $I_V = 1\ \text{mA}$ at $V_V = 400\ \text{mV}$. Calculate the **peak-to-valley current ratio (PVCR)** and comment on the device quality. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** A tunnel diode is a heavily doped p-n junction (degenerate semiconductors) on either side of the junction. Because of heavy doping, the depletion region is extremely narrow (~10 nm), allowing electrons to tunnel directly from the conduction band of the n-side to the empty states in the valence band of the p-side. - **At zero bias:** No net tunneling (Fermi levels aligned through tunneling-equivalent states). - **Small forward bias ($V < V_P$):** Electrons in the conduction band of n-side tunnel to empty states in the valence band of p-side. Current **rises sharply**. - **At $V = V_P$:** Maximum overlap between filled states on n-side and empty states on p-side. Current = $I_P$ (peak). - **For $V_P < V < V_V$:** The bands begin to misalign; tunneling overlap reduces. Current **decreases** — this is **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**. - **At $V = V_V$:** Bands fully misaligned; tunneling stops. Current = $I_V$ (valley). - **For $V > V_V$:** Conventional diffusion current takes over; current rises again. The **NDR region** is exploited in high-frequency oscillators and high-speed switches. **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** $$\text{PVCR} = \frac{I_P}{I_V} = \frac{10\ \text{mA}}{1\ \text{mA}} = 10$$ A PVCR of **10** indicates a **good-quality** tunnel diode. For high-performance applications (e.g., microwave oscillators), PVCR should be > 8. Higher PVCR means sharper NDR and better switching behavior. Common KTU benchmark values: PVCR = 4–15 (silicon), 15–60 (GaAs-based tunnel diodes). --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units of $\hbar$** — must convert to J·s (not eV·s) for SI calculations. > 2. **Confusing $k$ and $\kappa$** — $k$ is real (oscillatory), $\kappa$ is real (decaying). Do not interchange them. > 3. **Using $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$** correctly; many students forget the $10^{-19}$ factor. > 4. **Boundary conditions:** Both $\psi$ AND $d\psi/dx$ must be continuous. Writing only $\psi$ continuity costs full marks. > 5. **Skipping assumptions** in derivations (e.g., "thick barrier $\kappa a \gg 1$"). Always state them. > 6. **In Tunnel Diode I-V curve questions:** Always draw a *neat labeled graph* showing $I_P$, $I_V$, $V_P$, $V_V$, and the NDR region. Sketches without axis labels lose marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 High-Yield Points to Remember (Must-Memorize for KTU ESE):** - ✅ Tunneling is a **purely quantum mechanical** phenomenon; no classical analog. - ✅ TISE in three regions: I ($x<0$), II ($0\le x\le a$), III ($x>a$). - ✅ Wavenumber $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$ (free regions) and decay constant $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$ (barrier). - ✅ Wavefunction inside barrier: **exponentially decaying** $\exp(-\kappa x)$ — never exactly zero. - ✅ **Transmission coefficient (thick barrier):** $T \approx \dfrac{16 E (V_0 - E)}{V_0^{\,2}} \exp(-2\kappa a)$. - ✅ Tunneling probability depends **exponentially** on barrier width $a$ — this is why nanoscale matters. - ✅ **Boundary conditions:** $\psi$ and $d\psi/dx$ continuous at $x = 0$ and $x = a$. - ✅ **Esaki Tunnel Diode** uses heavily doped p-n junction; exhibits **NDR** in the I-V curve. - ✅ **Resonant Tunneling Diode (RTD):** double-barrier quantum well; resonance when $E = n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2 / 2 m a^2$. - ✅ **Applications:** Tunnel diodes, RTDs, STM, Flash memory, MOSFET gate leakage. - ✅ **PVCR (Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio)** is the key figure of merit for tunnel diodes. - ✅ Gate oxide tunneling forces modern CMOS to use **High-κ dielectrics** when $t_{ox} < 2\ \text{nm}$.

topics.title: Power Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Density** in nanoelectronics is formally defined as the amount of **electrical power dissipated per unit active area** of a semiconductor device, integrated circuit (IC), or chip. It is quantified as the ratio of total power consumption ($P_{total}$) to the effective operating die area ($A_{die}$).

$$\text{Power Density } (\rho_P) = \frac{P_{total}}{A_{die}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{cm}^2} \right]$$

In the context of **CMOS Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI)** and the continuation of **Moore's Law**, power density is a critical figure-of-merit (FoM) because it directly dictates the **thermal management budget**, **reliability (Mean Time To Failure - MTTF)**, and the **maximum sustainable clock frequency** of the processor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power density is the primary physical bottleneck that halted traditional **Dennard Scaling** around 2005–2006. When power density approached that of a **nuclear reactor core (~1000 W/cm²)** and eventually exceeded that of a **hot kitchen-plate heater (~5–10 W/cm²)**, the industry was forced into the era of **Multi-Core Computing** and **Dark Silicon** concepts.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Power Dissipation in CMOS

Power density is fundamentally driven by two main components in any CMOS circuit:

| Component | Symbol | Physical Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn}$ | Charging/discharging of load capacitances during switching |
| **Static (Leakage) Power** | $P_{stat}$ | Sub-threshold conduction, gate oxide tunneling, reverse-biased junction leakage |

The total power is given by:

$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Kitchen" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Crowded Kitchen**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen where chefs (transistors) are working hard to prepare dishes (switching operations). Each chef generates heat while cooking. Now, imagine shrinking the kitchen while hiring the *same number of chefs* and making them work *even harder* (higher frequency).
>
> - **Power** is the total heat generated by all chefs.
> - **Power Density** is the heat generated *per square foot* of the kitchen.
>
> If the kitchen becomes too small, the temperature rises dangerously — chefs start making mistakes (electromigration, timing errors), and the building itself can catch fire (thermal runaway). This is exactly what happens in a nanoscale chip when power density exceeds the cooling capacity. To survive, the restaurant either installs stronger exhaust fans (better packaging/cooling) or **turns off some chefs** (Dark Silicon).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Industry

| Metric | Typical Value (Modern Node) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power Density of a high-performance CPU** | 50 – 100 | **W/cm²** |
| **Power Density of a low-power mobile SoC** | 1 – 5 | **W/cm²** |
| **Hot plate on a kitchen stove** | ~5 – 10 | **W/cm²** |
| **Surface of the Sun** | ~6,000 | **W/cm²** |
| **Nuclear Reactor Core** | ~1,000 | **W/cm²** |
| **Maximum sustainable without exotic cooling** | ~100 – 200 | **W/cm²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Power Density Matters:** As feature size ($L_g$) shrinks by a factor of $S$ (scaling factor, $S > 1$), the **number of transistors per unit area increases by $S^2$**. If each transistor's power consumption does not scale down by at least $S^2$, the power density explodes, leading to **thermal failure**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Power Density Generation

The power density in a CMOS chip is the cumulative result of billions of transistors switching every nanosecond. Let us break down the theoretical foundation step-by-step.

### Step 1: Identifying the Source of Heat
Every time a CMOS gate (inverter) switches from `LOW` to `HIGH`, current flows through the PMOS transistor to charge the load capacitance ($C_L$). Conversely, during `HIGH` to `LOW`, current flows through the NMOS to discharge $C_L$. **All this charge-transfer work dissipates heat**.

### Step 2: The Dynamic Power Equation
For a single CMOS gate switching at frequency $f$ with an activity factor $\alpha$ (probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per clock cycle):

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{eff} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor ($0 \le \alpha \le 0.5$ for random data, $\alpha = 0.25$ typical)
- $C_{eff}$ = effective load capacitance (gate + interconnect + parasitic)
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

### Step 3: The Static (Leakage) Power Equation
In nanometer CMOS, leakage current ($I_{leak}$) is no longer negligible. It consists of:

$$P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

The leakage components include:
1. **Sub-threshold leakage** ($I_{sub}$) — current flowing when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$
2. **Gate oxide tunneling current** ($I_{gate}$) — direct tunneling through thin SiO₂
3. **Reverse-biased junction leakage** ($I_{rev}$)
4. **Gate-induced drain leakage (GIDL)**

### Step 4: From Power to Power Density
If a chip contains $N$ transistors in an active die area $A_{die}$:

$$\rho_P = \frac{N \cdot (P_{dyn\_per\_gate} + P_{stat\_per\_gate})}{A_{die}}$$

## 2.2 The Scaling Conundrum — Why Power Density Skyrocketed

Classical **Dennard Scaling (1974)** predicted that as dimensions scaled by factor $S$, both voltage and current would scale by $1/S$, keeping **power density CONSTANT**.

| Parameter | Scaling Relation | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length ($L$) | $L' = L/S$ | Smaller |
| Width ($W$) | $W' = W/S$ | Smaller |
| Oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$) | $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/S$ | Thinner |
| Voltage ($V_{DD}$) | $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}/S$ | Lower |
| Capacitance ($C$) | $C' = C/S$ | Lower |
| Current ($I$) | $I' = I/S$ | Lower |
| Power per transistor ($P$) | $P' = P/S^2$ | Decreases sharply |
| Transistor density | $N' = N \cdot S^2$ | Increases |
| **Power density ($\rho_P$)** | **Constant** | **Should stay the same** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality vs. Theory:** Dennard Scaling broke down because **voltage ($V_{DD}$) could not be scaled below ~0.7 V** without destroying transistor performance (sub-threshold slope limit = 60 mV/decade at room temperature). Thus, $V_{DD}$ stayed roughly constant, $C$ scaled as $1/S$, $f$ increased dramatically ($f' = f \cdot S$), and **power density rose catastrophically**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Limits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic switching power per gate | Watts |
| 2 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ | Static leakage power per gate | Watts |
| 3 | $\rho_P = P_{total} / A_{die}$ | Power density of chip | $\text{W/cm}^2$ |
| 4 | $E_{sw} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per switching event | Joules |
| 5 | $P_{chip} = N \cdot P_{gate} + f \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Total chip power | Watts |
| 6 | $\tau_d = C_L V_{DD} / I_{on}$ | Intrinsic gate delay | Seconds |
| 7 | $PDP = E_{sw} \cdot \tau_d$ | Power-Delay Product (FoM for logic) | Joules·s |
| 8 | $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS}-V_{th})/(\eta V_T)}(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$ | Sub-threshold leakage current | Amperes |
| 9 | $I_{gate} \propto (V_{DD}/t_{ox})^2 e^{-\beta t_{ox}}$ | Gate tunneling leakage (Fowler-Nordheim) | Amperes |
| 10 | $P_{max} = (T_{j,max} - T_{amb}) / \theta_{JA}$ | Maximum allowable chip power | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Power density directly determines the choice of **IC packaging** (plastic vs. ceramic vs. flip-chip BGA), the design of **heat sinks**, requirement of **forced-air or liquid cooling**, and even the **clock gating** strategies implemented by hardware architects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Density from CMOS Switching Activity

### Objective
Derive the dynamic power density of a CMOS chip containing $N$ gates, each with effective load capacitance $C_L$, switching at frequency $f$ with supply voltage $V_{DD}$ and activity factor $\alpha$.

### Step 1: Energy Stored in the Load Capacitor
During a $0 \to 1$ transition, the PMOS transistor charges $C_L$ from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. The energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 2: Energy Dissipated by the PMOS During Charging
The energy drawn from the supply $V_{DD}$ during this transition is:

$$E_{supply} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L V_{DD}) \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 3: Heat Dissipated in the PMOS Transistor
By energy conservation (energy supplied = energy stored + energy dissipated as heat):

$$E_{dissipated} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 4: Symmetry for Discharge
During the $1 \to 0$ transition, the energy stored in $C_L$ ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) is dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor. So a **full cycle** ($0 \to 1 \to 0$) dissipates:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 5: Power Per Gate
If the gate switches with activity factor $\alpha$ at clock frequency $f$, then the **number of full cycles per second** is $\alpha f$:

$$P_{dyn\_gate} = \alpha f \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

### Step 6: Total Dynamic Power
For $N$ gates on the chip:

$$P_{dyn\_total} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

### Step 7: Final Power Density Expression
Dividing by the die area $A_{die}$:

$$\boxed{\rho_P = \frac{N \cdot \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}{A_{die}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density Growth Under Constant-Field Scaling (Dennard Failure Mode)

### Objective
Show mathematically why power density **increases** when voltage scaling is abandoned.

### Step 1: Classical Constant-Field Scaling Rules
Under Dennard scaling by factor $S > 1$:
- Dimensions $\to L/S$, oxide thickness $\to t_{ox}/S$
- Voltage $\to V_{DD}/S$, capacitance $\to C/S$
- Frequency $\to f \cdot S$ (intrinsic delay $\tau = C V / I$ scales as $1/S$)

### Step 2: Power Per Transistor (Classical)
$$P_{gate} = C V_{DD}^2 f = \left(\frac{C}{S}\right) \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{S}\right)^2 (S f) = \frac{C V_{DD}^2 f}{S^2}$$

So power per transistor decreases as $1/S^2$.

### Step 3: Transistor Density Increases as $S^2$
Per unit area: $N/A \to S^2 \cdot N/A$.

### Step 4: Classical Power Density (Should be Constant)

$$\rho_P = (N/A) \cdot P_{gate} = \left(S^2 \cdot \frac{N}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{P_{gate}}{S^2}\right) = \frac{N \cdot P_{gate}}{A}$$

**Constant — as predicted by Dennard.**

### Step 5: Post-Dennard Reality (Voltage Stopped Scaling)
Starting from approximately the 90 nm node, $V_{DD}$ was held nearly constant ($V_{DD}' \approx V_{DD}$). The scaling became **"constant-voltage scaling"** instead of constant-field.

Now, the new power per transistor:

$$P_{gate}' = \left(\frac{C}{S}\right) (V_{DD})^2 (S f) = S \cdot C V_{DD}^2 f$$

### Step 6: New Power Density

$$\rho_P' = (N/A)' \cdot P_{gate}' = \left(S^2 \cdot \frac{N}{A}\right) \cdot \left(S \cdot P_{gate}\right) = S^3 \cdot \rho_P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: Power density grows as $S^3$!** For $S = 1.4$ per generation, this gives $\rho_P \to (1.4)^3 \approx 2.74 \times$ per generation. In just 4 generations ($S \approx 4$), power density increases by a factor of **64×** — which is exactly the "Power Wall" crisis observed around 2004.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Power Density Generation & Mitigation Pipeline

```mermaid

    nodeVDD --> nodePMOS
```

## 4.2 Schematic: CMOS Inverter Power Flow

```mermaid

    A[VDD Supply Rail] -->|Charging Current Ich| B[PMOS Transistor M1]
    B -->|Charges Capacitor| C[Load Capacitance CL]
    C -->|Discharging Current Idis| D[NMOS Transistor M2]
    D --> E[GND Reference Rail]
    C --> F[Output Node Vout]
    F -.->|Heat 0.5 CL VDD squared| G[Thermal Energy Qheat Charging Phase]
    F -.->|Heat 0.5 CL VDD squared| H[Thermal Energy Qheat Discharging Phase]
    G --> I[Total Dynamic Power alpha CL VDD squared f]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Power Density Evolution Across Technology Nodes

```mermaid

    N4 --> N5[2010 45nm node rhoP 110 W per cm2 POWER WALL]
    N5 --> N6[2015 14nm node rhoP 100 W per cm2 Multi Core Era]
    N6 --> N7[2020 7nm node rhoP 90 W per cm2 FinFET and DVFS]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the sharp rise until 2010 (Power Wall), followed by a *plateau*. This plateau is not because power density became unimportant — it is because **architectural innovations (multi-core, dark silicon, DVFS)** held the power density at ~100 W/cm², which is the practical air-cooling limit for commercial silicon.
