<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define power density. Why has it become a critical bottleneck in nanoscale CMOS design?**

**Model Answer:**

Power density is defined as the electrical power dissipated per unit active die area of an integrated circuit, expressed as $\rho_P = P_{total} / A_{die}$, with units of **W/cm²**.

It has become a critical bottleneck because:

1. **As feature sizes shrink**, the number of transistors per unit area increases as $S^2$, while supply voltage could not be scaled proportionally below ~0.7 V (due to the sub-threshold slope limit of 60 mV/decade).
2. **Operating frequencies have risen**, causing dynamic power ($\alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$) to grow rapidly.
3. The **combined effect** leads to power density growth as $S^3$ per technology generation under constant-voltage scaling.
4. **Heat removal** from a small area is fundamentally limited by the packaging thermal resistance $\theta_{JA}$, creating the **Power Wall** that halted single-core frequency scaling around 2005.
5. This forced the industry into **multi-core architectures** and **Dark Silicon** paradigms.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between dynamic power and static power dissipation in CMOS circuits.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dynamic Power ($P_{dyn}$) | Static Power ($P_{stat}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formula** | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ |
| **Occurrence** | Only during switching transitions | Always present (even when idle) |
| **Origin** | Charging/discharging of load capacitances | Sub-threshold leakage, gate tunneling, junction leakage |
| **Scaling behavior** | Reduced by lowering $V_{DD}$ or $f$ | **Increases** as $V_{th}$ decreases for sub-100 nm nodes |
| **Dominance** | Dominant in older nodes (>180 nm) | Dominant in modern nodes (<45 nm) |
| **Mitigation** | Clock gating, DVFS | Power gating (MTCMOS), high-$V_{th}$ transistors, multi-$V_{th}$ design |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — With Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation per CMOS gate. Explain how each term in the derived equation influences the power density of a modern integrated circuit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat power-density-versus-technology-node plot, explain the concept of the **"Power Wall"**. Discuss how Dennard scaling breakdown led to this phenomenon.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with mathematical justification why power density grows as $S^3$ under constant-voltage scaling, where $S$ is the scaling factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the various **low-power design techniques** used in nanoelectronic circuits to manage power density, including clock gating, power gating, DVFS, and multi-core architectures.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a)

**Derivation of Dynamic Power:**

Consider a CMOS inverter with load capacitance $C_L$ connected to its output node.

**Step 1:** During a $0 \to 1$ transition, the PMOS transistor turns ON and charges $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$.

Energy drawn from supply:

$$E_{supply} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD} \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2:** Energy stored in the capacitor:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3:** Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS:

$$E_{heat,charge} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4:** During the subsequent $1 \to 0$ transition, the NMOS discharges $C_L$. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is fully dissipated as heat in the NMOS.

**Step 5:** Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle:

$$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6:** If the gate has switching activity factor $\alpha$ (probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per clock cycle) and operates at frequency $f$, then the average dynamic power is:

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

**Influence on Power Density:**

- **$V_{DD}^2$ dependence:** The strongest contributor. Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 1.2 V to 0.7 V (a 42% reduction) cuts dynamic power by **70%**.
- **Capacitance $C_L$:** Determined by gate geometry, fan-out, and interconnect. Minimized through SOI, FinFET, and gate-all-around (GAA) architectures.
- **Frequency $f$:** Doubled only when compensated by $V_{DD}$ reduction or multi-core parallelism.
- **Activity factor $\alpha$:** Reduced using clock gating, operand isolation, and architectural optimizations.

The chip-level power density becomes:

$$\rho_P = \frac{N \cdot \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}{A_{die}}$$

### Solution to Question A(b)

**The Power Wall:**

Refer to the data table:

| Year | Node | Power Density ($\text{W/cm}^2$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1992 | 500 nm | 5 |
| 1997 | 250 nm | 20 |
| 2002 | 130 nm | 50 |
| 2005 | 90 nm | 75 |
| 2008 | 45 nm | **110** ← Power Wall |

**Plot Description:** A sharp rising curve reaching a plateau around 100–120 W/cm², representing the maximum sustainable air-cooled limit.

**Reasons for Dennard Scaling Breakdown:**

1. **Threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) cannot be scaled below ~0.3 V** without incurring exponential sub-threshold leakage current. Since $V_{DD} \geq V_{th} + V_{margin}$, $V_{DD}$ remained stuck near 1 V.
2. **Leakage current ($I_{off}$) increased exponentially** as $V_{th}$ shrank, contributing significant static power.
3. **Gate oxide tunneling** became severe as $t_{ox}$ approached 1–2 nm, forcing the introduction of **high-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂).
4. **Short-channel effects** (DIBL, hot-carrier injection) degraded transistor gain, requiring non-scalable innovations like **strained silicon, FinFETs**, and later **Gate-All-Around (GAA)**.

**Consequence:** Single-core clock frequencies plateaued at ~3–4 GHz since 2005. The industry responded with **multi-core CPUs**, where computational throughput is increased by parallelism while keeping per-core frequency and power density manageable.

### Solution to Question B(a)

**Mathematical Proof of $S^3$ Power Density Growth:**

Under **constant-voltage scaling** (where dimensions scale but $V_{DD}$ remains constant):

- Gate length: $L' = L/S$
- Gate width: $W' = W/S$
- Oxide thickness: $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/S$
- Capacitance: $C' = C/S$ (proportional to area)
- Frequency: $f' = S \cdot f$ (delay scales as $1/S$)
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$ (constant, no scaling)
- Transistor density: $N' = N \cdot S^2$ per unit area

**Power per transistor (scaled):**

$$P_{gate}' = \alpha C' (V_{DD}')^2 f' = \alpha \left(\frac{C}{S}\right) V_{DD}^2 (S f) = S \cdot \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f = S \cdot P_{gate}$$

**Power density (scaled):**

$$\rho_P' = \left(\frac{N'}{A}\right) \cdot P_{gate}' = \left(S^2 \cdot \frac{N}{A}\right) \cdot (S \cdot P_{gate})$$

$$\boxed{\rho_P' = S^3 \cdot \rho_P}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $S = 1.5$ (typical node shrink factor):
- $\rho_P$ increases by $(1.5)^3 = 3.375 \times$ per generation.
- In 4 generations, $\rho_P$ grows by $(1.5)^{12} \approx 129 \times$ — clearly unsustainable.

This mathematical reality is precisely why **Dennard Scaling collapsed** and gave rise to the **Power Wall**.

### Solution to Question B(b)

**Low-Power Design Techniques for Managing Power Density:**

**1. Clock Gating**
- Disables the clock signal to idle circuit blocks.
- Reduces dynamic power by setting $\alpha \to 0$ for the gated logic.
- Implemented by ANDing the clock with an enable signal at the flip-flop level.

**2. Power Gating (Multi-Threshold CMOS - MTCMOS)**
- Uses **sleep transistors** (high-$V_{th}$ devices) to completely cut $V_{DD}$ to inactive blocks.
- Reduces static leakage power to near zero during sleep mode.
- Trade-off: sleep transistor adds area and IR drop.

**3. Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)**
- Adjusts $V_{DD}$ and $f$ dynamically based on workload.
- Since $P \propto V_{DD}^2 f$, reducing $V_{DD}$ by 30% yields ~50% power savings.
- Used extensively in mobile SoCs (ARM big.LITTLE architecture).

**4. Multi-Core Architecture & Dark Silicon**
- Replaces one high-frequency core with several lower-frequency cores.
- Computational throughput scales linearly with core count at constant power.
- **Dark Silicon:** A fraction of the chip must remain powered OFF at any time to stay within the thermal envelope (~100 W/cm²).

**5. Near-Threshold and Sub-Threshold Computing**
- Operates transistors at $V_{DD}$ below $V_{th}$ (~0.4 V).
- Achieves 10–100× energy efficiency at the cost of performance.

**6. Architectural Innovations**
- **FinFET and GAA transistors:** Better electrostatic control, lower leakage.
- **3D Stacking (TSV):** Reduces interconnect capacitance by shortening wires.
- **Asymmetric Multi-Processing (AMP):** Heterogeneous cores for varied workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Energy and Power:** Dynamic power equation uses $f$, while energy does NOT. Students often write $E_{sw} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ — this is WRONG. Energy per cycle is $\alpha C V_{DD}^2$.
> 2. **Activity Factor Value:** Do NOT use $\alpha = 1$ for random data — use $\alpha = 0.5$ (probability of any transition) or $\alpha = 0.25$ (probability of $0 \to 1$ transition). Clarify which convention you use.
> 3. **Dennard Scaling Table:** Students often forget to show $V_{DD}$ scaling leads to constant $\rho_P$. Always tabulate all parameters and show the cancellation explicitly.
> 4. **Missing Units:** Power density MUST carry units of $\text{W/cm}^2$. Marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> 5. **Power Wall Date:** Examiners expect you to associate the Power Wall with **2004–2005** (Intel Pentium 4 "Prescott" thermal crisis). Mentioning only "recent years" loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Power Density at a Glance"**
>
> - **Definition:** $\rho_P = P_{total} / A_{die}$ — measured in **W/cm²**.
> - **Dynamic Power Formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — remember the **$V_{DD}^2$** dominance.
> - **Static Power Formula:** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — dominated by sub-threshold and gate-tunneling leakage.
> - **Dennard Scaling:** Predicted constant $\rho_P$ under constant-field scaling; **broke down** at ~90 nm due to $V_{DD}$ scaling limit.
> - **Constant-Voltage Scaling Reality:** Power density grows as **$S^3$** per generation.
> - **Power Wall:** Stuck at ~100 W/cm² (air-cooling limit) since ~2005.
> - **Reference Points:** Hot plate = 5–10 W/cm²; Nuclear reactor = 1000 W/cm²; Sun's surface = 6000 W/cm².
> - **Energy per Switch:** $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \alpha C V_{DD}^2$ (NOT multiplied by $f$).
> - **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = E_{sw} \cdot \tau_d$ — key FoM for logic families.
> - **Maximum Allowable Power:** $P_{max} = (T_{j,max} - T_{amb}) / \theta_{JA}$ — set by packaging.
> - **Mitigation Tools:** Clock gating, Power gating (MTCMOS), DVFS, Multi-core, Dark Silicon, Near-threshold computing.
> - **Architectural Shift:** Single-core $\to$ Multi-core $\to$ Heterogeneous (big.LITTLE, chiplets).
> - **Sub-threshold Slope Limit:** 60 mV/decade at 300 K — the **fundamental physical reason** Dennard Scaling failed.
> - **Modern Devices:** FinFET (22 nm onward) and GAA (3 nm onward) reduce leakage and improve electrostatic integrity.
> - **Historical Milestone:** Intel Pentium 4 "Prescott" (90 nm, 2004) marked the practical **end of frequency scaling**.
> - **Future Outlook:** Backside power delivery, chiplet architectures, 2D materials (MoS₂, graphene), cryogenic CMOS, and 3D heterogeneous integration are the next frontiers to manage power density.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Uniform Dopant Concentration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Uniform Dopant Concentration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In semiconductor device physics, a junction formed when the net doping concentration varies as a function of position within the semiconductor crystal is referred to as a **Non-Uniformly Doped Junction** (or graded junction). Mathematically, if the net doping is expressed as $N(x) = N_D(x) - N_A(x)$, then a junction is classified as *non-uniform* whenever $\frac{dN(x)}{dx} \neq 0$.

The two canonical forms encountered in KTU syllabus are:

1. **Linearly Graded Junction (LGJ):** The net dopant concentration varies linearly across the metallurgical junction, expressed as $N(x) = ax$, where $a$ has units of $\text{cm}^{-4}$ and represents the **doping gradient**.
2. **Exponentially Graded Junction:** The doping profile follows an exponential variation, often used in modeling diffused or ion-implanted junctions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of Nanoelectronics, the analysis of non-uniform dopant concentration forms the analytical foundation for understanding heterojunctions, quantum wells, and short-channel nanoscale MOSFETs where the source/drain profiles are intrinsically non-abrupt.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine two crowds of people (electrons and holes) standing in a long hallway. In an *abrupt* junction, all the "donor" people are squeezed at one end and all the "acceptor" people at the other end — there is a sharp, sudden wall between them. In a *non-uniform* junction, the donor crowd gradually thins out as you move toward the acceptor side, like a gradient of salt dissolving in water. This gradual transition drastically changes the electric "pressure" (electric field) and the size of the no-man's land (depletion region).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- Silicon permittivity: $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$
- Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = 0.0259 \text{ V}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A core KTU 2024 takeaway: For nanoelectronic devices with channel lengths below **45 nm**, the source/drain junctions are never abrupt — they are graded. Therefore, the classical abrupt-junction theory significantly overestimates the peak electric field, leading to unreliable predictions of band-to-band tunneling and breakdown voltage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

The analytical procedure to study any non-uniform junction follows a precise logical sequence:

- **Step 1 — Establish the Doping Profile:** Define $N(x)$ mathematically. For KTU problems, this is overwhelmingly $N(x) = ax$ (linearly graded) over the depletion region $-x_p \leq x \leq x_n$.
- **Step 2 — Apply the Depletion Approximation:** Assume that the mobile carriers (free electrons and holes) are completely swept out of the depletion region. The space charge is contributed *only* by the ionized dopants, so $\rho(x) = q \cdot N(x)$.
- **Step 3 — Invoke Poisson's Equation:** The one-dimensional Poisson equation in the depletion region becomes $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$.
- **Step 4 — Apply Charge Neutrality:** The total positive charge on the n-side must equal the total negative charge on the p-side: $\int_{-x_p}^{0} qax \, dx + \int_{0}^{x_n} qax \, dx = 0$, which gives the geometric relationship between $x_p$ and $x_n$.
- **Step 5 — Integrate Poisson's Equation Twice:** The first integration yields the electric field $E(x)$; the second integration yields the electrostatic potential $V(x)$.
- **Step 6 — Apply the Built-in Boundary Condition:** The total potential drop across the depletion region must equal the built-in potential $V_{bi}$, allowing the depletion width $W$ to be solved analytically.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why depletion approximation?** Because solving the full continuity equations is analytically intractable. The depletion approximation is a highly accurate simplification for the reverse-biased or equilibrium cases typically examined in KTU problems.
- **Why does charge neutrality hold?** Gauss's law demands that the net charge enclosed by a surface far outside the depletion region must be zero. This translates to equal areas under the charge density curve.
- **How does grading change the field profile?** In an abrupt junction, $\rho$ is a *constant* in each region, leading to a *linear* $E(x)$ and a *parabolic* $V(x)$. In a linearly graded junction, $\rho$ is *linear* in $x$, leading to a *parabolic* $E(x)$ and a *cubic* $V(x)$. This is a critical KTU 2024 insight.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Net doping profile (LGJ) | $N(x) = ax$ | $a$ is grading constant $[\text{cm}^{-4}]$ |
| 2 | Space charge density | $\rho(x) = qax$ | Valid inside depletion region |
| 3 | Poisson's equation | $\dfrac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{qa x}{\varepsilon_s}$ | 1-D form |
| 4 | Charge neutrality | $x_n = x_p = W/2$ | Symmetric LGJ assumption |
| 5 | Electric field $E(x)$ | $E(x) = \dfrac{qa}{2\varepsilon_s}\left[\left(\dfrac{W}{2}\right)^2 - x^2\right]$ | Parabolic profile |
| 6 | Peak electric field | $E_{max} = \dfrac{qa W^2}{8 \varepsilon_s}$ | Occurs at $x = 0$ (junction) |
| 7 | Built-in potential | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{qa W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}$ | Cubic dependence on $W$ |
| 8 | Depletion width | $W = \left(\dfrac{12 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{qa}\right)^{1/3}$ | Cube-root scaling |
| 9 | Junction capacitance / area | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{W}$ | Standard parallel-plate model |
| 10 | $C$–$V$ dependence | $C \propto (V_{bi} - V)^{-1/3}$ | Hall signature of LGJ |
| 11 | Abrupt junction $C$–$V$ | $C \propto (V_{bi} - V)^{-1/2}$ | For contrast with LGJ |
| 12 | Total depletion width | $W = x_p + x_n$ | Always positive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The hallmark experimental fingerprint of a **linearly graded junction** is a $1/3$ exponent in the $1/C^3$ versus $V$ plot, whereas an abrupt junction gives a $1/2$ exponent. This is a frequently tested KTU viva and short-answer concept.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-uniform doping analysis is the cornerstone of designing:

- **Hyper-abrupt varactor diodes** for voltage-controlled oscillators (VCOs) in RF front-ends.
- **Nanoscale MOSFET source/drain extensions** where the junction is graded by design to suppress short-channel effects.
- **Avalanche photodiodes (APDs)** where the field profile must be tailored to control the multiplication region.
- **Solar cell emitter profiles** where grading enhances carrier collection efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Electric Field for a Linearly Graded Junction

**Given:** A symmetric linearly graded p-n junction with $N(x) = ax$ over $-W/2 \leq x \leq W/2$.

**Step 1 — State Poisson's equation in the depletion region:**

Inside the depletion region, the space charge density equals the ionized dopant charge:

$$\rho(x) = q a x$$

Poisson's equation in one dimension is:

$$\frac{d^2 V(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q a x}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2 — First integration to obtain the electric field:**

Since $E(x) = -\frac{dV(x)}{dx}$, we integrate Poisson's equation once:

$$\frac{dE(x)}{dx} = \frac{q a x}{\varepsilon_s}$$

$$E(x) = \int \frac{q a x}{\varepsilon_s} dx = \frac{q a x^2}{2 \varepsilon_s} + C_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary condition at the depletion edge:**

At $x = \pm W/2$, the electric field must vanish because we are at the edge of the depletion region where charge neutrality is restored:

$$E\left(\pm \frac{W}{2}\right) = 0$$

Applying this:

$$0 = \frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left(\frac{W}{2}\right)^2 + C_1 \implies C_1 = -\frac{q a W^2}{8 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Final expression for the electric field:**

$$\boxed{E(x) = \frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\left(\frac{W}{2}\right)^2 - x^2\right]}$$

This is a **parabolic** function of $x$, with a maximum at $x = 0$:

$$E_{max} = E(0) = \frac{q a W^2}{8 \varepsilon_s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Built-in Potential for a Linearly Graded Junction

**Step 1 — Integrate the electric field to get the potential:**

$$V(x) = -\int E(x) dx = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\frac{W^2 x}{4} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right] + C_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the symmetry boundary condition:**

By symmetry of the linearly graded profile, the potential at $x = 0$ can be taken as the reference (i.e., $V(0) = 0$). This gives $C_2 = 0$ trivially for the relative potential.

**Step 3 — Compute the total potential drop across the depletion region:**

The total built-in potential is the potential difference between $x = -W/2$ and $x = +W/2$:

$$V_{bi} = V\left(\frac{W}{2}\right) - V\left(-\frac{W}{2}\right)$$

Due to the antisymmetric nature of $V(x)$ about the origin:

$$V\left(\frac{W}{2}\right) = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\frac{W^2}{4} \cdot \frac{W}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{W}{2}\right)^3\right] = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\frac{W^3}{8} - \frac{W^3}{24}\right]$$

$$V\left(\frac{W}{2}\right) = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{3W^3 - W^3}{24} = -\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}$$

By antisymmetry, $V(-W/2) = +\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}$, so:

$$V_{bi} = -\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s} - \frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q a W^3}{6 \varepsilon_s}$$

Taking the magnitude (built-in potential is a positive quantity):

$$\boxed{V_{bi} = \frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Depletion Width in Terms of Built-in Potential

**Step 1 — Solve the boxed equation for $W$:**

Starting from:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}$$

Isolate $W^3$:

$$W^3 = \frac{12 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q a}$$

**Step 2 — Take the cube root:**

$$\boxed{W = \left(\frac{12 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q a}\right)^{1/3}}$$

This is a critically important result for KTU numerical problems. Notice the **cube-root** dependence on $V_{bi}$, in stark contrast to the square-root dependence for abrupt junctions.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Junction Capacitance for a Linearly Graded Junction

**Step 1 — Recall the parallel-plate capacitance formula per unit area:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{W}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for $W$:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{\left(\frac{12 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q a}\right)^{1/3}} = \varepsilon_s^{2/3} \left(\frac{q a}{12 V_{bi}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to applied reverse bias $V_R$:**

Under reverse bias, $V_{bi}$ is replaced by $(V_{bi} + V_R)$:

$$\boxed{C = \varepsilon_s^{2/3} \left(\frac{q a}{12 (V_{bi} + V_R)}\right)^{1/3} \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/3}}$$

This yields the famous linear relationship:

$$\frac{1}{C^3} = \frac{12 (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q a \varepsilon_s^2}$$

Plotting $1/C^3$ versus $V_R$ gives a straight line with slope $\frac{12}{qa\varepsilon_s^2}$, from which the grading constant $a$ can be experimentally extracted — a classic KTU experiment and derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Analysis Flow for Non-Uniform Junction Characterization

The following block-level flowchart maps the complete analytical pipeline used by KTU examiners to assess a non-uniform junction problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Doping Profile N of x] --> B[Identify Profile Type]
    B --> B1[Linearly Graded: N of x = a times x]
    B --> B2[Exponentially Graded: N of x = N0 exp of alpha x]
    B1 --> C[Apply Depletion Approximation]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Formulate Space Charge Density rho of x]
    D --> E[Apply 1D Poisson Equation]
    E --> F[Integrate Once to get E of x]
    F --> F1[Apply Boundary Condition: E at edge of W equals 0]
    F1 --> G[Integrate Again to get V of x]
    G --> G1[Apply V at x equals W over 2 minus V at x equals minus W over 2 equals Vbi]
    G1 --> H[Solve Algebraically for W]
    H --> I[Compute Peak Field Emax]
    H --> J[Compute Capacitance C per unit area]
    I --> K[End: Complete Electrical Characterization]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Abrupt vs. Linearly Graded Junctions

This flowchart contrasts the analytical and physical attributes of the two junction types — a frequently tested KTU comparison question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AJ[Abrupt Junction]
        AJ1[Constant N on each side] --> AJ2[Constant rho in each region]
        AJ2 --> AJ3[Linear E of x]
        AJ3 --> AJ4[Parabolic V of x]
        AJ4 --> AJ5[W scales as Vbi to the power 1 over 2]
        AJ5 --> AJ6[C scales as V to the power minus 1 over 2]
    end
    subgraph LGJ[Linearly Graded Junction]
        LGJ1[Linear N of x = a times x] --> LGJ2[Linear rho of x]
        LGJ2 --> LGJ3[Parabolic E of x]
        LGJ3 --> LGJ4[Cubic V of x]
        LGJ4 --> LGJ5[W scales as Vbi to the power 1 over 3]
        LGJ5 --> LGJ6[C scales as V to the power minus 1 over 3]
    end
```

### 4.3 Spatial Profile Mapping (Qualitative Representation)

The diagram below provides a qualitative spatial mapping of the doping, charge, field, and potential profiles across a linearly graded junction. Positive $x$ corresponds to the n-side, negative $x$ to the p-side.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Doping N of x : Linear ramp from negative a to positive a"] --> P2["Charge rho of x : Linear from negative qa W over 2 to positive qa W over 2"]
    P2 --> P3["Electric Field E of x : Parabolic, zero at edges, maximum at x equals 0"]
    P3 --> P4["Potential V of x : Antisymmetric cubic, total drop equals Vbi"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams substitute physical drawings with functional mappings, which is the KTU-accepted best practice for analyzing spatially varying profiles in nanoelectronic devices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define a linearly graded p-n junction. How is it mathematically distinguished from an abrupt junction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **linearly graded p-n junction** is a semiconductor junction in which the net dopant concentration varies linearly with position across the metallurgical junction. Mathematically, the net doping is expressed as $N(x) = ax$, where $a$ is the **doping gradient** with units of $\text{cm}^{-4}$ and $x$ is measured from the junction.

In contrast, an **abrupt junction** has constant and distinct doping concentrations on the p-side and n-side, with an instantaneous transition at $x = 0$. For an abrupt junction, $N(x) = -N_A$ for $x < 0$ and $N(x) = +N_D$ for $x > 0$, so $\frac{dN}{dx}$ is a Dirac delta function at the boundary, not a smooth gradient. *(3 marks: definition 1, mathematical distinction 1, doping profile comparison 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

State the relationship between depletion width $W$ and built-in potential $V_{bi}$ for a linearly graded junction, and mention its experimental significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a linearly graded junction, the depletion width is related to the built-in potential by the **cube-root** law:

$$W = \left(\frac{12 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q a}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Experimental significance:** This $W \propto V_{bi}^{1/3}$ relationship leads to a junction capacitance that scales as $C \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/3}$. Therefore, a plot of $1/C^3$ versus reverse bias $V_R$ yields a straight line, whose slope is $\frac{12}{q a \varepsilon_s^2}$. This is the standard experimental method (the **Schottky C-V profiling technique**) used to extract the grading constant $a$ in a real graded junction. *(3 marks: formula 1, cube-root distinction 1, experimental application 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the built-in potential $V_{bi}$ of a symmetric linearly graded p-n junction with doping profile $N(x) = ax$, using Poisson's equation and the depletion approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon linearly graded junction has a grading constant $a = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-4}$ at room temperature. If the measured zero-bias depletion width is $W = 0.5 \mu m$, compute (i) the built-in potential $V_{bi}$, and (ii) the peak electric field $E_{max}$. Given: $\varepsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Define the space charge density.** Under the depletion approximation, the charge density in the depletion region $(-W/2 \leq x \leq W/2)$ is:

$$\rho(x) = q a x$$

**Step 2: Write Poisson's equation.**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{q a x}{\varepsilon_s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: First integration to obtain $E(x)$.**

$$E(x) = \frac{q a x^2}{2 \varepsilon_s} + C_1$$

Applying the boundary condition $E(\pm W/2) = 0$:

$$C_1 = -\frac{q a W^2}{8 \varepsilon_s}$$

$$E(x) = \frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\left(\frac{W}{2}\right)^2 - x^2\right] \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Second integration to obtain $V(x)$.**

$$V(x) = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\frac{W^2 x}{4} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right] + C_2$$

By symmetry, $V(0) = 0$, so $C_2 = 0$. The potential at $x = W/2$ is:

$$V\left(\frac{W}{2}\right) = -\frac{q a}{2 \varepsilon_s}\left[\frac{W^3}{8} - \frac{W^3}{24}\right] = -\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5: Total built-in potential (antisymmetry argument).**

$$V_{bi} = V\left(\frac{W}{2}\right) - V\left(-\frac{W}{2}\right) = -\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s} - \left(+\frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}\right) = -\frac{q a W^3}{6 \varepsilon_s}$$

Taking magnitude:

$$\boxed{V_{bi} = \frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s}} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $a = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-4}$, $W = 0.5 \mu m = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$, $\varepsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$.

**(i) Built-in potential $V_{bi}$:**

$$V_{bi} = \frac{q a W^3}{12 \varepsilon_s} \quad \text{...(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution)}$$

Compute $W^3$:

$$W^3 = (0.5 \times 10^{-4})^3 = 0.125 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-13} \text{ cm}^3$$

Substitute:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{18})(1.25 \times 10^{-13})}{12 \times (1.04 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$V_{bi} = \frac{1.6 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-14}}{12.48 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.248 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{bi} = 1.603 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.603 \text{ mV}} \quad \text{...(1 mark for answer + units)}$$

**(ii) Peak electric field $E_{max}$:**

$$E_{max} = \frac{q a W^2}{8 \varepsilon_s} \quad \text{...(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution)}$$

Compute $W^2$:

$$W^2 = (0.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 0.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ cm}^2$$

Substitute:

$$E_{max} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{18})(2.5 \times 10^{-9})}{8 \times (1.04 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$E_{max} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-10}}{8.32 \times 10^{-12}} = 48.08 \text{ V/cm}$$

$$\boxed{E_{max} \approx 48.1 \text{ V/cm}} \quad \text{...(1 mark for answer + units)}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of Poisson's equation, derive the expression for the depletion capacitance per unit area of a linearly graded junction. Show that $1/C^3$ varies linearly with the applied reverse bias voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a silicon linearly graded junction, $C$–$V$ measurements yield a straight line on a $1/C^3$ versus $V_R$ plot with slope $S = 5 \times 10^{23} \text{ F}^{-3}\text{V}^{-1}\text{cm}^{6}$ and intercept corresponding to $V_{bi} = 0.65 \text{ V}$. Calculate the grading constant $a$. Given: $\varepsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Expression for capacitance per unit area.** From Section 3.4 derivation, the depletion capacitance per unit area of a linearly graded junction under reverse bias $V_R$ is:

$$C = \varepsilon_s^{2/3} \left(\frac{q a}{12 (V_{bi} + V_R)}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{...(2 marks for derivation reference)}$$

**Step 2: Cube both sides.**

$$C^3 = \varepsilon_s^2 \cdot \frac{q a}{12 (V_{bi} + V_R)}$$

**Step 3: Invert.**

$$\frac{1}{C^3} = \frac{12 (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q a \varepsilon_s^2} = \frac{12}{q a \varepsilon_s^2} V_R + \frac{12 V_{bi}}{q a \varepsilon_s^2} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.** This is a linear equation in $V_R$ of the form $y = mx + c$ where:

- **Slope** $m = \frac{12}{q a \varepsilon_s^2}$
- **Intercept** $c = \frac{12 V_{bi}}{q a \varepsilon_s^2}$

Therefore, $1/C^3$ varies **linearly** with reverse bias $V_R$, in contrast to $1/C^2$ for an abrupt junction. *(2 marks for the conclusion and distinction from abrupt junction)*

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the slope formula.** From Part (a):

$$S = \frac{12}{q a \varepsilon_s^2}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the grading constant $a$.**

$$a = \frac{12}{S \cdot q \cdot \varepsilon_s^2} \quad \text{...(2 marks for formula)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

- $S = 5 \times 10^{23} \text{ F}^{-3}\text{V}^{-1}\text{cm}^{6}$
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $\varepsilon_s^2 = (1.04 \times 10^{-12})^2 = 1.0816 \times 10^{-24} \text{ F}^2/\text{cm}^2$

Compute the denominator:

$$S \cdot q \cdot \varepsilon_s^2 = (5 \times 10^{23})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.0816 \times 10^{-24})$$

$$= 5 \times 1.6 \times 1.0816 \times 10^{23-19-24} = 8.6528 \times 10^{-20} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Therefore:

$$a = \frac{12}{8.6528 \times 10^{-20}} = 1.387 \times 10^{20}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 1.39 \times 10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-4}} \quad \text{...(3 marks: 2 for calculation, 1 for answer + units)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse units of $\varepsilon_s$ (F/cm) with $(\text{F/cm})^2$ when squaring, leading to errors spanning 24 orders of magnitude. Always write out the units explicitly during the substitution step. Additionally, ensure that the slope is correctly identified — a sign error (using $-S$ instead of $+S$) will yield a negative grading constant, which is physically impossible and should immediately signal a mistake. Finally, do not confuse the grading constant $a$ (units $\text{cm}^{-4}$) with doping concentration $N$ (units $\text{cm}^{-3}$); the grader specifically checks unit consistency in the final boxed answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Non-Uniform Dopant Concentration (Module 1):**

- **Definition:** Non-uniform junctions have $N(x) = f(x)$ rather than a step function. The **linearly graded junction** $N(x) = ax$ is the most analytically tractable and most frequently tested.
- **Key Mathematical Operator:** Poisson's equation in 1D is $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$, where $\rho(x) = qax$ in the depletion region.
- **Electric Field Profile:** Parabolic, $E(x) = \frac{qa}{2\varepsilon_s}\left[\left(\frac{W}{2}\right)^2 - x^2\right]$, with **peak at the metallurgical junction** $x = 0$.
- **Peak Field Formula:** $E_{max} = \frac{qaW^2}{8\varepsilon_s}$ — quadratic in $W$.
- **Built-in Potential:** $V_{bi} = \frac{qaW^3}{12\varepsilon_s}$ — **cubic** in $W$.
- **Depletion Width:** $W = \left(\frac{12\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{qa}\right)^{1/3}$ — **cube-root** dependence on $V_{bi}$.
- **Capacitance Signature:** $C \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/3}$ → $1/C^3$ is **linear** in $V_R$ → slope yields $a$.
- **Comparison with Abrupt:** Abrupt gives $1/C^2$ linearity; graded gives $1/C^3$ linearity. This is the **forensic experimental test** to determine junction type from C-V data.
- **Symmetry:** For a symmetric linearly graded profile, $x_p = x_n = W/2$, which is a useful simplification in KTU numericals.
- **Units Vigilance:** $a$ is in $\text{cm}^{-4}$, $W$ in cm, $V_{bi}$ in volts, $E$ in V/cm, $C$ in F/cm². Always check dimensional consistency.
- **Nanoelectronics Relevance:** Modern nanoscale MOSFETs have inherently graded source/drain junctions; the abrupt-junction assumption **fails below ~45 nm** channel length, making this topic a direct prerequisite for short-channel device analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Threshold Voltage Scaling — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) device physics and nanoelectronics, the **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)** is formally defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) required to induce a conducting inversion layer (channel) of electrons (NMOS) or holes (PMOS) at the semiconductor-oxide interface, such that the surface potential $\phi_s$ reaches the value $2\phi_F$ (twice the bulk Fermi potential), and the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration.

Mathematically, for an ideal long-channel NMOS device:
$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Where:
- $V_{FB}$ = Flat-band voltage
- $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ = Bulk Fermi potential
- $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm = **permittivity of silicon**
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C = **electronic charge**
- $N_A$ = Acceptor doping concentration in the p-type substrate
- $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ = Oxide capacitance per unit area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Threshold Voltage Scaling is the **backbone concept** of Module 1. Without understanding how $V_{th}$ behaves as the channel length $L$ shrinks into the nanometer regime, students cannot comprehend modern CMOS design, power-performance trade-offs, or multi-$V_{th}$ techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Reservoir" Model

Imagine a water reservoir (the p-type substrate) behind a dam (the MOS gate stack). The dam's gate is the **gate oxide + gate electrode**, and water represents electrons.

- The **water level** inside the reservoir = bulk Fermi level of the p-type substrate.
- To let water flow into the lower channel (forming the n-channel), the gate must lift the water level above a critical spillover point — this critical lifting force is exactly the **Threshold Voltage**.
- If the dam is **made thicker (larger $t_{ox}$)** → more force (voltage) is needed → $V_{th}$ rises.
- If the **drain pulls water aggressively** (high $V_{DS}$) → the dam's effective height lowers → $V_{th}$ falls → this is **DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering)**.
- If the **reservoir is filled more** (higher substrate bias $V_{SB}$) → water level rises relative to spillover → $V_{th}$ rises → this is the **Body Effect (Back-Bias Effect)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick intuition:** Scaling $V_{th}$ downward improves transistor speed and current drive ($I_{on}$), but **exponentially increases OFF-state leakage** because $I_{off} \propto 10^{-V_{th}/S}$ where $S$ is the subthreshold swing. This fundamental trade-off is what makes threshold voltage scaling the most critical design knob in nano-CMOS.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Bulk CMOS) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | **25.85 mV at 300 K** |
| Silicon Permittivity | $\varepsilon_{si}$ | **$1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm** |
| Oxide Permittivity | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **$3.45 \times 10^{-13}$ F/cm** |
| $\varepsilon_{si}/\varepsilon_{ox}$ ratio | — | **3.9** (relative permittivity of SiO₂ ≈ 3.9) |
| Intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i$ | **$1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ at 300 K** |
| Threshold Voltage (modern 45 nm) | $V_{th}$ | **≈ 0.3 V (low-$V_{th}$) to 0.5 V (high-$V_{th}$)** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Long-Channel Threshold Voltage — The Baseline

For an ideal long-channel MOSFET (channel length $L \gg$ depletion widths), the threshold voltage consists of three additive components:

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:
1. **Flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$** — compensates for the work-function difference between the gate material and the silicon substrate, plus any trapped oxide charge. $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$.
2. **Surface potential barrier $2\phi_F$** — the gate must overcome this to bend the bands enough to invert the surface.
3. **Depletion region charge term** — the gate voltage must additionally terminate the ionized acceptor charge in the depletion region underneath the gate, which is modeled by the $\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)} / C_{ox}$ term.

This gives the canonical long-channel expression:
$$V_{th} = \underbrace{V_{FB}}_{\text{work function}} + \underbrace{2\phi_F}_{\text{band bending}} + \underbrace{\frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}}_{\text{depletion charge}}$$

## 2.2 Body Effect (Back-Bias Effect)

When a substrate bias $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied (NMOS in p-substrate), the source-to-body junction becomes reverse biased, widening the depletion region. This raises the threshold voltage:

$$V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

Where the **body-effect coefficient** is:
$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ has units of **V$^{1/2}$**. Lower $\gamma$ (thinner $t_{ox}$, lighter $N_A$) means smaller $V_{th}$ sensitivity to substrate noise — a critical concern in mixed-signal SoCs.

## 2.3 Short-Channel Effects on Threshold Voltage

When the channel length $L$ enters the deep submicron regime, **two-dimensional electric field effects** dominate, and the threshold voltage no longer follows the 1-D long-channel model:

### (a) **Threshold Voltage Roll-Off ($V_{th}$ Roll-Off)**
- **Definition:** As $L$ decreases below ~0.25 µm (250 nm), $V_{th}$ *decreases* because the source and drain depletion regions consume a significant fraction of the channel charge that the gate must induce.
- **Empirical model (semi-empirical BSIM-style):**
$$V_{th}(L) = V_{th}(\infty) - \Delta V_{th,roll-off}$$
- The roll-off is approximated by:
$$\Delta V_{th} \approx \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}} \left[ \frac{r_j}{L} \left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{2x_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right) \right]$$
Where $r_j$ is the source/drain junction depth and $x_d$ is the depletion width.

### (b) **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)**
- **Definition:** For short-channel devices, the drain electric field penetrates toward the source, lowering the source-to-channel potential barrier and effectively reducing $V_{th}$.
- **Mathematical model:**
$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$
$$\sigma_{DIBL} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{2\varepsilon_{ox}} \cdot \frac{t_{ox}}{L} \cdot \frac{t_{ox}}{L_{eff}}$$
- More accurately, using the 2-D Poisson solution:
$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\eta \, C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{0.64 \, (\phi_{bi} - V_{DS})}{L}$$

### (c) **Reverse Short-Channel Effect (RSCE)**
- Caused by non-uniform channel doping (e.g., halo/retrograde wells used to suppress punch-through).
- For moderate channel lengths (≈ 0.13–0.25 µm), $V_{th}$ actually *increases* as $L$ decreases, then decreases sharply at very short $L$ — giving the famous **"hump" in the $V_{th}$–$L$ curve**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** All formulas below must be memorized — they appear in Part B (14-mark) derivations almost every semester.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | Long-channel threshold voltage | V |
| 2 | $\phi_F = (kT/q) \ln(N_A/n_i)$ | Bulk Fermi potential | V |
| 3 | $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 4 | $V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Threshold with body bias | V |
| 5 | $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}/C_{ox}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $V_{th}(L) = V_{th,long} - \Delta V_{th,DIBL} - \Delta V_{th,roll\text{-}off}$ | Short-channel effective $V_{th}$ | V |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | DIBL shift | V |
| 8 | $\sigma_{DIBL} \approx 0.5 \, (t_{ox}/L)^3 \cdot (\varepsilon_{si}/\varepsilon_{ox})$ | DIBL coefficient (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_{off} = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{th}/S}$ | Subthreshold leakage current | A |
| 10 | $S = (kT/q) \ln(10) \cdot (1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox})$ | Subthreshold swing | V/decade |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power TCAD tools (Sentaurus, ATLAS) used by Intel, TSMC, and Samsung to calibrate process design kits (PDKs). Every modern smartphone SoC (Apple A17, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3) has its $V_{th}$ targets verified against these exact models.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Long-Channel Threshold Voltage

Starting from Gauss's law at the onset of strong inversion:

**Step 1:** Apply charge neutrality at the surface. The total charge per unit area on the gate equals the sum of charge in the oxide, semiconductor depletion region, and inversion layer (negligible at threshold):
$$Q_G = Q_{ox} + Q_{semiconductor} = Q_{ox} + Q_B + Q_n \approx Q_{ox} + Q_B$$

At the **onset of strong inversion**, the surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$, and the depletion charge density is:
$$Q_B = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}$$

**Step 2:** The gate voltage is the sum of voltage drops:
$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \psi_s + \frac{\vert Q_B \vert}{C_{ox}}$$

Substituting $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ at threshold:
$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 3:** By definition, at threshold $V_{GS} = V_{th}$, giving the canonical expression:
$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Body Effect Equation

When source-to-substrate bias $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied, the depletion region charge changes to:
$$Q_B(V_{SB}) = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

The gate voltage at threshold becomes:
$$V_{GS}(V_{SB}) = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + V_{SB} + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

Subtracting the original $V_{th0}$ (when $V_{SB} = 0$):
$$V_{th}(V_{SB}) - V_{th0} = V_{SB} + \frac{1}{C_{ox}}\left[\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})} - \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}\right]$$

Recognizing that the $V_{SB}$ term inside the bracket cancels with part of the body coefficient and rearranging:
$$\boxed{V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}/C_{ox}$ is the body-effect coefficient.

## 3.3 Derivation of DIBL-Induced Threshold Voltage Shift

For a short-channel device, the 2-D Poisson equation in the channel must be solved. Assuming a constant doping profile and using the depletion approximation, the surface potential $\psi(x,y)$ along the channel satisfies:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

Using the **method of matched asymptotic expansions** (or numerical 2-D solution), the minimum surface potential $\psi_{min}$ occurs at some point $x_0$ inside the channel. The shift due to the drain bias is approximately:

$$\Delta \psi_{min} = -\frac{2\varepsilon_{si} \, t_{ox}}{\eta \, \varepsilon_{ox} \, L} \cdot (\phi_{bi} - V_{DS})$$

Where:
- $\eta$ is a fitting parameter accounting for channel doping profile (typically $1 < \eta < 2$),
- $\phi_{bi}$ is the built-in potential of the source/drain junction,
- $L$ is the effective channel length.

Since $\Delta V_{th} \approx \Delta \psi_{min}$ (the gate voltage must compensate for this barrier lowering), we obtain:
$$\boxed{\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\eta \, C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{0.64 (\phi_{bi} - V_{DS})}{L}}$$

The negative sign indicates $V_{th}$ *decreases* as $V_{DS}$ increases — the hallmark of DIBL.

## 3.4 Total Effective Short-Channel Threshold Voltage

Combining all short-channel corrections:
$$\boxed{V_{th}^{eff}(L, V_{DS}, V_{SB}) = V_{th,long}(V_{SB}) - \underbrace{\frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\eta \, C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{0.64 (\phi_{bi} - V_{DS})}{L}}_{\text{DIBL}} - \underbrace{\Delta V_{roll-off}(L)}_{\text{Roll-Off}} + \underbrace{\Delta V_{RSCE}(L)}_{\text{RSCE (hump)}}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor has $N_A = 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 5$ nm, gate is n$^+$ polysilicon, source-body bias $V_{SB} = 1$ V. Compute $V_{th}$ and $V_{th}(V_{SB})$.

**Given:**
- $\varepsilon_{si} = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm
- $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}$ F/cm
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
- $T = 300$ K, $kT/q = 0.02585$ V
- $V_{FB} \approx -0.95$ V (n$^+$ poly on p-Si with $N_A = 10^{17}$)

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\phi_F$:
$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \ln\left(\frac{10^{17}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.02585 \ln(6.67 \times 10^{6}) = 0.02585 \times 15.71 = 0.406 \text{ V}$$

Step 2 — Compute $C_{ox}$:
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$$

Step 3 — Compute depletion term:
$$\sqrt{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{17} \times (2 \times 0.406)}$$
$$= \sqrt{6.656 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.812} = \sqrt{5.40 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.32 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/cm}^2$$

Step 4 — Divide by $C_{ox}$:
$$\frac{2.32 \times 10^{-7}}{6.9 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.336 \text{ V}$$

Step 5 — Sum:
$$V_{th} = -0.95 + 2(0.406) + 0.336 = -0.95 + 0.812 + 0.336 = \boxed{0.198 \text{ V}}$$

Step 6 — Body effect: $\gamma = 0.336 / \sqrt{0.812} = 0.373$ V$^{1/2}$
$$V_{th}(V_{SB}=1) = 0.198 + 0.373 \times (\sqrt{0.812 + 1} - \sqrt{0.812})$$
$$= 0.198 + 0.373 \times (1.343 - 0.901) = 0.198 + 0.165 = \boxed{0.363 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** Substrate bias of 1 V raises $V_{th}$ by **0.165 V** — a significant shift used in adaptive body-biasing for low-power standby modes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define threshold voltage of a MOSFET. List the three main components of the long-channel threshold voltage expression and briefly explain the significance of each.**

**Model Answer:**
The threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) of a MOSFET is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting inversion layer between source and drain at the semiconductor-oxide interface. For an NMOS device, it is the voltage at which the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration, with surface potential $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$.

**Three components of $V_{th}$:**
1. **$V_{FB}$ (Flat-band voltage):** Compensates the work-function difference between gate and silicon, plus any fixed oxide charge. Sets the starting reference potential.
2. **$2\phi_F$ (Surface potential term):** The gate must bend the energy bands downward by $2\phi_F$ to invert the p-type surface into n-type. $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$.
3. **$\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ (Depletion charge term):** The gate must additionally terminate the ionized acceptor charge in the depletion region below the oxide.

Significance: Together these determine whether the transistor is ON/OFF and directly control leakage current and switching speed.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the body effect? Why does it become problematic in nanoscale CMOS designs?**

**Model Answer:**
The **body effect (back-bias effect)** describes the dependence of threshold voltage $V_{th}$ on the source-to-substrate voltage $V_{SB}$. When $V_{SB} > 0$, the source-body pn-junction becomes reverse-biased, widening the depletion region and increasing the charge that the gate must terminate. This raises $V_{th}$ according to:
$$V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

**Why problematic in nanoscale CMOS:**
- Modern bulk CMOS has high $N_A$ ($\sim 10^{18}$ cm⁻³), giving a large $\gamma$, so even small $V_{SB}$ variations cause large $V_{th}$ shifts.
- Random dopant fluctuations (RDF) at small geometries cause $\gamma$ to vary between nominally identical transistors → mismatch.
- Adaptive body biasing (ABB) requires precise $V_{th}$ control, which is harder as $\gamma$ increases.
- Fully-depleted SOI and FinFET devices intrinsically suppress body effect, which is a key motivation for moving away from bulk CMOS at advanced nodes.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of a long-channel NMOS transistor, clearly stating the underlying assumptions and the significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has the following parameters: substrate doping $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, gate is n$^+$ polysilicon, source-body bias $V_{SB} = 0.5$ V. Calculate:
- (i) Flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$ (assume $Q_{ox}/q = 5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻² and $\phi_M = 4.05$ eV)
- (ii) Fermi potential $\phi_F$
- (iii) Threshold voltage $V_{th0}$ (at $V_{SB} = 0$)
- (iv) Threshold voltage $V_{th}$ at $V_{SB} = 0.5$ V
- (v) Body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
Start with Gauss's law at the onset of strong inversion. At threshold, $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$, and applying charge neutrality:

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \psi_s + \frac{\vert Q_B \vert}{C_{ox}}$$

Substituting $Q_B = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}$ and $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ at threshold:
$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ and $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$.

**Assumptions:** (1) 1-D analysis (channel length long), (2) uniform substrate doping, (3) gradual channel approximation, (4) ideal oxide (no interface traps).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) **Flat-band voltage:**
$$\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S = \phi_M - (\chi_{Si} + E_g/2 + \phi_F)$$
With $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $\chi_{Si} = 4.05$ eV (so $\chi_{Si} + E_g/2 = 4.61$ eV):
$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \ln(5 \times 10^{16}/1.5 \times 10^{10}) = 0.02585 \times 15.32 = 0.396 \text{ V}$$
$$\phi_S = 4.61 + 0.396 = 5.006 \text{ eV (approx)}$$
$$\phi_{MS} = 4.05 - 5.006 = -0.956 \text{ V}$$
$$Q_{ox}/C_{ox} = q(5 \times 10^{10})/(3.45 \times 10^{-13}/10 \times 10^{-7}) = (8 \times 10^{-9})/(3.45 \times 10^{-7}) = 0.0232 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{FB} = -0.956 - 0.0232 = -0.979 \text{ V}$$

(ii) **$\phi_F = 0.396$ V** (computed above)

(iii) **$V_{th0}$:**
$$C_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}/(10 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.45 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$$
$$\sqrt{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{16} \times (2 \times 0.396)}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.664 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.792} = \sqrt{1.318 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.148 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/cm}^2$$
$$V_{th0} = -0.979 + 2(0.396) + 1.148 \times 10^{-7}/3.45 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$V_{th0} = -0.979 + 0.792 + 0.333 = \mathbf{0.146 \text{ V}}$$

(iv) **$\gamma$ and $V_{th}(V_{SB} = 0.5)$:**
$$\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox} = 1.148 \times 10^{-7}/3.45 \times 10^{-7} = 0.333 \text{ V}^{1/2}$$
$$V_{th}(0.5) = 0.146 + 0.333(\sqrt{0.792 + 0.5} - \sqrt{0.792})$$
$$= 0.146 + 0.333(1.137 - 0.890) = 0.146 + 0.0823 = \mathbf{0.228 \text{ V}}$$

(v) **$\gamma = 0.333$ V$^{1/2}$**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Restructured]**

**(a)** Explain the short-channel effects on threshold voltage. With neat expressions, describe **DIBL, threshold voltage roll-off, and reverse short-channel effect (RSCE)**. How do these phenomena affect modern nanoscale CMOS design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 28 nm bulk NMOS transistor has $t_{ox} = 1.2$ nm (equivalent oxide thickness, EOT), effective channel length $L_{eff} = 30$ nm, drain bias $V_{DS} = 1$ V, source-body bias $V_{SB} = 0$, and built-in potential $\phi_{bi} = 0.9$ V. The fitting parameter $\eta = 1.2$. Compute:
- (i) DIBL-induced threshold voltage shift $\Delta V_{th,DIBL}$
- (ii) Effective threshold voltage if long-channel $V_{th} = 0.45$ V
- (iii) Subthreshold leakage current $I_{off}$ at $V_{GS} = 0$, given $I_0 = 100$ µA and subthreshold swing $S = 90$ mV/decade
- (iv) What happens to $I_{off}$ if the channel length shrinks to 20 nm? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory:**
Short-channel effects (SCE) arise when $L$ approaches the source/drain depletion widths, violating the gradual channel approximation. Three key effects:

1. **$V_{th}$ Roll-Off:** As $L$ decreases, the source and drain depletion regions share control of the channel charge with the gate. The gate must induce less charge to invert the surface → $V_{th}$ drops.
$$\Delta V_{th,roll-off} \approx \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F+V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{r_j}{2L}$$

2. **DIBL:** The drain electric field penetrates toward the source, lowering the source-side barrier.
$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{si}}{\eta \, C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{0.64(\phi_{bi} - V_{DS})}{L}$$

3. **RSCE:** Non-uniform doping (halo implants) causes $V_{th}$ to *increase* for moderate $L$ before collapsing — produces a hump in the $V_{th}$–$L$ curve.

**Impact on design:** Forces adoption of FinFET/GAAFET architectures, multi-$V_{th}$ libraries, and precise doping control.

**(b) Numerical:**

(i) **DIBL shift:**
$$C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}/(1.2 \times 10^{-7}) = 2.875 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F/cm}^2$$
$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\frac{1.04 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 2.875 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{0.64(0.9 - 1)}{30 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$= -\frac{1.04 \times 10^{-12}}{3.45 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{0.64 \times (-0.1)}{3 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$= -3.014 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (-2.133 \times 10^{4}) = 6.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx \mathbf{-6.4 \text{ mV}}$$

Wait — note that $V_{DS} > \phi_{bi}$, so $\phi_{bi} - V_{DS} = -0.1$ V; the DIBL formula gives negative shift (lowering $V_{th}$):
$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} \approx \mathbf{-0.0064 \text{ V} = -6.4 \text{ mV}}$$

(ii) **Effective $V_{th}$:**
$$V_{th}^{eff} = V_{th,long} + \Delta V_{th,DIBL} = 0.45 - 0.0064 = \mathbf{0.4436 \text{ V}}$$

(iii) **Subthreshold leakage at $V_{GS} = 0$:**
$$I_{off} = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{th}/S} = 100 \times 10^{-0.4436/0.090} \text{ µA}$$
$$= 100 \times 10^{-4.93} = 100 \times 1.17 \times 10^{-5} = \mathbf{1.17 \text{ µA}}$$

(iv) **Effect of shrinking to 20 nm:**
Since DIBL scales as $1/L$, halving $L$ roughly doubles $|\Delta V_{th,DIBL}|$ to ≈ -12.8 mV.
New $V_{th} \approx 0.437$ V:
$$I_{off,new} = 100 \times 10^{-0.437/0.090} = 100 \times 10^{-4.86} \approx 1.38 \text{ µA}$$
This represents an **18% increase in leakage** for a 33% reduction in channel length — illustrating the exponential leakage penalty of aggressive scaling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors on DIBL:** DIBL always *lowers* $V_{th}$, so $\Delta V_{th,DIBL}$ is **negative**. Many students write it positive and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong units in $\gamma$:** $\gamma$ is in V$^{1/2}$, not V. The square-root operation in the body-effect equation is frequently missed.
> 3. **Confusion between $V_{FB}$ components:** Splitting $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ correctly is mandatory for full marks on Part B.
> 4. **Forgetting RSCE:** RSCE is the "hump" — describing it as just roll-off loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Every derivation MUST begin with stated assumptions (1-D analysis, gradual channel, etc.).

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **$V_{th}$ definition:** Gate voltage to invert the surface, $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$, surface carrier concentration equals bulk doping.
- [x] **Three components of $V_{th}$:** $V_{FB}$, $2\phi_F$, depletion charge term.
- [x] **Body effect equation:** $V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- [x] **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox}$ — has units V$^{1/2}$.
- [x] **DIBL:** Negative shift of $V_{th}$ with increasing $V_{DS}$; magnitude grows as $1/L$.
- [x] **Roll-off:** $V_{th}$ decreases as $L$ shrinks below 0.25 µm.
- [x] **RSCE:** "Hump" in $V_{th}$–$L$ curve due to halo/retrograde doping.
- [x] **Tradeoff:** Lowering $V_{th}$ increases speed but exponentially increases $I_{off}$ — the central design dilemma of nanoscale CMOS.
- [x] **Modern solution:** FinFET/GAAFET devices suppress SCE via better electrostatic control (lower $\sigma_{DIBL}$, lower body effect).
- [x] **Multi-$V_{th}$ design:** Critical and standard-cell libraries offer HVT, SVT, LVT, and sometimes ULVT variants to balance power and performance.
- [x] **Scaling theories:** Constant field (Dennard) → $V_{th}$ scales as $1/\kappa$; constant voltage → $V_{th}$ constant but electric field grows, causing reliability issues.
- [x] **Standard constants:** $V_T = 25.85$ mV (300 K), $\varepsilon_{si}/\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³.
- [x] **Industry values:** Modern 28 nm/14 nm/3 nm CMOS uses $V_{th}$ ranging from **0.25 V (LVT)** to **0.55 V (HVT)** depending on application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled sketch** of the $V_{th}$ vs $L$ curve showing long-channel plateau, roll-off, RSCE hump, and DIBL collapse. Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for a clean, annotated diagram even if the math has minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Electron Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hot Electron Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Hot Electron Effects

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of nanoscale Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs), **Hot Electron Effects** (also termed **Hot Carrier Effects, HCE**) refer to the non-equilibrium transport phenomena that occur when charge carriers (electrons or holes) acquire kinetic energies substantially greater than the thermal equilibrium lattice energy (kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K). 

In a short-channel MOSFET, the lateral electric field near the drain end of the channel becomes extremely high (on the order of **10⁵ V/cm to 10⁶ V/cm**). Electrons accelerated by this intense field gain kinetic energy far exceeding the thermal value of approximately **0.0259 eV at T = 300 K**. Such carriers, termed "hot electrons," can surmount the potential barriers (e.g., the **Si–SiO₂ barrier height of ≈ 3.1 eV** for electrons) and are injected into the gate oxide, the substrate, or the source/drain regions, causing severe device degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hot Electron Effects are a direct consequence of the failure of the **gradual channel approximation** and the **constant mobility model** in deep submicron devices, and they represent a critical reliability concern in CMOS scaling below the 100 nm node.

## 1.2 The Three Principal Hot Electron Injection Mechanisms

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically classifies hot electron injection into three distinct physical pathways, based on the origin of the high-energy carriers:

1. **Channel Hot Electron (CHE) Injection** – Electrons heated by the high lateral channel field near the drain overcome the Si–SiO₂ barrier and are injected into the gate oxide.
2. **Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) Injection** – Electrons generated in the bulk substrate (by a substrate bias or photoexcitation) gain energy from the vertical substrate field and are injected into the oxide.
3. **Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) Injection** – Carriers generated by **impact ionization** near the drain are injected into the oxide, leading to a positive feedback degradation loop.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolling Boulder on a Mountainside"

Imagine an electron in the channel of a MOSFET as a **boulder resting on a mountainside**. The mountain represents the potential energy landscape of the silicon lattice. 

- **In a long-channel device (long mountain)**: The boulder rolls gently downhill, losing energy to friction (phonon scattering), and never gains enough speed to fly off the edge. The electron stays "thermally" cool.
- **In a short-channel device (cliff edge)**: The mountain ends abruptly in a steep cliff (the drain depletion region). The boulder, having rolled only a short distance, suddenly finds itself on a near-vertical drop. It accelerates violently over a very short distance, gaining enormous kinetic energy. When it hits the bottom (the drain end), it bounces with such high energy that it can fly *upward* over the Si–SiO₂ "ridge" and land in the gate oxide "valley" on the other side.

> [!NOTE]
> The **mean free path** of an electron in silicon (≈ 7–10 nm at 300 K) sets the fundamental physical length scale. When the channel length approaches or falls below the carrier's mean free path, ballistic/quasi-ballistic transport and hot carrier effects dominate, making this a quintessential **nanoelectronics** phenomenon.

## 1.4 Why Hot Electron Effects are Critical in Nanoelectronics

> [!WARNING]
> **Device Reliability Concern:** Hot carrier injection causes:
> - **Threshold voltage (V_T) shift** due to trapped charges in the oxide.
> - **Transconductance (g_m) degradation**.
> - **Increased subthreshold leakage**.
> - **Long-term device failure** under normal operating conditions.
> - **Reduced mean-time-to-failure (MTTF)** of integrated circuits.

These effects fundamentally limit the operational voltage of scaled CMOS technology and have driven the industry's transition from **V_DD = 5 V → 3.3 V → 1.8 V → 1.2 V → sub-1 V** regimes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Hot Carriers — Failure of Classical Assumptions

The classical long-channel MOSFET analysis rests on two foundational pillars that **break down** in short-channel devices:

| Assumption | Long-Channel Validity | Short-Channel Failure |
|------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA) | Lateral field E_y << Vertical field E_x | E_y becomes comparable to E_x near drain |
| Constant Mobility (μ = μ₀) | E_y below critical field E_crit ≈ 10⁴ V/cm | E_y >> E_crit, velocity saturates |

The peak lateral field **E_m** at the drain end of a short-channel device can be approximated as:

$$E_m \approx \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$$

where **l** is the effective channel length in the velocity-saturation region (typically a few tens of nanometers).

## 2.2 Energy Distribution and the "Effective Electron Temperature"

When the lateral field is sufficiently high, the electron ensemble can no longer be described by the equilibrium Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution at lattice temperature T. Instead, an **effective electron temperature T_e** is introduced:

$$T_e = T_L \left[ 1 + \frac{3}{5}\left(\frac{\mu_0 E_m}{v_{sat}}\right)^2 \right]$$

> Here, **T_L** is the lattice temperature, **μ₀** is the low-field mobility, and **v_sat ≈ 1.07 × 10⁷ cm/s** is the saturation velocity for electrons in silicon.

The hot electron energy distribution is well-approximated by a **Maxwellian tail** with the form:

$$f(E) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E}{k_B T_e}\right)$$

This high-energy tail is the origin of all hot carrier injection phenomena.

## 2.3 The Lucky Electron Model (Shields, 1959; Hu, 1979)

The **Lucky Electron Model** is the cornerstone analytical tool for predicting hot carrier injection in MOSFETs. It assumes that an electron is "lucky" if it:

1. Travels a distance **d** in the high-field region without suffering an energy-losing collision.
2. Upon gaining energy **E ≥ qφ_b** (where φ_b is the barrier height), redirects its momentum toward the Si–SiO₂ interface.
3. Travels to the interface without further scattering.
4. Crosses the interface without scattering.

### 2.3.1 Probability of Avoiding a Collision

The probability of an electron traveling a distance d without a scattering event is given by:

$$P(d) = e^{-d/\lambda}$$

where **λ** is the mean free path of the electron (typically **λ ≈ 7.6 nm** for Si at 300 K, related to optical phonon emission).

### 2.3.2 Probability of Reaching Energy E in the Field

Using the lucky electron concept, the probability that an electron gains enough energy to overcome the barrier is:

$$P(E) = \frac{q\lambda E_m}{q\phi_b} \exp\left(-\frac{q\phi_b}{q\lambda E_m}\right) = \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE. Each formula is a guaranteed high-yield item.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units & Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Effective electron temperature | $T_e = T_L [1 + 0.6 (\mu_0 E_m / v_{sat})^2]$ | K ; E_m is peak field |
| 2 | Hot electron injection probability (lucky electron) | $P_{inj} = (\lambda E_m / \phi_b) \exp(-\phi_b / \lambda E_m)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Substrate current (impact ionization) | $I_{sub} = C_1 I_D V_{DS} \exp(-B_1 / E_m)$ | Amperes |
| 4 | Gate current (hot electron injection) | $I_G = C_2 I_D \exp(-\phi_b / \lambda E_m)$ | Amperes |
| 5 | Impact ionization coefficient | $\alpha(E) = A \exp(-B/E)$ | cm⁻¹ ; A ≈ 7×10⁵ cm⁻¹, B ≈ 1.23×10⁶ V/cm for e⁻ |
| 6 | Multiplication factor | $M = 1 / (1 - \int_0^{l} \alpha \, dx)$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | DIBL threshold shift | $\Delta V_T = -(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}) \cdot \eta$ | Volts ; η is DIBL coefficient |
| 8 | Velocity saturation field | $E_{sat} = 2 v_{sat} / \mu_0$ | V/cm ; ≈ 1.5×10⁴ V/cm for e⁻ |
| 9 | Threshold voltage roll-off (charge sharing) | $\Delta V_T \approx \frac{q N_A x_d}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{L - L'}{L}$ | Volts |
| 10 | Mean free path (optical phonon) | $\lambda = \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{2 \hbar \omega_{op}}{m^* E_m^2} \cdot v_{sat}$ | nm ; ω_op ≈ 6.2×10¹³ rad/s |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Relevance

The hot electron framework is not merely academic; it is the **foundation of the unified reliability model** used by every major semiconductor manufacturer (Intel, TSMC, Samsung, GlobalFoundries) for:

- **Lifetime prediction** of CMOS ICs (the 10-year operating life specification).
- **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** definition for analog/RF MOSFETs.
- **Design of LDD (Lightly Doped Drain)** and **HDD (Highly Doped Drain)** structures.
- **Development of silicon-on-insulator (SOI)** and **FinFET** architectures that suppress hot carrier effects.
- **Hot electron injection programming** in **Flash EEPROM** non-volatile memory — a beneficial application!

> [!NOTE]
> **Dual-use phenomenon:** While HCE is a reliability hazard for digital CMOS, the *same* physical principle of channel hot electron injection (CHEI) is exploited in the **write operation** of **NOR-type Flash memory cells** at SourceElectronics (SE) and **Drain Avalanche Hot Hole (DAHH)** injection in **EPROM** devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Lucky Electron Injection Probability

We will derive the gate current component of hot electron injection using first principles. This derivation is **directly tested** in KTU ESE Module 1.

### Step 1: Define the Mean Free Path

The mean free path λ is the average distance an electron travels between successive optical-phonon scattering events. In silicon at 300 K:

$$\lambda = \frac{m^* v_{sat}^2}{q E_m} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{scattering rate correction}}$$

For a more practical form, the universal relation often used is:

$$\lambda \approx 7.6 \text{ nm} \quad \text{(in Si at 300 K, field-dependent correction neglected)}$$

### Step 2: Probability of Ballistic Transport over Distance d

In a high-field region, the probability that a "lucky electron" avoids any energy-losing collision over a distance d is purely exponential (Poisson statistics):

$$P_{\text{no collision}}(d) = e^{-d/\lambda}$$

> **Logic:** A Poisson process yields an exponential distribution for inter-event distances. The probability of *zero* events in a path of length d is exp(-d/λ).

### Step 3: Energy Gained in the Field

Assuming the electron moves ballistically over a distance d in a uniform field E_m, the energy gained from the field is:

$$\Delta E = q E_m \cdot d$$

### Step 4: Probability of Achieving Barrier-Overcoming Energy

For the electron to surmount the Si–SiO₂ barrier φ_b (≈ 3.1 eV for electrons), the field-aided distance needed is:

$$d_{min} = \frac{\phi_b}{E_m}$$

The probability that an electron *gains enough energy* is the probability it travels at least d_min without losing energy:

$$P_{\text{energy}} = e^{-d_{min}/\lambda} = e^{-\phi_b / (\lambda E_m)}$$

### Step 5: Inclusion of Geometric Redirection Probability

The electron's momentum, after being heated, must redirect towards the Si–SiO₂ interface. The probability of being directed within a small solid angle dΩ of the normal is dΩ/(4π). After integrating over the accessible hemisphere, a factor of 1/(λE_m/φ_b) appears, leading to:

$$P_{\text{redirect}} \propto \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b}$$

### Step 6: Final Lucky Electron Injection Probability

Combining all the "luck" components, the total injection probability is:

$$\boxed{P_{inj} = \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m}\right)}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The exponential term dominates and represents the fundamental energy threshold. The pre-factor accounts for the geometric rarity of the right momentum direction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substrate Current from Impact Ionization

When a hot electron has kinetic energy exceeding the bandgap energy **E_g = 1.12 eV** (in Si), it can create an electron-hole pair upon collision with the lattice. The **impact ionization rate** α(E) follows the **Chynoweth expression**:

$$\alpha(E) = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B}{E}\right)$$

For electrons in Si: **A = 7.03 × 10⁵ cm⁻¹**, **B = 1.231 × 10⁶ V/cm**. For holes: A and B differ.

### Step 2a: Multiplication Factor

The multiplication factor M is defined as the ratio of total current to the initial current. For a uniform field approximation over depletion width l:

$$M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_0^l \alpha(E) \, dx}$$

### Step 2b: Substrate Current Expression

The substrate current due to impact-ionization-generated holes (which are swept into the substrate) is:

$$I_{sub} = (M - 1) \cdot I_D$$

For a non-uniform field, this is integrated. The KTU standard expression (often cited as the **Hu model**, 1982) is:

$$\boxed{I_{sub} = C_1 \cdot I_D \cdot V_{DS} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_1}{E_m}\right)}$$

> Here, C₁ and B₁ are empirical technology-dependent constants, and E_m is the maximum lateral field at the drain pinch-off point.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 90 nm n-MOSFET operates at V_DD = 1.2 V. The mean free path λ = 7.6 nm, Si–SiO₂ barrier φ_b = 3.1 eV, and the maximum lateral field E_m = 5 × 10⁵ V/cm. Calculate (a) the lucky electron injection probability, and (b) the ratio I_sub/I_D if C₁V_DS = 10⁻⁴ A and B₁ = 1.5 × 10⁶ V/cm.

### Solution (a):

First, compute the dimensionless quantity φ_b/(λE_m):

$$\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m} = \frac{3.1 \text{ eV}}{(7.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm})(5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm})}$$

$$= \frac{3.1}{3.8 \times 10^{-1}} = \frac{3.1}{0.38} \approx 8.16$$

Now the pre-factor:

$$\frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} = \frac{1}{8.16} \approx 0.1225$$

The exponential term:

$$e^{-8.16} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-4}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{inj} = 0.1225 \times 2.86 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Conclusion:** Only ~35 electrons per million are "lucky" enough to be injected into the oxide. This low probability is why hot carrier degradation is a *slow, cumulative* process (years in operation).

### Solution (b):

$$\frac{I_{sub}}{I_D} = C_1 V_{DS} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_1}{E_m}\right)$$

$$= 10^{-4} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^5}\right) = 10^{-4} \cdot e^{-3}$$

$$= 10^{-4} \times 0.0498 \approx 4.98 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The substrate current is ~5 ppm of the drain current, but it is a sensitive *monitor* of hot carrier activity. Engineers use the ratio I_sub/I_D as a "thermometer" for the peak channel field.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hot Electron Injection Modes

| Feature | Channel Hot Electron (CHE) | Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) | Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) |
|---------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Source of hot carriers** | Heated in channel near drain | Substrate bulk, by bias or photo | Generated by impact ionization |
| **Driving field** | Lateral E_y | Vertical E_x (substrate bias) | Lateral E_y (avalanche region) |
| **Worst-case bias** | V_G ≈ V_D (max I_sub) | V_G = 0, V_sub bias large | V_D = V_G = V_DD (worst) |
| **Degradation signature** | Interface traps + oxide traps | Oxide trapped charge | Interface state creation |
| **Primary indicator** | Gate current I_G | Gate current I_G (substrate) | Substrate current I_sub |
| **V_T shift direction** | ΔV_T > 0 (for nMOS, trapped e⁻) | ΔV_T > 0 | ΔV_T can be + or − |
| **Modern relevance** | Reduced in FinFETs (gated channel) | Major concern for SOI/back-bias | Dominant in LDD devices |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hot Electron Injection Pathway in a Short-Channel MOSFET

The following block diagram illustrates the physical processes and energy flow governing hot carrier phenomena in a nanoscale n-MOSFET.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Terminal] -->|Electron Injection| B[Channel Region]
    B -->|Drift in Lateral E field| C[Drain Depletion Region]
    C -->|High E field E approx 10 to 5 V per cm| D[Hot Electron Generation]
    D --> E1[Ballistic Energy Gain]
    D --> E2[Optical Phonon Scattering]
    D --> E3[Impact Ionization Event]
    E1 --> F[Energy Exceeds Si-SiO2 Barrier phi b approx 3.1 eV]
    E2 --> G[Energy Loss to Lattice]
```
