```mermaid
    E3 --> H[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    F --> I[Injection Across Si-SiO2 Interface]
    H --> J[Substrate Current I sub]
    H --> K[Secondary Hot Carriers]
    I --> L[Gate Oxide Trap Site]
    L --> M[Threshold Voltage Shift Delta V T]
    L --> N[Transconductance Degradation]
    K --> I
    J --> O[Body Effect Perturbation]
    M --> P[Long Term Device Failure]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Hot Electron Effect Block-Level Architecture Flow

The following architecture flow maps the relationship between device physics, circuit-level impact, and system-level reliability consequences.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Layer] -->|Nano Scale Channel L less than 100 nm| P2[High Lateral Field E m]
    P2 --> P3[Non Equilibrium Hot Carriers]
    P3 --> P4[Three Injection Pathways]
    P4 --> P4a[Channel Hot Electron]
    P4 --> P4b[Substrate Hot Electron]
    P4 --> P4c[Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier]
    P4a --> D1[Gate Current I G]
    P4b --> D1
    P4c --> D2[Substrate Current I sub]
    D1 --> R1[Oxide Trapped Charge N ot]
    D2 --> R2[Interface State Density N it]
    R1 --> C1[Threshold Voltage Shift]
    R1 --> C2[Mobility Degradation]
    R2 --> C1
    R2 --> C3[Subthreshold Swing Increase]
    C1 --> S1[Digital IC Timing Failure]
    C2 --> S1
    C3 --> S2[Static Leakage Increase]
    S1 --> F1[10 Year Reliability Violation]
    S2 --> F1
    F1 --> M1[Mitigation Strategies]
    M1 --> M1a[LDD Drain Engineering]
    M1 --> M1b[Reduced Supply Voltage V DD]
    M1 --> M1c[FinFET Multi Gate Architecture]
    M1 --> M1d[High K Gate Dielectric]
```

## 4.3 Energy Band Diagram — Hot Electron Injection Schematic

The following diagram represents the energy band perspective of a hot electron crossing the Si–SiO₂ barrier (block-architecture form, as per protocol):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EB1[Conduction Band Edge in Silicon] -->|Heated by Lateral Field| EB2[Hot Electron Energy Level E hot]
    EB2 -->|Energy Exceeds 3.1 eV| EB3[Si-SiO2 Conduction Band Offset]
    EB3 -->|Quantum Mechanical Tunneling plus Injection| EB4[Conduction Band of SiO2 Gate Dielectric]
    EB4 --> EB5[Trapping Site in Oxide]
    EB5 --> EB6[Fixed Negative Oxide Charge]
    FB1[Valence Band in Silicon] -->|Impact Ionization| FB2[Generated Hole]
    FB2 --> FB3[Substrate Contact Current]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEVT526, Module 1]`

**Define Hot Electron Effect. What are the three main hot electron injection mechanisms in a MOSFET?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **hot electron** is a charge carrier in a semiconductor whose kinetic energy is significantly greater than the thermal equilibrium value of **kT ≈ 25.85 meV at 300 K**. In short-channel MOSFETs, carriers accelerated by high lateral electric fields near the drain acquire energies of several electron-volts, becoming "hot."

> The three main hot electron injection mechanisms are:
> 1. **Channel Hot Electron (CHE) Injection:** Electrons heated by the lateral channel field overcome the Si–SiO₂ barrier and are injected into the gate oxide.
> 2. **Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) Injection:** Carriers heated in the substrate bulk by a vertical field are injected vertically into the oxide.
> 3. **Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) Injection:** Carriers generated by **impact ionization** near the drain junction are injected into the oxide, often creating interface states.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming 3 mechanisms + 1 mark for brief description of any one]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEVT526, Module 1]`

**Explain the "Lucky Electron Model" of hot electron injection. Why is it called "lucky"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Lucky Electron Model** (proposed by **Hu et al., 1979**) is a statistical model that calculates the probability of an electron acquiring enough kinetic energy in the high-field region near the drain to overcome the Si–SiO₂ energy barrier (≈ 3.1 eV) and be injected into the gate oxide.

> It is called "lucky" because the model assumes the electron is fortunate enough to:
> 1. Travel a sufficient distance **ballistically** (without any phonon scattering) in the high-field region.
> 2. Redirect its momentum towards the Si–SiO₂ interface.
> 3. Reach the interface without further scattering.
> 4. Cross the interface barrier.

> The injection probability follows:
> $$P_{inj} = \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m}\right)$$
> where **λ** is the optical-phonon mean free path, **E_m** is the peak field, and **φ_b** is the barrier height.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark concept + 1 mark for the four "luck" conditions + 1 mark for the formula]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEEVT526]`

**a)** Derive the Lucky Electron Model expression for the hot electron injection probability. Explain the significance of each term in the final equation. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A 65 nm n-MOSFET has the following parameters: mean free path λ = 6.8 nm, Si–SiO₂ barrier φ_b = 3.15 eV, peak lateral field E_m = 4.5 × 10⁵ V/cm, and substrate current empirical constant B₁ = 1.3 × 10⁶ V/cm. Calculate:
   (i) The lucky electron injection probability. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) The substrate current to drain current ratio I_sub/I_D. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

> **Step 1: Setup and Physical Assumptions (1 mark)**
> In the high-field region near the drain pinch-off, an electron experiences a uniform lateral field E_m. The mean free path between optical-phonon scattering events is λ. We seek the probability P that a carrier starting at the pinch-off point travels ballistically for distance d and acquires energy ≥ qφ_b.

> **Step 2: Ballistic Transport Probability (1 mark)**
> The probability of zero scattering events over a path of length d (Poisson distribution) is:
> $$P_{\text{ballistic}}(d) = e^{-d/\lambda}$$

> **Step 3: Energy Threshold Criterion (1 mark)**
> The electron gains energy qE_m·d from the field. To overcome the barrier φ_b:
> $$qE_m \cdot d \geq q\phi_b \implies d \geq d_{min} = \frac{\phi_b}{E_m}$$

> **Step 4: Energy Probability (1 mark)**
> Substituting d_min into the ballistic probability:
> $$P_{\text{energy}} = \exp\left(-\frac{d_{min}}{\lambda}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m}\right)$$

> **Step 5: Geometric Redirection (1 mark)**
> The electron's momentum after gaining energy must be redirected into a small solid angle toward the Si–SiO₂ interface normal. Integrating the angular distribution over the accessible hemisphere (a factor accounting for the rare 3D coincidence) yields the pre-factor:
> $$P_{\text{redirect}} = \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b}$$

> **Step 6: Final Expression and Significance (2 marks)**
> $$\boxed{P_{inj} = \frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m}\right)}$$
>
> **Significance of each term:**
> - **λE_m/φ_b (pre-factor):** Accounts for the geometric and statistical rarity of the electron's momentum being directed toward the oxide interface. Larger fields increase this term.
> - **exp(−φ_b/λE_m):** The dominant exponential decay representing the fundamental energy threshold. If the field is too weak, this term → 0.
> - **λ:** Sets the fundamental length scale. In modern devices, as L → λ, this term saturates and the model becomes less accurate.
> - **φ_b:** The barrier height (3.1 eV for Si–SiO₂, but only ~1.7 eV for high-κ dielectrics like HfO₂, making high-κ stacks *more* susceptible to hot carrier injection — a critical design trade-off).

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**(i) Lucky Electron Injection Probability (3 Marks):**

Compute the exponent first:

$$\frac{\phi_b}{\lambda E_m} = \frac{3.15 \text{ eV}}{(6.8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm})(4.5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm})}$$

$$= \frac{3.15}{3.06 \times 10^{-1}} = \frac{3.15}{0.306} \approx 10.29$$

Compute the pre-factor:

$$\frac{\lambda E_m}{\phi_b} = \frac{1}{10.29} \approx 0.0972$$

Exponential term:

$$e^{-10.29} \approx 3.36 \times 10^{-5}$$

Final injection probability:

$$P_{inj} = 0.0972 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Answer:** $P_{inj} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-6}$ (about 3 hot electron injections per million channel electrons).

**(ii) Substrate Current Ratio (4 Marks):**

Using the Hu model:

$$\frac{I_{sub}}{I_D} = C_1 V_{DS} \exp\left(-\frac{B_1}{E_m}\right)$$

Assuming the technology standard value $C_1 V_{DS} = 10^{-4}$ (typical at V_DD = 1.0 V):

$$\frac{B_1}{E_m} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^6}{4.5 \times 10^5} \approx 2.889$$

$$e^{-2.889} \approx 0.0556$$

$$\frac{I_{sub}}{I_D} = 10^{-4} \times 0.0556 = 5.56 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Answer:** $I_{sub}/I_D \approx 5.56 \times 10^{-6}$ (approximately 5.6 ppm of the drain current flows to the substrate due to impact ionization).

> **Physical interpretation:** Even though the absolute substrate current is tiny, the I_sub/I_D ratio is a *magnified* signature of the peak field. Engineers often plot log(I_sub/I_D) vs. 1/V_DS to extract the empirical B₁ constant, which is a direct measure of the maximum channel field.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEEVT526]`

**a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the three hot electron injection mechanisms in a short-channel MOSFET. Compare CHE, SHE, and DAHC mechanisms in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Discuss the degradation mechanisms caused by hot electron effects. Explain how the **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD)** structure mitigates hot carrier effects in nanoscale MOSFETs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Injection Mechanisms (7 Marks)

> **Energy Band Description (3 marks):**
> In a short-channel n-MOSFET biased in saturation, the conduction band near the drain end slopes steeply downward toward the drain. Electrons in the channel gain energy from this potential drop. The Si–SiO₂ interface presents a vertical barrier of **3.1 eV**.
>
> - **CHE:** A "hot" electron with kinetic energy > 3.1 eV (after being heated by the lateral field) can be injected into the oxide conduction band if its momentum has a component directed toward the interface.
> - **SHE:** When a negative bias is applied to the substrate, a vertical field exists across the Si–SiO₂ interface. Electrons in the p-substrate (perhaps generated by junction leakage or optical absorption) are accelerated toward the interface. Those gaining > 3.1 eV energy from the vertical field are injected into the oxide.
> - **DAHC:** Near the drain, the lateral field is so high that channel electrons undergo impact ionization, generating electron-hole pairs. The generated holes flow to the substrate (forming I_sub), and the secondary electrons, if directed toward the interface, are injected into the oxide. The high-energy "secondary" electrons often have a higher injection probability than the primary CHEs.

> **Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | CHE | SHE | DAHC |
> |-----------|-----|-----|------|
> | Origin of energy | Lateral channel field | Substrate bias vertical field | Impact ionization near drain |
> | Worst-case V_GS | V_GS ≈ 0.5 V_DD | V_GS = 0 | V_GS = V_DS = V_DD |
> | Observable current | I_G (gate current) | I_G (substrate-generated) | I_sub + I_G |
> | Dominant in | Conventional MOSFETs | SOI, EPROM, biased-substrate designs | LDD MOSFETs at high V_DS |
> | Damage type | Interface traps + oxide charge | Mostly oxide trapped charge | Interface state creation |
> | Modern suppression | Multi-gate / FinFET | Buried oxide in SOI | LDD + retrograde well |

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Degradation & LDD Mitigation (7 Marks)

> **Degradation Mechanisms (3 marks):**
> 1. **Interface Trap Creation (N_it):** Hot electrons breaking Si–H bonds at the Si–SiO₂ interface, creating dangling bonds (Pb centers). These are electrically active acceptor-like states, leading to **negative ΔV_T shift** and reduced mobility.
> 2. **Oxide Trapped Charge (N_ot):** Electrons trapped in pre-existing or generated defects in the oxide bulk, causing a **positive ΔV_T shift** in nMOSFETs.
> 3. **Anti-trapped holes:** Generated by impact ionization and trapped near the interface, contributing to ΔV_T drift.
> 4. **Percolation path formation:** In ultra-thin oxides (< 2 nm), hot carrier stress can form conductive percolation paths, leading to **stress-induced leakage current (SILC)** and eventual **dielectric breakdown**.

> **LDD Mitigation Strategy (4 marks):**
> In a conventional MOSFET, the abrupt n⁺ drain creates a high peak field E_m at the drain junction. The **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD)** introduces a low-dose n⁻ region (10¹⁸ – 10¹⁹ cm⁻³) between the channel and the n⁺ drain contact.
>
> - **Field reduction:** The depletion region now extends laterally into the n⁻ LDD region, spreading the field over a longer distance and reducing the peak E_m.
> - **Quantitative effect:** E_m drops from ~7 × 10⁵ V/cm (conventional) to ~3 × 10⁵ V/cm (LDD), reducing the substrate current by **two to three orders of magnitude**.
> - **Trade-off:** LDD adds series resistance (the "LDD resistance" or R_ext), which degrades drive current. Modern solutions use **silicide-spaced LDD** or **raised source/drain (RSD)** to mitigate this.
> - **Evolution:** In sub-45 nm nodes, the LDD concept has evolved into **ultra-shallow junctions (USJ)** with **halo (pocket) implants** and **stress engineering** to simultaneously control E_m and enhance carrier mobility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing barrier heights:** Students often use φ_b = 3.1 eV (Si–SiO₂ electron barrier) without realizing that for *holes* the barrier is ≈ 4.7 eV, and for *high-κ* dielectrics (HfO₂) the conduction band offset is only ~1.5 eV. Always state which interface you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting units of E_m:** The exponential argument φ_b/(λE_m) must be dimensionless. E_m must be in **V/cm**, λ in **cm**, φ_b in **eV**, and q (which cancels). Failure to convert units is the #1 numerical error.
> 3. **Mixing up I_sub and I_G:** I_sub monitors **impact ionization** (vertical holes to substrate). I_G monitors **oxide injection** (vertical electrons to gate). They are NOT the same current.
> 4. **Neglecting the role of V_GS:** Students often write V_GS = V_DD for "worst case" but the **worst-case CHE occurs at V_GS ≈ V_DD/2** (where the lucky electron sees the most favorable vertical field redirection). The worst-case **DAHC** is at V_GS = V_DS = V_DD.
> 5. **Lucky Electron Model Limitations:** Don't apply the model to **ballistic** devices (channel length < λ) — it fails. Don't apply it to **quantum-confined** systems (FinFETs below 10 nm) without modification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Hot Electron Effects:**

- ✅ A **hot electron** is any carrier with kinetic energy ≫ kT (≈ 25.85 meV at 300 K). In MOSFETs, hot electrons are created in the high-field region near the drain.
- ✅ The **Lucky Electron Model** gives: $P_{inj} = (\lambda E_m / \phi_b) \exp(-\phi_b / \lambda E_m)$ — this is THE formula to memorize.
- ✅ The **mean free path** λ in Si at 300 K is ≈ **7.6 nm**; this sets the critical length scale below which hot carrier effects dominate.
- ✅ **Si–SiO₂ electron barrier** φ_b ≈ **3.1 eV**; hole barrier ≈ **4.7 eV**; HfO₂ electron barrier ≈ **1.5 eV** (lower → *worse* HCE).
- ✅ **Three injection mechanisms:** CHE (channel heating), SHE (substrate heating), DAHC (avalanche at drain).
- ✅ **Worst-case bias for CHE:** V_GS ≈ V_DD/2; for DAHC: V_GS = V_DS = V_DD.
- ✅ **Impact ionization** generates electron-hole pairs when carrier energy > **E_g = 1.12 eV** in Si.
- ✅ **Chynoweth equation:** $\alpha(E) = A \exp(-B/E)$ is the standard impact ionization rate.
- ✅ **Substrate current** is the most sensitive *electrical* monitor of the peak channel field: $I_{sub} = C_1 I_D V_{DS} \exp(-B_1/E_m)$.
- ✅ **Gate current** is the *direct* monitor of hot electron injection: $I_G = C_2 I_D \exp(-\phi_b / \lambda E_m)$.
- ✅ **Degradation modes:** (1) interface trap creation (N_it), (2) oxide trapped charge (N_ot), (3) SILC in thin oxides.
- ✅ **Mitigation techniques:** LDD, retrograde wells, halo implants, high-κ dielectrics (with careful band offset engineering), SOI, FinFET/multi-gate architectures, and **lowering V_DD**.
- ✅ **Beneficial use:** The SAME hot electron injection physics is exploited in **Flash memory** (channel hot electron injection, CHEI) and **EPROM** programming.
- ✅ **Reliability rule of thumb:** Lifetime $\propto \exp(B_2 / E_m)$, and the industry rule is to limit I_sub/I_D below **10⁻⁷** for 10-year operation.
- ✅ **Modern relevance:** In FinFETs, the **multi-gate structure** suppresses the lateral field near the drain, reducing hot carrier degradation by **>100×** compared to planar MOSFETs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-Threshold Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sub-Threshold Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET), the **Sub-Threshold Current** (also called **Weak Inversion Current** or **Off-State Leakage Current**) is defined as the drain current $I_D$ that flows between the source and the drain terminals when the applied gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ is **below** the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$. Mathematically, the device is said to be operating in the **sub-threshold regime** when:

$$V_{GS} < V_{TH}$$

In this regime, the conducting channel beneath the gate oxide has not been fully formed (the semiconductor surface is only weakly inverted), yet a non-negligible drift-diffusion current still flows. This current arises fundamentally from the **thermionic emission of charge carriers over the source-to-channel potential barrier**, which is governed by the Boltzmann distribution of carrier energies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Sub-threshold conduction is the dominant OFF-state leakage mechanism in nanoscale CMOS. It directly dictates the **standby power dissipation** of integrated circuits and determines the minimum operating voltage $V_{DD}$ for a transistor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large earthen dam holding back a reservoir of water. The dam wall represents the **gate oxide + gate voltage combination**, which is supposed to prevent water (electrons) from flowing downstream (from source to drain).

- **Above Threshold ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$):** The dam is intentionally lowered via a controlled spillway gate. Water rushes through in a wide, turbulent channel — this is the strong inversion ON current.
- **Below Threshold ($V_{GS} < V_{TH}$):** The spillway gates are fully closed. However, individual water molecules at the top of the reservoir still possess enough **thermal kinetic energy** to evaporate and drift over the dam wall. This slow, steady leakage is the **sub-threshold current**.

Even when the transistor is logically "OFF", it is never truly zero — analogous to how the dam can never achieve a 100% perfect seal against thermally energetic water molecules. As device dimensions shrink to the nanometer regime, this leakage becomes proportionally more significant and ultimately limits low-power operation.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are fundamental to sub-threshold analysis:

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85~mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300~K$)
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = \mathbf{1.380 \times 10^{-23}~J/K}$
- **Elementary Charge:** $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}~C}$
- **Sub-Threshold Swing (SS):** Measured in **mV/decade**, theoretical minimum $S_{min} = \ln(10) \cdot V_T \approx \mathbf{60~mV/dec}$ at 300 K
- **Body Factor (Body Effect Coefficient):** $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{DM}}{C_{OX2}} \geq 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Decade"?** Sub-threshold current changes **exponentially** with $V_{GS}$, not linearly. Therefore, the industry standard quantifies the gate voltage change required to alter the drain current by **one decade (factor of 10)** — this is the **sub-threshold swing $S$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Sub-Threshold Current

In a conventional long-channel MOSFET, the sub-threshold current arises from the following sequential physical mechanisms:

1. **Barrier Formation:** When $V_{GS}$ is applied but is less than $V_{TH}$, a finite potential energy barrier $\Phi_B$ exists between the source and the channel surface. The barrier height is modulated by $V_{GS}$.
2. **Carrier Concentration via Boltzmann Statistics:** The electron concentration $n$ at any point beneath the oxide is governed by:
$$n = n_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{q\Phi}{k_B T}\right)$$
   where $\Phi$ is the local electrostatic potential. Only electrons with energy greater than the barrier peak contribute to transport.
3. **Diffusion-Dominated Transport:** Unlike above-threshold operation where drift current dominates, sub-threshold transport is **purely diffusion-driven** because the channel surface potential gradient is shallow and there is no inversion layer to support significant drift.
4. **Exponential Dependence on Gate Voltage:** A linear change in gate voltage produces an exponential change in surface potential, which in turn produces an exponential change in inversion charge density and drain current.

### 2.2 The Ideal Sub-Threshold Current Equation

For an n-MOSFET operating in the sub-threshold regime, the classical (long-channel) drain current equation is:

$$I_D = \mu_n \, C_{OX} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (n - 1) \, V_T^2 \, \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \, \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

When $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ (typical saturation in digital circuits, e.g., $V_{DS} = V_{DD} \gg 3V_T$), the bracketed term reduces to unity:

$$I_D \approx I_0 \, \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$$

where $I_0$ is a technology-dependent prefactor:

$$I_0 = \mu_n \, C_{OX} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (n - 1) \, V_T^2$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-essential** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q}$ | Volts; $25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 2 | Sub-Threshold Current (general) | $I_D = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)\cdot\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | Amperes |
| 3 | Sub-Threshold Current (saturated, $V_{DS} \gg V_T$) | $I_D \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$ | Amperes |
| 4 | Prefactor $I_0$ | $I_0 = \mu_n C_{OX}\dfrac{W}{L}(n-1)V_T^2$ | Amperes |
| 5 | Sub-Threshold Swing $S$ | $S = \dfrac{d V_{GS}}{d (\log_{10} I_D)} = n V_T \ln(10)$ | V/decade |
| 6 | Theoretical Minimum Swing | $S_{min} = V_T \ln(10)$ (when $n=1$) | $\approx 60$ mV/dec at 300 K |
| 7 | Body Factor | $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{DM}}{C_{OX2}} = 1 + \dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}{4 \Phi_F}}}{C_{OX}}$ | Dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |
| 8 | Off-State Current | $I_{OFF} = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{DD}/S}$ | Amperes |
| 9 | DIBL Coefficient | $\Delta V_{TH}^{DIBL} = -\sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | Volts |
| 10 | Total $V_{TH}$ Shift (DIBL included) | $V_{TH}^{eff} = V_{TH0} - \sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | Volts |

### 2.4 Why Sub-Threshold Current Matters in Modern Electronics

- **Standby Power:** In billions of transistors on a chip, the cumulative OFF-state leakage (mostly sub-threshold) can dissipate significant static power: $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{OFF} \cdot N_{transistors}$.
- **Minimum Operating Voltage:** The lowest possible $V_{DD}$ for a digital circuit is bounded by the requirement that $V_{DD} \geq S \cdot \log_{10}(I_{ON}/I_{OFF})$. Lowering $S$ allows lower $V_{DD}$.
- **Memory Refresh:** DRAM and SRAM cells rely on extremely low leakage to retain stored charge.
- **IoT & Wearable Devices:** Energy harvesting and battery-powered applications require sub-threshold or near-threshold computing.
- **Nanoscale Reliability:** As $L_g \to 10$ nm, short-channel effects (DIBL, GIDL, BTBT) dramatically amplify sub-threshold leakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** The **OFF current must be low** to save power, but the **ON current must be high** for performance. The sub-threshold swing $S$ governs this trade-off — a smaller $S$ gives a sharper transition between OFF and ON states, enabling both lower leakage and faster switching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sub-Threshold Drain Current from Surface Potential

We begin by considering the electron concentration $n(y)$ along the channel at a distance $y$ from the source. In weak inversion, the local charge density at the semiconductor-oxide interface is given by the Boltzmann relation:

$$n(y) = n_i \cdot \exp\left(\frac{q \psi_s(y)}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $\psi_s(y)$ is the local surface potential. The charge per unit area on the inversion layer is:

$$Q_n(y) = -q \, n_s(y) \cdot t_{inv}(y)$$

For the diffusive sub-threshold current, we use the gradual channel approximation. The drift component is negligible in weak inversion, so the drain current is dominated by diffusion:

$$I_D = -q \, D_n \, W \, \frac{d n_s(y)}{dy}$$

We now express $n_s(y)$ in terms of $\psi_s(y)$ and apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{d n_s}{dy} = \frac{d n_s}{d \psi_s} \cdot \frac{d \psi_s}{dy}$$

Using the Boltzmann form:

$$\frac{d n_s}{d \psi_s} = \frac{q \, n_s}{k_B T} = \frac{n_s}{V_T}$$

Substituting back:

$$I_D = -q \, D_n \, W \, \frac{n_s(y)}{V_T} \cdot \frac{d \psi_s(y)}{dy}$$

Integrating along the channel from $y=0$ (source) to $y=L$ (drain):

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = -q \, D_n \, W \, \frac{1}{V_T} \int_{\psi_{s0}}^{\psi_{sL}} n_s(\psi_s) \, d\psi_s$$

Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel:

$$I_D \, L = -q \, D_n \, W \, \frac{1}{V_T} \int_{\psi_{s0}}^{\psi_{sL}} n_s(\psi_s) \, d\psi_s$$

By Einstein's relation $D_n = \mu_n V_T$, and after integrating (the full integration involves Fermi-Dirac corrections which simplify in weak inversion to give the exponential form), we obtain the **canonical sub-threshold current expression**:

$$I_D = \mu_n \, C_{OX} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (n-1) \, V_T^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

The derivation explicitly shows that sub-threshold current arises from **diffusion of carriers down the surface potential gradient**, not drift.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sub-Threshold Swing $S$

We start from the saturated sub-threshold current ($V_{DS} \gg V_T$):

$$I_D = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(I_D) = \ln(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}$$

Rearranging for $V_{GS}$:

$$V_{GS} = V_{TH} + n V_T \, \ln\!\left(\frac{I_D}{I_0}\right)$$

Now, the **sub-threshold swing** is defined as the change in $V_{GS}$ required to change $I_D$ by one decade (a factor of 10). We convert from natural log to base-10:

$$S = \frac{d V_{GS}}{d \log_{10}(I_D)} = \frac{d V_{GS}}{d (\ln I_D / \ln 10)}$$

Substituting the expression for $V_{GS}$:

$$S = \frac{n V_T \, \ln(10)}{1} = n V_T \ln(10)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{S = n \, V_T \, \ln(10)}$$

In an **ideal device** where the body factor $n = 1$ (i.e., $C_{DM} = 0$, which is physically impossible in a conventional MOSFET but achievable in certain advanced devices), we get:

$$S_{min} = V_T \ln(10) = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln(10)$$

Evaluating at $T = 300$ K:

$$S_{min} = \frac{1.380 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times 2.3026$$

Step 1 — Compute numerator:

$$k_B T = 1.380 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.140 \times 10^{-21}~J$$

Step 2 — Divide by $q$:

$$V_T = \frac{4.140 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 25.85 \times 10^{-3}~V = 25.85~mV$$

Step 3 — Multiply by $\ln(10) \approx 2.3026$:

$$S_{min} = 25.85 \times 2.3026 \approx 59.53~mV/dec \approx 60~mV/dec$$

This is the famous **Boltzmann tyranny limit** of $60$ mV/decade at room temperature.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Off-State Current and $V_{DD}$ Scaling

When $V_{GS} = 0$ (logic OFF state), the off-current is:

$$I_{OFF} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{0 - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$$

For digital switching, the ON-state current (approximated at $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$) is:

$$I_{ON} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{DD} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$$

The ON/OFF current ratio is therefore:

$$\frac{I_{ON}}{I_{OFF}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{n V_T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{S / \ln 10}\right)$$

Taking $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{ON}}{I_{OFF}}\right) = \frac{V_{DD}}{S}$$

Solving for the minimum supply voltage required to maintain an ON/OFF ratio of $10^N$:

$$\boxed{V_{DD}^{min} = N \cdot S}$$

For example, for an ON/OFF ratio of $10^5$ with $S = 100$ mV/dec:

$$V_{DD}^{min} = 5 \times 100 = 500~mV$$

This is why reducing the sub-threshold swing $S$ is critical for ultra-low-voltage operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sub-Threshold Leakage Pathway

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional interaction of physical mechanisms** that collectively determine the sub-threshold current in a nanoscale MOSFET. It maps the cause-and-effect chain from terminal voltages to terminal currents.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Terminal Voltages] --> B{VGS relative to VTH}
    B -- VGS less than VTH --> C[Sub-Threshold Regime]
    B -- VGS greater than VTH --> D[Strong Inversion Regime]
    C --> E[Surface Potential Modulation]
    E --> F[Carrier Concentration via Boltzmann Statistics]
    F --> G[Diffusion Dominated Transport]
    G --> H[Sub-Threshold Drain Current ID]
    H --> I[Off-State Power Dissipation]
    H --> J[Standby Leakage in Memory Cells]
    K[Short Channel Effects DIBL] --> E
    L[Body Effect via Substrate Bias] --> E
    M[Interface Trap Charges] --> E
    N[Temperature T] --> F
    N --> G
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow: Gate Voltage to Drain Current Mapping

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** mapping the gate voltage input to the drain current output, highlighting the exponential nature of sub-threshold conduction and the role of the sub-threshold swing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input VGS] --> B[Surface Potential PS]
    B --> C[Inversion Charge Density Qn]
    C --> D[Diffusion Current Density Jn]
    D --> E[Drain Current ID]
    P[Prefactor I0] --> E
    T[Body Factor n] --> C
    V[Thermal Voltage VT] --> C
    V --> D
```

### 4.3 Decision-Flow Diagram: Leakage Mitigation Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[High Sub-Threshold Leakage Detected] --> Q1{Channel Length?}
    Q1 -- Aggressively Scaled less than 50 nm --> P1[Apply DIBL Suppression]
    P1 --> R1[Use Retrograde Well Doping]
    R1 --> S1[Employ Pocket Halo Implants]
    Q1 -- Long Channel --> Q2{Body Factor Issue?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Reduce Depletion Capacitance]
    P2 --> R2[Use Thin Body SOI or FinFET]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Temperature Issue?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Cool the Device]
    Q3 -- No --> P4[Consider Tunnel FET or NCFET]
    P4 --> R4[Achieve Swing less than 60 mV/dec]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Above $60$ mV/dec at room temperature is the Boltzmann limit. Devices like **Tunnel FETs (TFETs)**, **Negative Capacitance FETs (NCFETs)**, and **Nano-electromechanical (NEM) switches** are actively researched to circumvent this fundamental barrier by changing the carrier injection mechanism from thermionic emission to band-to-band tunneling or by engineering an internal voltage amplification.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> Define sub-threshold current in a MOSFET. Why is it significant in nanoscale device design?

**Model Answer:**
Sub-threshold current is the drain current that flows in a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ is below the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$. It arises from thermionic emission of charge carriers over the source-to-channel potential barrier, governed by the Boltzmann distribution. It is significant in nanoscale device design because: (i) it is the dominant component of OFF-state leakage in modern CMOS, directly impacting standby power consumption; (ii) it imposes a lower bound on the sub-threshold swing ($S \geq 60$ mV/dec at 300 K), limiting supply voltage scaling; and (iii) in highly scaled devices, short-channel effects such as DIBL and BTBT dramatically amplify this current.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Sessional II Equivalent]**
> What is sub-threshold swing? State its theoretical minimum value at room temperature.

**Model Answer:**
The sub-threshold swing $S$ is defined as the change in gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ required to change the drain current $I_D$ by one decade (a factor of 10) in the sub-threshold region. Mathematically:
$$S = \frac{d V_{GS}}{d (\log_{10} I_D)} = n V_T \ln(10)$$
Its theoretical minimum value occurs when the body factor $n = 1$, giving $S_{min} = V_T \ln(10) \approx 60$ mV/decade at room temperature ($T = 300$ K). This is known as the Boltzmann limit.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Sample Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for sub-threshold drain current in a MOSFET. Explain the role of the body factor $n$ in the sub-threshold swing equation.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A nanoscale n-MOSFET has the following parameters at 300 K: $\mu_n = 400~\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$, $C_{OX} = 2 \times 10^{-6}~\text{F/cm}^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{TH} = 0.4$ V, $n = 1.5$. Calculate: (i) The sub-threshold swing $S$ in mV/dec. (ii) The off-state current $I_{OFF}$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ V and $V_{DS} = 1$ V. (iii) The drain current when $V_{GS} = 0.2$ V.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 of these notes. The student is expected to write the diffusion-dominated current equation, apply Boltzmann statistics, integrate from source to drain, and arrive at the canonical sub-threshold current formula:

$$I_D = \mu_n \, C_{OX} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (n-1) \, V_T^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

The body factor $n = 1 + C_{DM}/C_{OX2}$ arises because the gate voltage is divided between the oxide capacitance and the depletion-layer capacitance. A larger $n$ means a smaller fraction of $V_{GS}$ is effective in modulating the surface potential, leading to a **larger sub-threshold swing** $S = n V_T \ln(10)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i): Compute $V_T$ at 300 K.**

$$V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{1.380 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.85~mV$$

**Step (ii): Compute sub-threshold swing.**

$$S = n \, V_T \, \ln(10) = 1.5 \times 25.85 \times 2.3026 \approx 89.3~mV/dec$$

**Step (iii: Compute the prefactor $I_0$.**

First convert units: $C_{OX} = 2 \times 10^{-6}~\text{F/cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-2}~\text{F/m}^2$, $\mu_n = 400~\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s} = 0.04~\text{m}^2/\text{V·s}$.

$$I_0 = \mu_n \, C_{OX} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (n-1) \, V_T^2$$

$$I_0 = 0.04 \times 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 10 \times 0.5 \times (0.02585)^2$$

Step-by-step:
- $\mu_n C_{OX} = 0.04 \times 0.02 = 8 \times 10^{-4}~\text{A/V}$
- Multiply by $W/L = 10$: $8 \times 10^{-3}~\text{A/V}$
- Multiply by $(n-1) = 0.5$: $4 \times 10^{-3}~\text{A/V}$
- Multiply by $V_T^2 = (0.02585)^2 = 6.683 \times 10^{-4}~\text{V}^2$:

$$I_0 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.683 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.67 \times 10^{-6}~A = 2.67~\mu A$$

**Step (iv: Compute $I_{OFF}$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ V, $V_{DS} = 1$ V.**

Since $V_{DS} = 1$ V $\gg V_T = 25.85$ mV, the term $[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)] \approx 1$.

$$I_{OFF} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{0 - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-0.4}{1.5 \times 0.02585}\right)$$

Exponent numerator: $V_{TH} = 0.4$ V.
Exponent denominator: $n V_T = 1.5 \times 0.02585 = 0.0388$ V.
Exponent ratio: $-0.4 / 0.0388 = -10.31$.

$$I_{OFF} = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{-10.31} = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.31 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$I_{OFF} \approx 8.84 \times 10^{-11}~A = 88.4~pA$$

**Step (v): Compute $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = 0.2$ V.**

$$\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T} = \frac{0.2 - 0.4}{0.0388} = \frac{-0.2}{0.0388} = -5.155$$

$$I_D = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{-5.155} = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.78 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I_D \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-8}~A = 15.4~nA$$

**Final Tabulated Answers:**
- (i) $S \approx 89.3$ mV/dec
- (ii) $I_{OFF} \approx 88.4$ pA
- (iii) $I_D (V_{GS}=0.2V) \approx 15.4$ nA

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Supplementary Style]

**(a) [7 Marks)** Discuss the short-channel effects that degrade sub-threshold behavior in nanoscale MOSFETs. With suitable expressions, explain Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL).

**(b) [7 Marks)** A 28 nm n-MOSFET has a DIBL coefficient of $\sigma_{DIBL} = 80$ mV/V, threshold voltage $V_{TH0} = 0.45$ V at $V_{DS} = 0.05$ V, and operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V. Calculate: (i) The effective threshold voltage at $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$. (ii) The percentage increase in off-state leakage current due to DIBL, given $n = 1.4$ and $V_T = 25.85$ mV.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Discussion of Short-Channel Effects:**

In nanoscale MOSFETs (channel length $L_g < 100$ nm), the depletion regions of the source and drain junctions extend into the channel, reducing the gate's electrostatic control over the channel potential. The key effects degrading sub-threshold behavior are:

1. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL):** The drain voltage lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier, effectively reducing $V_{TH}$.
2. **Channel Length Modulation:** Effective $L$ shortens with $V_{DS}$, increasing current.
3. **Surface Punch-Through:** At very short $L$, source and drain depletion regions merge.
4. **Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL):** High $V_{DG}$ causes band-to-band tunneling near the drain.
5. **Hot Carrier Injection:** High-field carriers get injected into the gate oxide.

**DIBL Expression:** The threshold voltage shift due to DIBL is:

$$\Delta V_{TH}^{DIBL} = -\sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

$$\boxed{V_{TH}^{eff} = V_{TH0} - \sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i): Effective $V_{TH}$ at $V_{DS} = V_{DD} = 1.0$ V.**

$$V_{TH}^{eff} = V_{TH0} - \sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DD} = 0.45 - (0.08 \times 1.0)$$

$$V_{TH}^{eff} = 0.45 - 0.08 = 0.37~V$$

**Step (ii): Compute $I_{OFF}$ without DIBL (long-channel approximation) at $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$.**

$$I_{OFF}^{ideal} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH0}}{n V_T}\right)$$

Exponent: $\dfrac{-0.45}{1.4 \times 0.02585} = \dfrac{-0.45}{0.0362} = -12.43$

$$I_{OFF}^{ideal} \propto e^{-12.43}$$

**Step (iii: Compute $I_{OFF}$ with DIBL (actual device).**

$$I_{OFF}^{actual} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH}^{eff}}{n V_T}\right) = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-0.37}{0.0362}\right)$$

Exponent: $\dfrac{-0.37}{0.0362} = -10.22$

**Step (iv): Ratio of leakage currents.**

$$\frac{I_{OFF}^{actual}}{I_{OFF}^{ideal}} = \exp(-10.22 - (-12.43)) = \exp(2.21) \approx 9.12$$

So actual off-state leakage is $\approx 9.12\times$ the ideal value.

**Step (v): Percentage increase.**

$$\%\text{Increase} = (9.12 - 1) \times 100\% \approx 812\%$$

**Final Tabulated Answers:**
- (i) $V_{TH}^{eff} = 0.37$ V
- (ii) Leakage increase $\approx 812\%$ (about 9.1 times the ideal off-current)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)]$ factor:** When asked for $I_{OFF}$, students often skip this term entirely. Even when $V_{DS} \gg V_T$, the examiner expects at least a one-line statement that the term is approximately unity.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors in $\mu_n C_{OX}$:** $\mu_n$ is usually given in $\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ while $C_{OX}$ in $\text{F/cm}^2$. Mixing SI and CGS units is the single biggest numerical-error source. **Always convert to consistent units first.**
> 3. **Confusing $V_{TH}$ and $V_{TH}^{eff}$:** With DIBL, the operating threshold is not the long-channel value. Students often use $V_{TH0}$ when they should be using $V_{TH}^{eff}$ in sub-threshold current expressions.
> 4. **Forgetting $\ln(10)$:** The sub-threshold swing formula is $S = n V_T \ln(10)$, **not** $S = n V_T$. Forgetting $\ln(10)$ gives an answer $\approx 57\%$ of the correct value.
> 5. **Temperature assumption:** Always state $T = 300$ K unless otherwise specified. Writing $V_T \approx 26$ mV without justification costs marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Sub-Threshold Current:**

- **Definition:** Sub-threshold current flows when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$; it is the dominant OFF-state leakage in nanoscale MOSFETs.
- **Origin:** Thermionic emission of carriers over the source-channel barrier, governed by Boltzmann statistics; transport is **diffusion-dominated**.
- **Master Equation:** $I_D = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ for general bias; $\approx I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$ when $V_{DS} \gg V_T$.
- **Sub-Threshold Swing:** $S = n V_T \ln(10)$ in mV/decade.
- **Boltzmann Limit:** $S_{min} = 60$ mV/decade at 300 K (the famous barrier to $V_{DD}$ scaling).
- **Body Factor:** $n = 1 + C_{DM}/C_{OX2}$; a measure of how effectively the gate controls the channel.
- **Prefactor $I_0$:** $I_0 = \mu_n C_{OX} (W/L) (n-1) V_T^2$ — technology-dependent scaling factor.
- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 25.85$ mV at $T = 300$ K.
- **DIBL Effect:** $V_{TH}^{eff} = V_{TH0} - \sigma_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$; aggravates sub-threshold leakage in short-channel devices.
- **Scaling Relation:** Minimum $V_{DD}$ for ON/OFF ratio of $10^N$ is $V_{DD}^{min} = N \cdot S$.
- **Circumventing 60 mV/dec:** Tunnel FETs (TFET), Negative Capacitance FETs (NCFET), and NEM switches use **non-thermionic** injection mechanisms.
- **Real-World Impact:** Governs standby power, memory retention, IoT energy budget, and ultimately the minimum feature size of CMOS technology nodes.
- **Always state $T = 300$ K** when computing $V_T$ in KTU numericals unless explicitly given otherwise.
- **Units consistency:** Convert $\mu_n$ and $C_{OX}$ to the same unit system (preferably SI) **before** multiplying.
- **Exponential form is key:** All sub-threshold analysis is in $\log_{10}$ (decades), not linear scale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity Saturation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Velocity Saturation** is a fundamental high-field transport phenomenon in semiconductors wherein the **drift velocity** of charge carriers (electrons or holes) ceases to increase linearly with the applied electric field and asymptotically approaches a maximum, field-independent value known as the **saturation velocity ($v_{sat}$)**. 

In the context of nanoelectronics, velocity saturation arises because, beyond a critical electric field ($E_{crit}$), the additional kinetic energy acquired by carriers from the field is rapidly dissipated through the emission of **optical phonons**, preventing any further net acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Velocity saturation is the primary *non-linear* transport mechanism that limits the *transconductance* and *drain current* in short-channel MOSFETs and nanoscale FETs. It is the *root cause* of the saturation of carrier velocity at high lateral fields in the channel.

### 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Room-Temperature Saturation Velocities ($T = 300\,K$):**
> - **Silicon (Si):** $v_{sat,n} \approx \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s}$ (electrons), $v_{sat,p} \approx \mathbf{0.6 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s}$ (holes)
> - **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs):** $v_{sat,n} \approx \mathbf{1.2 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s}$ (electrons), with a pronounced negative-differential-mobility region
> - **Critical Field for Si (electrons):** $E_{crit} \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{4}\,V/cm}$ (at which $v = v_{sat}$)
> - **Effective mass of electron in Si:** $m_n^{*} \approx \mathbf{0.26\,m_0}$ (longitudinal), $\mathbf{0.19\,m_0}$ (transverse)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a ball bearing through a tube filled with sticky syrup**:

- **Low electric field (gentle push):** The ball moves slowly and steadily. Push harder, and the ball moves proportionally faster. This is the **linear regime** where $v = \mu E$.
- **High electric field (violent push):** The ball moves so fast that it begins colliding with the syrup molecules violently, generating intense **heat and vibrations** (analogous to **optical phonons**). The energy you pour in is no longer converted into forward motion but into lattice vibrations.
- **Result:** No matter how violently you push, the ball's forward speed plateaus at a maximum value — the **saturation velocity**.

In a transistor channel, the **electric field along the channel** plays the role of your "push," and the **optical phonon lattice** plays the role of the syrup. Once $E > E_{crit}$, the carriers hit the "speed limit" — the **velocity saturation ceiling**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Velocity saturation is *not* due to relativistic effects. It is a *scattering-limited* steady state where energy gain = energy loss per unit time, giving a *constant* average velocity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Regimes of Carrier Transport

The relationship between drift velocity ($v_d$) and electric field ($E$) for electrons in a semiconductor is divided into three distinct regimes:

**Regime 1 — Low-Field Linear (Ohmic) Regime**
- For $E \ll E_{crit}$, scattering events are rare and carriers accelerate freely between collisions.
- The drift velocity is governed by the **mobility** $\mu$:

$$v_d = \mu \cdot E$$

- The mobility is determined primarily by **acoustic phonon** and **impurity scattering**.
- This regime is dominant in *long-channel* devices or weakly-doped regions.

**Regime 2 — High-Field Non-Linear (Saturation Onset) Regime**
- As $E$ approaches $E_{crit}$, the carrier energy distribution becomes "hot" — carriers are no longer in thermal equilibrium with the lattice.
- The mobility becomes *field-dependent* and decreases with increasing $E$.
- This is described by the **Caughey-Thomas empirical model** (a KTU favorite):

$$v_d = \frac{\mu_0 \, E}{\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 \, E}{v_{sat}}\right)^{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}}$$

where $\beta$ is an empirical fitting parameter (typically $\beta = 1$ to $2$ for Si).

**Regime 3 — Velocity Saturation Regime**
- For $E \gg E_{crit}$, the denominator dominates, and $v_d \to v_{sat}$.
- The physical mechanism is **steady-state emission of optical phonons** (energy $\hbar\omega_{op} \approx 0.063\,eV$ for Si).
- Carriers gain energy from the field and lose it almost instantly to the lattice, maintaining a near-constant average kinetic energy.

### 2.2 Physical Mechanism: Why Saturation Occurs

1. **Energy Gain from Field:** A carrier of charge $q$ in field $E$ gains power $P_{gain} = q \cdot v_d \cdot E$.
2. **Energy Loss to Phonons:** The dominant energy-loss channel at high fields is **polar optical phonon scattering** (in GaAs/InP) or **non-polar optical phonon scattering** (in Si).
3. **Steady-State Balance:** In the saturated regime:

$$q \cdot v_{sat} \cdot E = \left(\frac{dE}{dt}\right)_{phonons}$$

The right-hand side scales linearly with $E$ (more phonons emitted at higher fields), which forces $v_d$ to saturate at a constant value independent of $E$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield equations you MUST memorize for KTU ESE 2024 examinations on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_d = \mu E$ | Low-field ohmic drift velocity | $cm/s$ |
| 2 | $v_{sat} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8\hbar\omega_{op}}{3\pi m^{*}}}$ | Theoretical saturation velocity (Si, optical phonon limited) | $cm/s$ |
| 3 | $v_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 E}{\left[1+\left(\mu_0 E / v_{sat}\right)^{\beta}\right]^{1/\beta}}$ | Caughey-Thomas empirical model | $cm/s$ |
| 4 | $E_{crit} = \dfrac{v_{sat}}{\mu_0}$ | Critical field marking onset of saturation | $V/cm$ |
| 5 | $v_{d,sat} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\hbar\omega_{op}}{m^{*}}}$ | Simplified saturation velocity (energy-equipartition) | $cm/s$ |
| 6 | $I_{D,sat} = W \, C_{ox} \, (V_{GS} - V_T) \, v_{sat}$ | Velocity-saturated drain current (short-channel MOSFET) | $A$ |
| 7 | $t_{transit} = \dfrac{L}{v_{sat}}$ | Minimum carrier transit time across channel | $s$ |
| 8 | $f_T \approx \dfrac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L}$ | Cutoff frequency of short-channel transistor | $Hz$ |

> **Note on Notation:** In the formulas above, $\vert$ symbols have been written as plain English to avoid markdown breakage. In your KTU answer script, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation freely.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Scaling Limit:** As channel length $L \to 10\,nm$, the lateral field $E = V_{DS}/L$ easily exceeds $10^{5}\,V/cm$, making velocity saturation the *dominant* current-limiting mechanism. This is why KTU's nanoelectronics syllabus emphasizes it.
- **RF/Microwave Design:** Cutoff frequency $f_T$ of a transistor is set by carrier transit time, which bottoms out at $L/v_{sat}$.
- **GaAs vs Si Choice:** GaAs has higher low-field mobility (faster at low $E$) but similar $v_{sat}$ at high $E$, which is why GaAs is used for *low-noise* RF, while Si dominates digital logic.
- **High-Electron-Mobility Transistors (HEMTs):** Velocity saturation defines the maximum $f_T$ in HEMTs used in 5G/6G front-ends.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Saturation Velocity from Energy Balance

We derive the theoretical expression for the saturation velocity using a steady-state energy balance between the power supplied by the electric field and the power dissipated via optical phonon emission.

**Step 1:** Write the power delivered to a carrier by the field.
A single carrier of charge $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ in field $E$ gains power:

$$P_{gain} = q \cdot v_d \cdot E$$

**Step 2:** Write the power lost to optical phonon emission.
Each phonon carries energy $\hbar\omega_{op}$. The phonon emission rate is proportional to the carrier's *excess energy* above the optical phonon threshold. At saturation, we approximate the *average* energy loss rate as:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{\hbar\omega_{op}}{\tau_{op}}$$

where $\tau_{op}$ is the optical phonon scattering time (a material constant, $\tau_{op} \approx 0.1\,ps$ for Si).

**Step 3:** Equate power gain and power loss at steady state.

$$q \cdot v_{sat} \cdot E = \frac{\hbar\omega_{op}}{\tau_{op}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the carrier kinetic energy in terms of $E$ and the mean free path.
The time between phonon collisions is $\tau_{op} = \lambda_{op}/v_{sat}$, where $\lambda_{op}$ is the optical phonon mean free path. Substituting:

$$q \cdot E \cdot \lambda_{op} = \hbar\omega_{op}$$

This shows that the carrier gains *exactly* one optical phonon quantum of energy $\hbar\omega_{op}$ between collisions — a defining feature of velocity saturation.

**Step 5:** Express $v_{sat}$ in terms of material parameters.
Using the kinetic energy relation $\frac{1}{2}m^{*}v_{sat}^{2} \approx \hbar\omega_{op}$ (the carrier's average kinetic energy at saturation is comparable to one optical phonon quantum), we get:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{sat} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\hbar\omega_{op}}{m^{*}}} \\
v_{sat} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times (0.063\,eV) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,J/eV)}{0.26 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\,kg}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate numerically.

$$v_{sat} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.016 \times 10^{-20}}{2.369 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v_{sat} = \sqrt{8.51 \times 10^{10}\,m^{2}/s^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{v_{sat} \approx 2.92 \times 10^{5}\,m/s = 2.92 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s}$$

> **Note:** The exact value depends on the effective mass chosen and corrections for non-parabolic bands. The accepted experimental value for Si electrons is $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$, which differs from this idealized estimate by a factor of $\approx 3$ due to band-structure complexities (many-valley conduction, non-parabolicity).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity-Saturated Drain Current in a Short-Channel MOSFET

For a short-channel MOSFET where the lateral field $E_y = V_{DS}/L$ exceeds $E_{crit}$ throughout the channel, the drift velocity is *uniform* and equal to $v_{sat}$.

**Step 1:** Start with the basic drift-current expression in the channel.

$$I_D = W \cdot Q_n(y) \cdot v_d(y)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $v_d(y) = v_{sat}$ (the velocity-saturated assumption).

$$I_D = W \cdot Q_n(y) \cdot v_{sat}$$

**Step 3:** The inversion charge per unit area is given by the gradual-channel approximation:

$$Q_n(y) = C_{ox} \cdot [V_{GS} - V_T - V(y)]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the drain current $I_D$ is *constant along the channel* (continuity). To find the *integrated* value, integrate $Q_n$ from source to drain:

$$\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dy = W \cdot v_{sat} \int_{0}^{L} C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_T - V(y)] \, dy$$

**Step 5:** Perform the integration. Using $\int_{0}^{L} V(y)\,dy = \dfrac{V_{DS} \cdot L}{2}$ (linear potential drop assumption at saturation):

$$I_D \cdot L = W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot v_{sat} \cdot \left[(V_{GS} - V_T)L - \frac{V_{DS} L}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $I_D$ when $V_{DS}$ is large enough to sustain full velocity saturation:

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot v_{sat} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Long-Channel Saturation Current:**
> Long-channel: $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ — *quadratic* in $(V_{GS}-V_T)$.
> Velocity-saturated: $I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} v_{sat}(V_{GS}-V_T)$ — *linear* in $(V_{GS}-V_T)$.
> **This is the key signature KTU examiners love to test:** short-channel devices lose the square-law behavior and become *linear* due to velocity saturation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Transport Regimes

| Property | Low-Field Regime | Velocity Saturation Regime | Ballistic Regime (Nano) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Field range | $E \ll E_{crit}$ | $E \gg E_{crit}$ | $L \ll L_{mean-free}$ |
| $v_d$ vs $E$ | Linear ($v = \mu E$) | Saturated ($v = v_{sat}$) | $v_d < v_{inj}$ (no scattering) |
| Mobility role | Dominant | Becomes irrelevant | Meaningless |
| Governing scattering | Acoustic phonons, impurities | Optical phonons | None (ballistic) |
| Governing physics | Ohm's law | Energy balance | Injection velocity |
| Typical device | Long-channel MOSFET | Sub-100nm MOSFET | CNTFET, GNR FET, 5nm Si |
| $I_D$ dependence on $V_{GS}$ | Quadratic | Linear | Nearly linear |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Velocity Saturation Mechanism

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *physical mechanism* of velocity saturation — from electric field application to the final saturated drift velocity. This is a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical cross-section, ensuring Mermaid compiles reliably.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Lateral Electric Field E] --> B[Force on Carrier F = qE]
    B --> C[Carrier Acceleration a = qE slash m*]
    C --> D{Carrier Energy vs Optical Phonon Energy}
    D -->|E less than Eth| E[Low Field: Acoustic Phonon Scattering Dominates]
    D -->|E greater than Eth| F[High Field: Optical Phonon Emission Activates]
    E --> G[Linear Drift: v = mu E]
    F --> H[Energy Gain from Field equals Energy Loss to Lattice]
    H --> I[Steady State Achieved]
    I --> J[Drift Velocity Saturates at v_sat]
    G --> K[Output: Linear I-V Characteristic]
    J --> L[Output: Linear I-V in VGS, Loss of Square Law]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: $v_d$ vs $E$ Behavior Across Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Low Field Linear Regime]
        A1[E small] --> A2[v proportional to E]
        A2 --> A3[Mobility mu constant]
    end
    subgraph B[Intermediate Non Linear Regime]
        B1[E near E_crit] --> B2[Carrier Energy Hot]
        B2 --> B3[Mobility Decreases with E]
    end
    subgraph C[Velocity Saturation Regime]
        C1[E very large] --> C2[Optical Phonon Emission Dominates]
        C2 --> C3[Drift Velocity Caps at v_sat]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D[Short Channel MOSFET Behavior]
    D --> E[I_D linear in VGS minus V_T]
    D --> F[Cutoff Frequency f_T set by L over v_sat]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Guide (For Your KTU Answer Script)

When you draw the $v_d$ vs $E$ curve in your exam, mark these critical features clearly:

1. **Origin** $(0, 0)$ — both zero.
2. **Linear region** with slope $\mu_0$ — the initial straight line.
3. **Knee point** at $E = E_{crit}$, $v = v_{sat}$ — the onset of saturation.
4. **Horizontal asymptote** at $v = v_{sat}$ — the saturated region.
5. **GaAs special feature:** Slight *negative slope* beyond $E \approx 3\,kV/cm$ (Gunn effect, valley transfer) before re-saturating at higher fields.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagrams:** Always label the axes ($E$ in $V/cm$ on x-axis, $v_d$ in $cm/s$ on y-axis) and mark both $\mu_0$ (slope) and $v_{sat}$ (asymptote) explicitly. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a well-labeled diagram alone.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions. Each answer should be **3–4 lines maximum**, but with **precise terminology and one key formula**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Define velocity saturation in semiconductors. State the typical saturation velocity for electrons in silicon at 300 K.**

**Model Answer:**
Velocity saturation is the high-field transport phenomenon in semiconductors where the drift velocity of charge carriers ceases to increase with the applied electric field and approaches a maximum constant value, the *saturation velocity* ($v_{sat}$). It arises when the energy gained by carriers from the field is balanced by energy loss through optical phonon emission, producing a steady-state kinetic energy.

For silicon at $T = 300\,K$:

$$v_{sat,n} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the physical mechanism responsible for velocity saturation? Why doesn't $v_d$ keep increasing with $E$?**

**Model Answer:**
Velocity saturation is caused by **optical phonon scattering**. At high electric fields, carriers gain enough kinetic energy to emit optical phonons (energy $\hbar\omega_{op} \approx 0.063\,eV$ in Si) almost continuously. Each emission event randomizes the carrier's momentum, preventing further net acceleration. A steady state is reached when the *power input* from the field equals the *power dissipated* to the lattice:

$$q \cdot v_{sat} \cdot E = \frac{\hbar\omega_{op}}{\tau_{op}}$$

Hence, $v_d$ plateaus at $v_{sat}$ independent of further increases in $E$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the saturation velocity of electrons in silicon using the energy balance between power input from the electric field and power loss via optical phonon emission. State the standard result.

**(b)** With the help of the Caughey-Thomas empirical model, explain how the drift velocity of carriers varies with applied electric field in a semiconductor. Sketch and label the $v_d$ vs $E$ curve, indicating the low-field, intermediate, and saturation regions.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the power input to a single carrier from the field [1 mark]:**
A carrier of charge $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ in field $E$ absorbs power:

$$P_{in} = q \cdot v_d \cdot E$$

**Step 2 — Define the power loss via optical phonon emission [1 mark]:**
At high fields, the dominant energy loss is emission of optical phonons of energy $\hbar\omega_{op}$. If the average time between emissions is $\tau_{op}$, the power loss is:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{\hbar\omega_{op}}{\tau_{op}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state energy balance [2 marks]:**
At velocity saturation, $P_{in} = P_{loss}$:

$$q \cdot v_{sat} \cdot E = \frac{\hbar\omega_{op}}{\tau_{op}}$$

Since $\tau_{op} = \lambda_{op}/v_{sat}$, where $\lambda_{op}$ is the optical phonon mean free path, this becomes:

$$q \cdot E \cdot \lambda_{op} = \hbar\omega_{op}$$

**Step 4 — Connect to kinetic energy to get $v_{sat}$ [2 marks]:**
The carrier's average kinetic energy at saturation is of order $\hbar\omega_{op}$, so:

$$\frac{1}{2} m^{*} v_{sat}^{2} \approx \hbar\omega_{op}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v_{sat} \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\hbar\omega_{op}}{m^{*}}}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical value [1 mark]:**
For Si with $\hbar\omega_{op} = 0.063\,eV$ and $m^{*} = 0.26\,m_0$:

$$v_{sat} \approx 2.9 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$$ (idealized); experimentally observed $v_{sat} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$ for Si electrons at $300\,K$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the Caughey-Thomas model [2 marks]:**

$$v_d = \frac{\mu_0 E}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 E}{v_{sat}}\right)^{\beta}\right]^{1/\beta}}$$

where $\mu_0$ is the low-field mobility, $v_{sat}$ is the saturation velocity, and $\beta$ is an empirical exponent (typically $\beta = 1$ for Si).

**Step 2 — Analyze the three limits [3 marks]:**

- **Low field** ($\mu_0 E \ll v_{sat}$): The bracket $\to 1$, giving $v_d \approx \mu_0 E$ — linear regime.
- **Intermediate** ($\mu_0 E \approx v_{sat}$): Mobility begins to drop; $v_d$ rises sub-linearly.
- **High field** ($\mu_0 E \gg v_{sat}$): The bracket dominates, giving $v_d \to v_{sat}$ — saturated regime.

**Step 3 — Sketch and label the curve [2 marks]:**
The student should draw a curve starting at the origin with slope $\mu_0$, bending at $E = E_{crit} = v_{sat}/\mu_0$, and approaching a horizontal asymptote $v = v_{sat}$. Mark the knee, the asymptote, and the critical field.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** For a short-channel n-MOSFET operating in the velocity-saturation regime, derive the expression for the saturation drain current. Show that it is *linear* in $(V_{GS} - V_T)$ rather than the long-channel quadratic form.

**(b)** A silicon n-MOSFET has $L = 50\,nm$, $W = 1\,\mu m$, $C_{ox} = 20\,fF/\mu m^{2}$, $V_T = 0.3\,V$, $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$, and $V_{GS} = 1.0\,V$. Calculate the velocity-saturated drain current and the cutoff frequency $f_T$. Assume $V_{DS}$ is large enough to maintain full saturation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the assumption [1 mark]:** In a short-channel device, the lateral field $E = V_{DS}/L$ is large enough to drive $v_d = v_{sat}$ across the entire channel.

**Step 2 — Start with the channel current equation [1 mark]:**

$$I_D = W \cdot Q_n \cdot v_{sat}$$

**Step 3 — Express inversion charge [1 mark]:**

$$Q_n = C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_T - V(y))$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from source ($y = 0$) to drain ($y = L$) [2 marks]:**

$$I_D \cdot L = W \cdot v_{sat} \cdot C_{ox} \cdot \left[(V_{GS} - V_T)L - \int_0^L V(y)\,dy\right]$$

Assuming linear potential drop: $\int_0^L V(y)\,dy = V_{DS}L/2$.

**Step 5 — Solve and conclude [2 marks]:**

$$I_{D,sat} = W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot v_{sat} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)$$

This is *linear* in $(V_{GS} - V_T)$, in contrast to the long-channel result which is *quadratic*:

$$I_{D,sat}^{long} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_T)^2$$

Hence, velocity saturation *destroys* the square-law behavior of the MOSFET.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $L = 50\,nm = 50 \times 10^{-7}\,cm$, $W = 1\,\mu m$, $C_{ox} = 20\,fF/\mu m^{2} = 20 \times 10^{-15}\,F/cm \times 10^{4}$ (need unit conversion), $V_T = 0.3\,V$, $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$, $V_{GS} = 1.0\,V$.

**Step 1 — Convert units carefully [1 mark]:**
- $W = 1\,\mu m = 10^{-4}\,cm$
- $C_{ox} = 20\,fF/\mu m^{2} = 20 \times 10^{-15}\,F / (10^{-4}\,cm)^{2} = 20 \times 10^{-15} / 10^{-8}\,F/cm^{2} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\,F/cm^{2}$

**Step 2 — Apply the velocity-saturated current formula [2 marks]:**

$$I_{D,sat} = W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot v_{sat} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values [2 marks]:**

$$I_{D,sat} = (10^{-4}\,cm) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6}\,F/cm^{2}) \times (10^{7}\,cm/s) \times (0.7\,V)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate [1 mark]:**

$$I_{D,sat} = 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{7} \times 0.7 = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}\,A = 1.4\,mA$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $f_T$ [1 mark]:**

$$f_T = \frac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L} = \frac{10^{7}\,cm/s}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^{-7}\,cm} = \frac{10^{7}}{3.14 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{11}\,Hz \approx 318\,GHz$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Velocity Saturation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $v_{sat}$ with $v_{inj}$:** $v_{sat}$ is the *scattering-limited* steady-state drift velocity. $v_{inj}$ (injection velocity) is the *ballistic* velocity at the source. They differ in nanoscale devices — do not interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** $v_{sat}$ is typically in $cm/s$ in KTU problems, while $L$ is often in $nm$. Always convert to a *common* unit (preferably cm or m) before substituting.
> 3. **Writing $v = \mu E$ at high fields:** This is the *single most common error*. If the question mentions a *short-channel* or *nanoscale* device, $v_d$ is *not* $\mu E$ — it is $v_{sat}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to label the diagram:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for an unlabeled $v_d$ vs $E$ curve. Always mark $\mu_0$ (slope), $E_{crit}$ (knee), and $v_{sat}$ (asymptote).
> 5. **Quoting $v_{sat}$ for the wrong material:** Si electrons $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$; Si holes $\approx 0.6 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$; GaAs electrons $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$. Wrong values = 0 marks for the numerical.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Velocity Saturation (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Drift velocity plateaus at $v_{sat}$ when $E \gg E_{crit}$ due to optical phonon scattering.
- **Standard Si values:** $v_{sat,n} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$, $v_{sat,p} \approx 0.6 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$, $E_{crit} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{4}\,V/cm$.
- **Theoretical formula:** $v_{sat} \approx \sqrt{2\hbar\omega_{op}/m^{*}}$.
- **Caughey-Thomas model:** $v_d = \mu_0 E / [1 + (\mu_0 E / v_{sat})^{\beta}]^{1/\beta}$.
- **Critical field:** $E_{crit} = v_{sat}/\mu_0$.
- **Short-channel $I_D$:** $I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS} - V_T)$ — *linear*, not quadratic.
- **Cutoff frequency:** $f_T \approx v_{sat}/(2\pi L)$.
- **Transit time limit:** $t_{transit} = L/v_{sat}$ — sets the *ultimate speed limit* of a FET.
- **Material comparison:** GaAs has higher low-field mobility; Si has higher thermal conductivity and process maturity.
- **Nano relevance:** At $L < 100\,nm$, velocity saturation is the dominant current-limiting mechanism in conventional MOSFETs.
- **Beyond $v_{sat}$:** At even smaller $L$ ($< 10\,nm$), *velocity overshoot* and *ballistic transport* dominate — covered in later modules.
- **Exam mantra:** *Linear in $V_{GS}$, loss of square law, optical phonons, $1 \times 10^{7}\,cm/s$ for Si.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DIBL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** DIBL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** is a short-channel effect in Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs) wherein an increase in the drain-to-source voltage ($V_{DS}$) causes a reduction in the threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$) of the device. The phenomenon arises from the electrostatic coupling between the drain and the channel region in aggressively scaled devices, where the channel length becomes comparable to the depletion-region widths of the source and drain p-n junctions.

In KTU 2024 nanoelectronics parlance, DIBL is formally expressed as:

$$V_{TH}(V_{DS}) = V_{TH0} - \eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

where:
- $V_{TH0}$ is the long-channel (intrinsic) threshold voltage at $V_{DS} \to 0$
- $\eta_{DIBL}$ is the **DIBL coefficient** (typically in **mV/V**)
- $V_{DS}$ is the applied drain-to-source bias

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** DIBL is classified as one of the **short-channel effects (SCEs)** that degrade the ideal square-law behavior of nanoscale MOSFETs. It must be studied alongside the **threshold voltage roll-off**, **channel length modulation**, and **hot-carrier effects**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Dam" Model

Imagine a **hydroelectric dam** (the potential barrier at the source-channel junction) holding back a lake of electrons (the source inversion charge):

- **Normal operation (low $V_{DS}$):** The dam is tall and strong. Only when the gate (water pump) pressurizes enough does current flow. The threshold condition is well-defined.
- **DIBL (high $V_{DS}$):** As the drain-side water level (drain potential) rises, the lake pushes **horizontally against the dam base**, and the dam wall **topples inward**, lowering the effective barrier height. The source electrons now "leak" over the lowered barrier **even without the gate being fully turned on**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world meaning:** This translates into the transistor **turning on earlier than expected**, causing **off-state leakage current** and **static power dissipation** in nanometer-scale CMOS circuits — a primary concern in modern Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) design.

## 1.3 Physical Origin of DIBL — A Qualitative Picture

In a **long-channel MOSFET**, the gate electrode has near-complete electrostatic control over the channel potential. The drain only influences a small fraction of the channel near the drain end (a few Debye lengths).

In a **short-channel MOSFET** (channel length $L \le 0.25\,\mu m$ in classical nodes, and $< 50\,nm$ in modern nodes), the source and drain depletion regions occupy a significant fraction of the channel. The drain electrode's electric field **penetrates** into the source-side barrier region through the **2D electrostatic potential distribution**, reducing the height of the source-to-channel potential barrier.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Constants used in DIBL analysis (at T = 300 K):**
> - Thermal voltage: $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\,mV}$
> - Silicon permittivity: $\varepsilon_{Si} = 11.7 \cdot \varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-10}\,F/m}$
> - Oxide permittivity: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-11}\,F/m}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Structured Logic Flow

The complete physical mechanism of DIBL can be decomposed into the following sequential steps:

1. **Channel Length Scaling**
   - As $L$ decreases toward the deep-submicron regime, the source-drain separation shrinks below the sum of the source and drain depletion widths.

2. **Depletion Region Interaction**
   - The depletion regions of the source-body and drain-body p-n junctions begin to **overlap** within the channel region.

3. **2D Potential Distribution**
   - The standard gradual-channel approximation (GCA) — which assumes 1D electrostatics along the vertical (gate) direction — **breaks down**. A 2D Poisson equation becomes mandatory:
   
   $$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = \frac{q \, N_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}$$
   
   where $x$ is the lateral (source-to-drain) direction, $y$ is the vertical (gate-to-substrate) direction, and $N_A$ is the substrate doping.

4. **Drain Potential Penetration**
   - The boundary condition at the drain end forces the potential to rise. Because the channel is short, this high potential **propagates** toward the source, creating a 2D field component that lowers the potential barrier at the source-channel junction.

5. **Barrier Height Reduction**
   - The source-to-channel potential barrier $\Phi_B$ (measured from source Fermi level to the peak of the channel potential) decreases by an amount $\Delta \Phi_B$ proportional to $V_{DS}$.

6. **Threshold Voltage Shift**
   - To restore the barrier to the threshold level, the gate must compensate by a lower overdrive. Hence $V_{TH}$ shifts downward:
   
   $$\Delta V_{TH} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

7. **Off-State Leakage Enhancement**
   - Lower $V_{TH}$ at high $V_{DS}$ causes exponential increase in subthreshold leakage:
   
   $$I_{OFF} \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}}{V_T \cdot n}\right)$$
   
   where $n$ is the body-effect coefficient ($1 < n < 2$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** | **Applicability** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $V_{TH}(V_{DS}) = V_{TH0} - \eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | Threshold voltage with DIBL | V | All short-channel MOSFETs |
| 2 | $\eta_{DIBL} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{TH}}{\Delta V_{DS}}$ | DIBL coefficient definition | V/V or mV/V | Empirical extraction |
| 3 | $\eta_{DIBL} \approx \dfrac{3 \, t_{ox}}{L} \cdot \dfrac{V_T}{V_{bi}}$ | Approximate DIBL coefficient (Yau model) | dimensionless | Long-channel limit approximation |
| 4 | $\Phi_B(x=0) = \Phi_{B0} - \eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | Source-side barrier lowering | V | 2D analysis |
| 5 | $I_{DS} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | Long-channel square law (no DIBL) | A | $L \gg L_{min}$ |
| 6 | $L_{min} \approx 0.4 \left[ r_j \, t_{ox} (W_S + W_D)^2 \right]^{1/3}$ | Minimum channel length (no SCE) | m | Classical design rule |
| 7 | $W_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{Si}(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{q N_A}}$ | Drain depletion width | m | Abrupt junction approx. |
| 8 | $SS = \ln(10) \cdot \dfrac{kT}{q} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}} \right)$ | Subthreshold swing with DIBL | mV/dec | Subthreshold regime |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{TH,SCE} = \dfrac{q N_A W_D}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{r_j}{L}$ | Reverse short-channel effect (RSCE) | V | Non-uniform doping |
| 10 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[ \dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}(V_{DS})}{n V_T} \right]$ | Subthreshold current with DIBL | A | Leakage estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Caution:** In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ has been rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$. When writing in your exam scripts, **never use unescaped vertical bars inside markdown tables** — they break the column structure.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of DIBL Analysis

DIBL is not merely an academic curiosity — it has direct consequences in chip design:

- **Leakage Power Reduction:** Understanding $\eta_{DIBL}$ allows circuit designers to choose $V_{DD}$ budgets that minimize standby power.
- **Technology Node Selection:** Foundries (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) characterize $\eta_{DIBL}$ for each node to ensure $< 100\,\text{mV/V}$ even at $L \approx 5\,nm$.
- **Device Architecture Innovation:** DIBL motivated the development of:
  - **FinFETs (3D tri-gate)** — improved electrostatic integrity
  - **Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs** — even tighter gate control
  - **High-$\kappa$ metal-gate stacks** — reduced equivalent oxide thickness
- **Reliability Modeling:** DIBL is a primary input to **BSIM-CMG** and **BSIM-IMG** compact models used by SPICE simulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DIBL Coefficient from the 2D Potential Model

We will derive the DIBL coefficient $\eta_{DIBL}$ by solving the **2D Poisson equation** in the channel region under subthreshold conditions.

### Step 1: Set up the 2D Poisson equation

In the subthreshold (weak-inversion) regime, the mobile charge is negligible, so Poisson's equation in the channel region becomes:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi(x,y)}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi(x,y)}{\partial y^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Logic:** The right-hand side represents the ionized acceptor charge density in the p-type silicon body. We retain the 2D form because in short-channel devices, the potential varies significantly in **both** the lateral ($x$) and vertical ($y$) directions.

### Step 2: Apply the boundary conditions

We assume a **parabolic potential profile** in the vertical direction (a standard approximation for the subthreshold region):

$$\psi(x,y) = \psi_S(x) + a(x) \, y + b(x) \, y^2$$

where $\psi_S(x)$ is the surface potential, and $a(x)$, $b(x)$ are position-dependent coefficients.

The boundary conditions are:

- **At the oxide-silicon interface** ($y = 0$): the electric flux is continuous:
  
  $$\left. \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{C_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{Si}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{FB} - \psi_S(x) \right]$$

- **At the bulk neutral region** ($y = t_{Si}$ or $y \to \infty$ for bulk MOSFET): the potential returns to the bulk value, and the field vanishes:
  
  $$\left. \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right|_{y \to \infty} = 0, \quad \psi(x, y \to \infty) = 0$$

- **At the source end** ($x = 0$): $\psi_S(0) = \Phi_{Bi}$ (built-in potential of the source-body junction).
- **At the drain end** ($x = L$): $\psi_S(L) = \Phi_{Bi} + V_{DS}$.

### Step 3: Reduce to a 1D differential equation
