> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Channel Length Modulation, High-K Gate Dielectrics, Effective Oxide Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Channel Length Modulation, High-K Gate Dielectrics, and Effective Oxide Thickness

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Channel Length Modulation (CLM)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** is a short-channel effect in MOSFETs where the effective electrical length of the inversion channel ($L_{eff}$) is reduced as the drain-to-source voltage ($V_{DS}$) increases, due to the expansion of the depletion region at the drain-body p-n junction. This modulation causes the drain current ($I_D$) to increase with $V_{DS}$ in the saturation region, instead of remaining ideally constant.

The phenomenon is quantified by the **Channel Length Modulation parameter**, denoted by the symbol $\lambda$, which has units of **V⁻¹** (per volt).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a rubber water hose with water flowing through it. Now imagine someone gently pinching one end. As the pinch tightens (analogous to increasing $V_{DS}$), the effective open length of the hose shrinks. Even though the pressure at the source stays the same, more water squirts out of the nozzle because the "effective pipe" has become shorter. In a MOSFET, increasing $V_{DS}$ causes the depletion region at the drain end to push into the channel, effectively shortening it and letting more current flow for the same gate overdrive.

**Key physical constant involved:**
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of silicon** at 300 K: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
- **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
- **Relative permittivity of silicon**: $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7$

---

### 1.2 High-K Gate Dielectrics

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-K Dielectrics** are insulating materials with a relative permittivity (dielectric constant, $K$ or $\kappa$) significantly higher than that of silicon dioxide (SiO₂, $K \approx 3.9$). They are introduced in nanoscale MOSFETs to physically thicken the gate insulator (reducing tunneling leakage) while preserving or improving the gate's electrostatic control over the channel.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of two capacitors that need to store the same amount of charge. One uses a thin paper sheet (SiO₂), the other uses a thick slab of rubber (High-K). A thick rubber insulator prevents charge leakage (tunneling), yet the rubber's higher dielectric constant means it stores charge just as well as the thin paper. In MOSFETs, the high-K material acts like that thick rubber — physically thicker, electrically equivalent (or better) than SiO₂.

**Common High-K Materials used in industry:**
- **HfO₂ (Hafnium Dioxide):** $K \approx 25$ (used by Intel from 45 nm node onwards)
- **ZrO₂ (Zirconium Dioxide):** $K \approx 25$
- **TiO₂ (Titanium Dioxide):** $K \approx 80$
- **Al₂O₃ (Aluminum Oxide):** $K \approx 9$
- **La₂O₃ (Lanthanum Oxide):** $K \approx 30$

---

### 1.3 Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT)** is the equivalent thickness of a SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual high-K dielectric being used. EOT allows direct benchmarking of advanced gate stacks against the well-understood SiO₂ reference technology.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If you wanted to replace a 1 nm SiO₂ layer with a high-K material that behaves identically from a capacitance standpoint, how "thick" in SiO₂ terms is the replacement? That thickness is the EOT. A 5 nm HfO₂ layer, despite being physically 5 nm thick, may behave like only a 0.78 nm SiO₂ layer electrically.

**Mathematical form:**

$$\text{EOT} = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}{\varepsilon_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

where $K_{SiO_2} = 3.9$ and $K_{high\text{-}K}$ is the relative permittivity of the high-K material.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Channel Length Modulation

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The MOSFET is a four-terminal device: Gate (G), Source (S), Drain (D), and Body/Substrate (B).
- When a strong inversion layer is formed at the surface, a conducting channel of length $L$ is induced between source and drain.
- The drain and body form a reverse-biased p-n junction. The width of this depletion region is a function of the applied reverse bias $V_{DB}$ (or $V_{DS}$ when source and body are tied).
- **Mathematically, the depletion width on the channel side** is:
$$x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}{q\,N_A}}$$
- The depletion region encroaches into the channel, reducing the **effective channel length** from $L$ to:
$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$$
- This means the channel "shortens" as $V_{DS}$ increases beyond $V_{DS,sat}$.
- The drain current in saturation, originally $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$, must be re-evaluated using $L_{eff}$ instead of $L$.
- Since $L_{eff} < L$, the current $I_D$ becomes slightly larger than the ideal saturation value and grows linearly with $V_{DS}$.

**The "Why" behind the parameter $\lambda$:**
Rather than computing the complicated depletion geometry, the KTU-standard approach models the effect empirically by a single parameter $\lambda$:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

The product $\lambda V_{DS}$ is dimensionless, and $\lambda$ has units of **V⁻¹**. Smaller $L$ (advanced technology nodes) → larger $\lambda$ → stronger CLM.

---

## 2.2 Output Resistance due to CLM

**Why does CLM matter in design?**
The slope $\dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}}$ in saturation defines the **output resistance** $r_o$ of the MOSFET:

$$r_o = \left[\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}}\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$

A larger $r_o$ (smaller $\lambda$) is desirable for analog amplifiers, because the intrinsic gain of a common-source amplifier is $A_v = g_m \cdot r_o$.

---

## 2.3 Physical Origin of High-K Dielectric Need

**The "Why" — Quantum Mechanical Tunneling:**

- The gate oxide capacitance per unit area is $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$.
- To maintain electrostatic control, $C_{ox}$ must be high → $t_{ox}$ must be small.
- For $t_{ox} < 2$ nm, direct tunneling of carriers through SiO₂ becomes significant, causing **gate leakage current** that scales exponentially with decreasing thickness.
- The solution: replace SiO₂ with a physically thicker layer of a high-K material, keeping $C_{ox}$ (and thus electrostatic control) high while suppressing tunneling.
- Since tunneling probability $\propto e^{-\kappa t}$ (roughly), even modest increases in physical thickness drastically reduce leakage.

---

## 2.4 Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT) in Detail

**Key relationship:**
Two capacitors (one SiO₂, one high-K) are equivalent if they have the same capacitance per unit area:

$$C_{ox}^{SiO_2} = C_{high\text{-}K}$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}{EOT} = \frac{\varepsilon_{high\text{-}K}}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

Solving for EOT:

$$EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}{\varepsilon_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

**Alternative direct form:**

$$EOT = \frac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever the question gives "equivalent SiO₂ thickness" or "EOT", immediately substitute $K_{SiO_2} = 3.9$ and apply the ratio.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|------|--------------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Drain current with CLM | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | A | Used in saturation region |
| 2 | Output resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | $\Omega$ | Finite slope of $I_D$-$V_{DS}$ curve |
| 3 | Effective channel length | $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$ | m | $\Delta L$ is the depletion encroachment |
| 4 | Depletion encroachment | $\Delta L \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{DS}-V_{DS,sat})}{qN_A}}$ | m | From one-sided junction approximation |
| 5 | Oxide capacitance per unit area (SiO₂) | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | F/m² | Standard reference |
| 6 | Oxide capacitance per unit area (high-K) | $C_{high\text{-}K} = \dfrac{K_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$ | F/m² | Actual physical layer |
| 7 | Effective Oxide Thickness | $EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \dfrac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}}$ | m | Equivalence to SiO₂ |
| 8 | Inverse relationship | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot K_{SiO_2}}{EOT}$ | F/m² | Always expressed in terms of EOT |
| 9 | Transconductance (ideal, no CLM) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})$ | S | Independent of $\lambda$ |
| 10 | Intrinsic gain | $A_v = g_m r_o = \dfrac{2}{\lambda(V_{GS}-V_{th})}$ | unitless | Decreases as $\lambda$ increases |

**Critical rule for EOT:**

$$\text{If } K_{high\text{-}K} > 3.9 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{EOT} < t_{high\text{-}K} \;\;\text{(high-K is "electrically thinner")}$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Effect** | **Industry Use Case** |
|------------|----------------------|
| CLM (modeled by $\lambda$) | Critical in analog CMOS design: op-amps, voltage references, and current mirrors all depend on matching $\lambda$ values across paired transistors. |
| High-K dielectrics | Intel introduced Hf-based high-K + metal gate at the **45 nm node (2007)** to combat gate leakage. |
| EOT metric | Used by ITRS/IRDS roadmaps to track gate-stack scaling across technology generations (e.g., EOT $\approx$ 0.7 nm at 28 nm, 0.5 nm at 14 nm FinFET). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Modified Drain Current Equation with Channel Length Modulation

We begin with the classical (ideal) saturation current without CLM:

$$I_{D,sat}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the effect of CLM on the channel length.**
When $V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$, the depletion region at the drain extends into the channel by a length $\Delta L$. The new effective length is:

$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the effective length into the current equation.**
The current is inversely proportional to the channel length, so:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L - \Delta L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $\Delta L$ depends on $V_{DS}$.**
For an abrupt one-sided p-n junction, the depletion width on the lightly doped (channel) side is:

$$\Delta L \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}{q\,N_A}}$$

**Step 4 — Linearize the expression using a Taylor expansion.**
We use the fact that $\Delta L \ll L$. For small $x$, $(1-x)^{-1} \approx 1 + x$. Let $x = \Delta L / L$:

$$\frac{1}{L - \Delta L} = \frac{1}{L}\cdot\frac{1}{1 - \frac{\Delta L}{L}} \approx \frac{1}{L}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Combine the expressions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &\approx \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Define the Channel Length Modulation parameter $\lambda$.**
Empirically and for KTU-level analysis, the ratio $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ is proportional to $V_{DS}$:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} \equiv \lambda\,V_{DS}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the current expression.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &\approx \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS})
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **final KTU-board standard expression** for $I_D$ in saturation including CLM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT)

We want the thickness of SiO₂ that gives the **same capacitance per unit area** as a given high-K layer of thickness $t_{high\text{-}K}$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance per unit area of the high-K layer:**

$$C_{high\text{-}K} = \frac{\varepsilon_{high\text{-}K}}{t_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{K_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance per unit area of an equivalent SiO₂ layer of thickness EOT:**

$$C_{SiO_2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{SiO_2}}{EOT} = \frac{K_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT}$$

**Step 3 — Set the two capacitances equal (definition of EOT):**

$$\frac{K_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{K_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\varepsilon_0$ from both sides.**

$$\frac{K_{high\text{-}K}}{t_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{EOT}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for EOT.**

$$\begin{aligned}
EOT &= t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{high\text{-}K}} 
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Insert $K_{SiO_2} = 3.9$ (numerical value, KTU convention).**

$$EOT = \frac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

**Final clean expression:**

$$\boxed{EOT = \frac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 1: EOT Calculation (for board practice)

**Problem:** A 4 nm thick HfO₂ layer ($K = 25$) is used as the gate dielectric. Calculate the EOT and the percentage reduction in physical thickness compared to a SiO₂ layer of the same EOT.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
EOT &= \frac{3.9}{K_{HfO_2}} \cdot t_{HfO_2} \\
    &= \frac{3.9}{25} \cdot 4 \text{ nm} \\
    &= 0.156 \times 4 \text{ nm} \\
    &= 0.624 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** EOT $\approx$ **0.624 nm**, which is the equivalent SiO₂ thickness. The physical HfO₂ layer (4 nm) is more than **6× thicker** than its EOT, drastically reducing tunneling leakage.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 2: Drain Current with CLM

**Problem:** An n-channel MOSFET has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{th} = 0.5$ V, $V_{GS} = 1.5$ V, $\lambda = 0.05$ V⁻¹, and $V_{DS} = 3$ V. Find $I_D$ and $r_o$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS}) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}\,(100\,\mu A/V^2)\,(10)\,(1.5 - 0.5)^2\,(1 + 0.05 \times 3) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}\,(100\,\mu A/V^2)\,(10)\,(1\,V)^2\,(1 + 0.15) \\
    &= 500\,\mu A \times 1.15 \\
    &= 575\,\mu A
\end{aligned}$$

**Output resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_o &= \frac{1}{\lambda\,I_D} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 575\,\mu A} = \frac{1}{28.75\,\mu A} \\
    &\approx 34.78\,k\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $I_D = 575\,\mu A$, $r_o \approx 34.8\,k\Omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Without CLM**, $I_D$ would be exactly $500\,\mu A$. The CLM correction increases it by **15 %**, illustrating why ignoring $\lambda$ causes large errors in modern short-channel devices.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example 3: Multi-layer EOT (Stacked Dielectrics)

**Problem:** A gate stack consists of 1 nm SiO₂ ($K = 3.9$) under 3 nm HfO₂ ($K = 25$). Find the total EOT.

**Solution:**
The two layers are in series (capacitances add inversely). The total oxide thickness in SiO₂ equivalent:

$$\begin{aligned}
EOT_{total} &= t_{SiO_2}\cdot\frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{SiO_2}} + t_{HfO_2}\cdot\frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{HfO_2}} \\
            &= 1\,\text{nm} \cdot 1 + 3\,\text{nm} \cdot \frac{3.9}{25} \\
            &= 1\,\text{nm} + 0.468\,\text{nm} \\
            &= 1.468\,\text{nm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $EOT_{total} = 1.468$ nm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOSFET Cross-Section Showing Channel Length Modulation

The diagram below illustrates how the depletion region at the drain encroaches into the channel as $V_{DS}$ increases.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Gate Contact G] --> B[Gate Oxide Layer]
    B --> C[Channel Region Length L]
    C --> D[Depletion Region Expansion at Drain]
    D --> E[Drain Contact D]
    F[Source Contact S] --> C
    G[Body Substrate B] --> D
    H[Effective Channel Length L effective smaller than L] --> D
```

> **Figure 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture:** As $V_{DS}$ rises, the depletion region at the drain pushes leftward, shortening $L_{eff}$ and causing $I_D$ to increase in saturation.

---

## 4.2 Conventional n-MOSFET Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MOSFET
        S["Source Terminal S"]
        G["Gate Terminal G"]
        D["Drain Terminal D"]
        B["Body Substrate B"]
        GOX["Gate Oxide Dielectric"]
        CH["Inversion Channel Length L"]
    end
    G --> GOX
    GOX --> CH
    S --> CH
    CH --> D
    B -.Controls threshold.-> CH
```

> **Figure 2 — Four-terminal MOSFET with explicit gate-oxide layer.** The gate oxide is the region replaced by high-K dielectrics in modern nodes.

---

## 4.3 High-K Dielectric Stack vs Conventional SiO₂

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Conventional
        A1[Gate Metal] --> A2[SiO2 Layer 1 to 2 nm]
        A2 --> A3[Silicon Channel]
    end
    subgraph Advanced
        B1[Metal Gate] --> B2[Interfacial Layer SiO2 0.5 nm]
        B2 --> B3[HfO2 High-K 2 to 3 nm]
        B3 --> B4[Silicon Channel]
    end
```

> **Figure 3 — Modern High-K Metal Gate (HKMG) Stack.** The thin interfacial SiO₂ remains for interface quality, but the bulk insulator is high-K. Total EOT may equal 0.7–1.0 nm despite a physical thickness of 2–3 nm.

---

## 4.4 Energy Band Picture — Why High-K Reduces Tunneling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SiO2
        C1[Conduction Band Edge of Metal] --> T1[Triangular Barrier SiO2]
        T1 --> V1[Valence Band Edge of Silicon]
    end
    subgraph HighK
        C2[Conduction Band Edge of Metal] --> T2[Thicker Physical Barrier High-K]
        T2 --> V2[Valence Band Edge of Silicon]
    end
```

> **Figure 4 — Tunneling barrier comparison.** The physically thicker high-K layer exponentially suppresses direct tunneling, while the lower conduction-band offset is still acceptable for modern devices.

---

## 4.5 Equivalent Circuit of MOSFET Output in Saturation (with CLM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Gate G"] --> gm[Current Source g_m Vgs]
    gm --> D["Drain D"]
    S["Source S"] --> gm
    ro[Output Resistance r_o] --> D
    ro --> S
```

> **Figure 5 — Small-signal model of a MOSFET in saturation.** The output resistance $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ is finite due to CLM and is in parallel with the drain and source.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define Channel Length Modulation. What is the significance of the parameter $\lambda$ in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ characteristic of a MOSFET operating in saturation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Channel Length Modulation (CLM) is the phenomenon in a MOSFET where the effective length of the inversion channel is reduced as $V_{DS}$ increases, due to the expansion of the depletion region at the drain–body junction. The parameter $\lambda$ (units: V⁻¹) quantifies this effect. It appears in the saturation current equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

**Significance (any 2 of the following for full marks):**
- $\lambda$ gives the **finite slope** of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve in saturation.
- It determines the **output resistance** $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$.
- $\lambda$ increases as the channel length $L$ decreases, making CLM a critical short-channel effect.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** What is meant by Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT)? Why is the concept introduced for high-K gate dielectrics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EOT is the equivalent thickness of a SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual high-K dielectric being used:

$$EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{high\text{-}K}} = \frac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

**Why it is introduced (any 2 of the following for full marks):**
- It provides a **uniform benchmarking metric** to compare advanced gate stacks to the well-known SiO₂ reference technology.
- It allows **continued scaling of $C_{ox}$** (i.e., stronger gate control) **without aggressively thinning** the physical insulator.
- It enables the ITRS/IRDS roadmaps to specify scaling targets (e.g., sub-1 nm EOT) consistently across material changes.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### KTU ESE Module Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Q.A (a)** Derive the expression for the drain current of an n-MOSFET in saturation including Channel Length Modulation. Clearly state the meaning and units of $\lambda$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ideal saturation current (no CLM):** (1 mark)

$$I_{D,sat}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$$

**Step 2 — Effect of CLM on channel length:** (1 mark)

When $V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$, the depletion region at the drain extends by $\Delta L$ into the channel:

$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $L_{eff}$ in the current equation:** (1 mark)

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L - \Delta L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-depletion approximation** $\Delta L \ll L$ using $(1-x)^{-1} \approx 1+x$: (2 marks)

$$I_D \approx \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Define $\lambda$ and write final expression:** (2 marks)

Since $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \lambda V_{DS}$ (linearly proportional to $V_{DS}$ for KTU-level analysis):

$$\boxed{I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})}$$

**Units and meaning of $\lambda$:** $\lambda$ has units of **V⁻¹**. It is the Channel Length Modulation parameter that quantifies how strongly the channel length shrinks per volt of $V_{DS}$. Larger $\lambda$ implies stronger CLM (more pronounced in short-channel devices).

---

#### **Q.A (b)** For an n-MOSFET, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200\,\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 25$, $V_{th} = 0.4$ V, $V_{GS} = 1.2$ V, $\lambda = 0.02$ V⁻¹, $V_{DS} = 5$ V. Compute the drain current $I_D$, output resistance $r_o$, and the percentage increase in $I_D$ due to CLM. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $I_D$ using the CLM expression:** (3 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2\,(1+\lambda V_{DS}) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}\,(200\,\mu A/V^2)\,(25)\,(1.2 - 0.4)^2\,(1 + 0.02 \times 5) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}\,(200\,\mu A/V^2)\,(25)\,(0.8\,V)^2\,(1.10) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}\,(200)\,(25)\,(0.64)\,(1.10)\,\mu A \\
    &= 1760\,\mu A \\
    &= 1.76\,mA
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Output resistance $r_o$:** (2 marks)

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 1.76\,mA} = \frac{1}{35.2\,\mu A} \approx 28.41\,k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Percentage increase due to CLM:** (2 marks)

The ideal current (without CLM) is $I_D^{ideal} = 1600\,\mu A$:

$$\%\,\text{increase} = \frac{I_D - I_D^{ideal}}{I_D^{ideal}} \times 100 = \frac{1760 - 1600}{1600} \times 100 = 10\,\%$$

**Final Results:**
- $I_D = 1.76$ mA
- $r_o \approx 28.4$ kΩ
- CLM increases $I_D$ by **10 %**.

---

### KTU ESE Module Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Q.B (a)** With the help of a clear derivation, define Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT) for a high-K gate dielectric. Why is it preferred over physical thickness in modern process design? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of EOT:** (1 mark)

EOT is the equivalent thickness of a SiO₂ layer that would yield the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual high-K layer in use.

**Step 2 — Capacitance of the high-K layer:** (1 mark)

$$C_{high\text{-}K} = \frac{K_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance of the equivalent SiO₂ layer:** (1 mark)

$$C_{SiO_2} = \frac{K_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two capacitances and solve for EOT:** (2 marks)

$$\frac{K_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{K_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; EOT = \frac{K_{SiO_2}}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$$

$$\boxed{EOT = \frac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

**Step 5 — Why EOT is preferred over physical thickness:** (2 marks — any two of the following)

- **Scalability benchmarking:** EOT provides a uniform metric to compare different gate stack materials against the historical SiO₂ reference, enabling consistent technology-node scaling.
- **Electrostatic control:** The transconductance, threshold voltage, and short-channel behavior of the MOSFET depend on $C_{ox}$ — and $C_{ox}$ depends on EOT, not on physical thickness.
- **Leakage management:** Designers can keep the physical layer thick (suppressing tunneling) while shrinking the EOT (improving control) by using materials with higher $K$.

---

#### **Q.B (b)** A 3 nm thick HfO₂ ($K = 25$) layer is deposited on a thin interfacial SiO₂ layer of 0.7 nm. The HKMG stack is used as the gate dielectric of a 28 nm MOSFET. Calculate: (i) the total EOT of the stack, (ii) the equivalent gate capacitance per unit area, and (iii) the percentage reduction in physical thickness compared to a pure SiO₂ layer of the same EOT. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute EOT of the interfacial SiO₂ layer:** (1 mark)

For pure SiO₂, $EOT_{SiO_2} = t_{SiO_2} \cdot \dfrac{3.9}{3.9} = 0.7$ nm

**Step 2 — Compute EOT of the HfO₂ layer:** (1 mark)

$$EOT_{HfO_2} = \frac{3.9}{25} \cdot 3\,\text{nm} = 0.156 \times 3\,\text{nm} = 0.468\,\text{nm}$$

**Step 3 — Total EOT (series combination):** (1 mark)

$$EOT_{total} = 0.7 + 0.468 = 1.168\,\text{nm}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent gate capacitance per unit area:** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{ox} &= \frac{K_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT_{total}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,F/m}{1.168 \times 10^{-9}\,m} \\
       &= \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{1.168 \times 10^{-9}} \\
       &= 0.02956\,F/m^2 \\
       &\approx 29.56\,fF/\mu m^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Physical thickness of a pure SiO₂ layer with the same EOT:** (1 mark)

A pure SiO₂ layer with the same EOT (1.168 nm) is only **1.168 nm** thick, while the actual HKMG stack is $0.7 + 3 = 3.7$ nm thick.

**Step 6 — Percentage increase in physical thickness:** (1 mark)

$$\%\,\text{increase} = \frac{3.7 - 1.168}{1.168} \times 100 \approx 216.8\,\%$$

**Final Results:**
- $EOT_{total} = 1.168$ nm
- $C_{ox} \approx 29.56$ fF/μm²
- The HKMG stack is **~3.17× physically thicker** than an equivalent pure SiO₂ layer, dramatically reducing gate leakage current.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$:** Many students write only the ideal $I_D$ expression in saturation. Even a 3-mark question on CLM will lose 1–2 marks if $\lambda$ is omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up $K$ values:** Always use $K_{SiO_2} = 3.9$ when computing EOT. Using $K = 11.7$ (silicon value) is a frequent, fatal error.
> 3. **Series addition of physical thicknesses is wrong for EOT:** When dielectrics are stacked, EOTs add — not physical thicknesses. Board mark loss is common here.
> 4. **Units of $\lambda$:** Examiners do deduct marks if $\lambda$ is stated without the V⁻¹ unit.
> 5. **Confusing $C_{ox}$ with $C_{high\text{-}K}$:** $C_{ox}$ in MOSFET equations always refers to the **SiO₂-equivalent** capacitance computed using EOT, never the raw high-K capacitance.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** is a short-channel effect where $L_{eff}$ shrinks with $V_{DS}$ due to drain-depletion encroachment. Quantified by $\lambda$ (V⁻¹).
- **CLM-modified saturation current:** $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$
- **Output resistance:** $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ — a key parameter for analog CMOS gain.
- **High-K dielectrics** (e.g., HfO₂, $K \approx 25$) replace SiO₂ to maintain gate capacitance with thicker physical layers, reducing tunneling leakage.
- **EOT formula:** $\text{EOT} = \dfrac{3.9}{K_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K}$ — measures "electrical thickness" relative to SiO₂.
- For **stacked dielectrics**, EOTs add in series; physical thicknesses do not.
- **Industry milestones:** Hf-based high-K + metal gate introduced by Intel at the **45 nm node (2007)**.
- **KTU board benchmark values to memorize:** $K_{SiO_2} = 3.9$, $K_{HfO_2} \approx 25$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **Rule of thumb:** Smaller $L$ → larger $\lambda$ → more pronounced CLM → lower $r_o$ → lower analog gain.
- **Rule of thumb for high-K:** Higher $K$ → smaller EOT for the same physical thickness → better scaling and lower leakage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of High-K Gate Dielectrics on MOSFET Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT526 - NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Effects of High-K Gate Dielectrics on MOSFET Performance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the era of aggressive CMOS scaling, as the physical thickness of the conventional silicon dioxide (SiO₂) gate dielectric approaches the sub-2 nm regime, the resulting **Quantum Mechanical Tunneling (QMT)** current becomes catastrophically high, rendering the transistor unviable due to standby power dissipation. To circumvent this fundamental physical bottleneck without sacrificing electrostatic control of the channel, the industry replaced SiO₂ with **High-K Gate Dielectrics** — insulating materials possessing a dielectric constant (κ) significantly larger than that of SiO₂ (κ_SiO₂ ≈ 3.9). A high-K dielectric is functionally defined as a thin-film insulator material, typically a transition metal oxide (e.g., HfO₂, ZrO₂), which offers an **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)** of sub-1 nm while maintaining a considerably larger physical thickness (t_phys) to suppress direct tunneling. High-K dielectrics were first introduced in high-volume manufacturing at the **45 nm technology node (Intel, 2007)**, marking a paradigm shift in the gate stack architecture, eventually evolving into the modern **High-K Metal Gate (HKMG)** integration scheme. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A high-K dielectric is a material with a relative permittivity significantly higher than SiO₂ (κ ≫ 3.9), employed as the gate insulator to achieve a low EOT while keeping physical thickness large enough to mitigate gate leakage current. ## 1.2 The Physical Intuition: The "Water Pipe Pressure" Analogy Imagine a municipal water pipeline carrying water to your home: - **SiO₂ analogy:** A very *thin rubber pipe* that easily bursts under pressure (representing a high leakage tunneling current). - **High-K analogy:** A *much thicker, stronger pipe* (greater physical thickness) made of a special material (high-K) that can withstand the same pressure with negligible leakage. The magical property of the high-K material is that although the *pipe wall is physically thicker* (suppressing leakage), it still allows the *same electric field to reach the channel* because the material amplifies the capacitance per unit area — analogous to a thicker pipe that somehow produces the same water pressure at the faucet. ## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (HfO₂) | Significance | |-----------|--------|----------------------|--------------| | Relative Dielectric Constant | κ | 20 – 25 | ~6× greater than SiO₂ | | Band Gap | E_g | ~5.7 eV | Controls leakage & breakdown | | Conduction Band Offset | ΔE_C | ~1.5 eV (vs Si) | Barrier for electrons | | Valence Band Offset | ΔE_V | ~3.4 eV (vs Si) | Barrier for holes | | Breakdown Field | E_BD | ~6.7 MV/cm | Reliability metric | > [!NOTE] > **The Golden Rule of High-K Dielectrics:** The material should possess a *high κ*, *large E_g*, and *large band offsets with silicon* simultaneously. This combination is exceptionally rare, which is why Hf-based oxides dominate the industry. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Scaling Crisis of SiO₂ As per the ITRS roadmap, the continued Dennard scaling demanded the gate oxide thickness (t_ox) to shrink proportionally with channel length (L_G). The relationship governing this is the **silicon dioxide scaling limit**, where beyond t_ox < 2 nm: 1. **Direct Tunneling Current Density** rises exponentially as the potential barrier becomes transparent to electrons. 2. **Gate Leakage Power** becomes comparable to or exceeds the dynamic switching power. 3. **Boron Penetration** (from p⁺ poly-Si gates) into the channel region causes severe V_T instability. 4. **Polysilicon Gate Depletion** effect worsens, increasing the effective oxide thickness. The classic direct tunneling current density through a thin oxide is governed by the **Fowler-Nordheim / Direct Tunneling Model**: $$ J_{tunnel} = \frac{q^3 \cdot E_{ox}^{\,2}}{16\pi^2 \hbar \phi_B} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\sqrt{2m^*\,\phi_B^{\,3}}}{3\hbar q E_{ox}}\right) $$ where the **E-field across the oxide** is: $$ E_{ox} = \frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{V_{GS} - V_{FB} - \psi_s}{t_{ox}} $$ ## 2.2 The Concept of Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) The single most important parameter in high-K dielectric engineering is the **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)**. It is defined as the thickness of a hypothetical SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the high-K dielectric in use. ### Derivation of EOT The gate capacitance per unit area of a parallel-plate capacitor with dielectric constant κ and physical thickness t_phys is: $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}} $$ For SiO₂ (with κ_SiO₂ = 3.9) to produce the same capacitance, the required thickness (EOT) is: $$ EOT = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{phys} = \frac{3.9}{\kappa} \cdot t_{phys} $$ ### Worked Numerical Insight If we desire EOT = 1 nm using HfO₂ (κ ≈ 22): $$ t_{phys} = \frac{\kappa}{3.9} \cdot EOT = \frac{22}{3.9} \times 1 \approx 5.64 \text{ nm} $$ This means we obtain the *electrical performance* of 1 nm SiO₂ with a *physical insulator* that is 5.6 nm thick — eliminating tunneling while preserving drive current. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Description | Typical Units | |---------|-----------|-------------|---------------| | EOT Definition | $EOT = \dfrac{3.9}{\kappa} \cdot t_{phys}$ | Equivalent SiO₂ thickness | nm | | Physical Thickness | $t_{phys} = \dfrac{\kappa}{3.9} \cdot EOT$ | Actual film thickness | nm | | Gate Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\kappa \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² | | Drive Current (Long-Channel) | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | Saturation drain current | A | | Tunneling Probability | $T \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^*\phi_B \, t_{phys}}\right)$ | WKB tunneling estimate | dimensionless | | Effective Capacitor Stack | $\dfrac{1}{C_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{C_{HK}} + \dfrac{1}{C_{IL}}$ | HKMG stack includes interfacial layer | – | | Total EOT | $EOT_{total} = EOT_{HK} + EOT_{IL}$ | Additive contribution of interfacial layer | nm | | Transconductance | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)$ | Gain parameter | S | | Subthreshold Slope | $S = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left(1 + \dfrac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | Switching sharpness | mV/decade | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The EOT is *additive across dielectric stacks*. The total EOT of a HKMG structure equals the sum of the EOT of the high-K layer and the EOT of the thin interfacial SiO₂ (or SiON) layer. Therefore, the IL layer must remain as thin as possible (~0.5 nm) to preserve the benefit of the high-K material. ## 2.4 Why High-K Dielectrics Are Transformative — The Engineering Impact 1. **Leakage Suppression:** Direct tunneling decreases *exponentially* with barrier thickness. Increasing t_phys from 1 nm to 5 nm reduces leakage current by **2–3 orders of magnitude (~100–1000×)**. 2. **Drive Current Preservation:** Since C_ox is maintained via high κ, the drive current $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ is preserved. 3. **Power-Performance Trade-off Recovery:** Static (leakage) power is dramatically reduced while dynamic (switching) performance is retained. 4. **Enabling Further Scaling:** The technology node can advance from 65 nm through 22 nm, 14 nm, and beyond, with EOT values continuing to scale. 5. **Boron Diffusion Barrier:** High-K materials act as effective barriers to boron penetration, stabilizing threshold voltage in PMOS devices. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Quantitative Comparison of Leakage Current **Objective:** Compare the gate leakage current through a 1 nm SiO₂ layer versus a HfO₂ layer providing the same EOT of 1 nm. **Given:** - Electron effective mass in SiO₂: m*_(SiO₂) ≈ 0.5 m₀ - Electron effective mass in HfO₂: m*_(HfO₂) ≈ 0.17 m₀ - Conduction band offset of SiO₂ vs Si: φ_B(SiO₂) ≈ 3.15 eV - Conduction band offset of HfO₂ vs Si: φ_B(HfO₂) ≈ 1.5 eV - m₀ = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, ħ = 1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s - 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J - V_GS – V_FB – ψ_s ≈ 1 V (typical inversion bias) ### Step 1: Compute the Physical Thickness of HfO₂ $$ t_{phys}(HfO_2) = \frac{\kappa_{HfO_2}}{\kappa_{SiO_2}} \times EOT = \frac{22}{3.9} \times 1 \text{ nm} = 5.64 \text{ nm} $$ ### Step 2: Compute the Electric Field Across Each Oxide For the **SiO₂ case** (t_phys = 1 nm = 1 × 10⁻⁹ m): $$ E_{ox}(SiO_2) = \frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 1 \times 10^{9} \text{ V/m} $$ For the **HfO₂ case** (t_phys = 5.64 nm): $$ E_{ox}(HfO_2) = \frac{V_{ox}}{t_{phys}} = \frac{1}{5.64 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.77 \times 10^{8} \text{ V/m} $$ ### Step 3: Compute the Direct Tunneling Probability using the WKB Approximation The Wentzel-Kramers-Brillouin (WKB) tunneling probability is: $$ T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \int_0^{t_{phys}} \kappa(x)\, dx \right) $$ For a rectangular barrier, this simplifies to: $$ T \approx \exp\!\left(- \frac{2 t_{phys}}{\hbar} \sqrt{2 m^* \phi_B}\right) $$ **Case A: SiO₂ (1 nm)** $$ \sqrt{2 m^*_{SiO_2} \phi_B} = \sqrt{2 \times (0.5 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (3.15 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19})} $$ $$ = \sqrt{2 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.045 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{4.596 \times 10^{-49}} = 6.78 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg}^{1/2} \text{J}^{1/2} $$ $$ T_{SiO_2} \approx \exp\!\left(- \frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} \times 6.78 \times 10^{-25}\right) = \exp(-12.85) \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Case B: HfO₂ (5.64 nm)** $$ \sqrt{2 m^*_{HfO_2} \phi_B} = \sqrt{2 \times (0.17 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19})} $$ $$ = \sqrt{2 \times 1.549 \times 10^{-31} \times 2.403 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{7.443 \times 10^{-50}} = 2.728 \times 10^{-25} $$ $$ T_{HfO_2} \approx \exp\!\left(- \frac{2 \times 5.64 \times 10^{-9}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} \times 2.728 \times 10^{-25}\right) = \exp(-29.16) \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-13} $$ ### Step 4: Compute the Leakage Current Reduction Ratio $$ \frac{J_{HfO_2}}{J_{SiO_2}} \approx \frac{T_{HfO_2}}{T_{SiO_2}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-13}}{2.6 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-8} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Conclusion:** The leakage current through the HfO₂ stack is approximately **~10⁷ times (ten million times) lower** than the equivalent SiO₂ stack, despite delivering the *same gate capacitance and drive current*. This is the heart of why HKMG is the cornerstone of modern CMOS technology. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Subthreshold Swing Improvement The subthreshold swing (SS) quantifies the gate voltage required to change the drain current by one decade in the weak inversion regime. **Given Formula:** $$ SS = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right) = 60 \text{ mV/dec} \times \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right) $$ **Step 1: Effect of Increasing C_ox via High-K Dielectric** The depletion capacitance (C_dep) of the silicon substrate is fixed by doping and is typically ~C_dep ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻² F/m². The oxide capacitance is: $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}} $$ For **SiO₂ (1 nm):** $$ C_{ox}(SiO_2) = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 $$ For **HfO₂ (5.64 nm):** $$ C_{ox}(HfO_2) = \frac{22 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{5.64 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Confirm Capacitance Equivalence** Both C_ox values are identical (3.45 × 10⁻² F/m²) — verifying the EOT design principle. The subthreshold swing is therefore *not degraded* by switching to a physically thicker high-K dielectric. **Step 3: Subthreshold Swing Value** $$ SS = 60 \text{ mV/dec} \times \left(1 + \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{3.45 \times 10^{-2}}\right) = 60 \times 1.435 \approx 86 \text{ mV/dec} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The SS remains identical between SiO₂ and HfO₂ at the same EOT because the *oxide capacitance is the controlling parameter*. This is precisely why EOT — and not physical thickness — is the relevant metric for electrostatic integrity. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: The Interfacial Layer Penalty **Scenario:** In a real HKMG stack, a thin (~0.5 nm) SiO₂ interfacial layer (IL) forms between HfO₂ and the Si substrate. Compute the total EOT. **Given:** - HfO₂ physical thickness: t_HK = 4 nm, κ_HK = 22 - IL (SiO₂) physical thickness: t_IL = 0.5 nm, κ_IL = 3.9 **Step 1: EOT of High-K Layer** $$ EOT_{HK} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{HK}} \cdot t_{HK} = \frac{3.9}{22} \times 4 = 0.709 \text{ nm} $$ **Step 2: EOT of Interfacial Layer** $$ EOT_{IL} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{IL}} \cdot t_{IL} = \frac{3.9}{3.9} \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ nm} $$ **Step 3: Total EOT (Additive Capacitance Model)** $$ EOT_{total} = EOT_{HK} + EOT_{IL} = 0.709 + 0.5 = 1.209 \text{ nm} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Common Pitfall:** A frequently made mistake in KTU examinations is to *forget* the contribution of the interfacial SiO₂ layer. The total EOT is **never** equal to EOT_HK alone. The IL alone contributes 0.5 nm — meaning the HKMG stack can never beat the EOT of a pure-SiO₂ IL of equivalent physical thickness. This is precisely why the industry has spent over a decade engineering **IL-free or IL-engineered stacks** (e.g., LaSiO_x capping layers, plasma nitridation). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effects of High-K on MOSFET Performance | Performance Parameter | Effect of High-K Dielectric | Underlying Physics | Trade-off | |----------------------|------------------------------|-------------------|-----------| | **Gate Leakage (I_g)** | ↓ Decreases by 2–4 orders | Exponentially suppressed tunneling (∝ t_phys) | None | | **Drive Current (I_on)** | ≈ Preserved at same EOT | C_ox maintained through high κ | None | | **Subthreshold Swing (SS)** | ≈ Unchanged | Depends only on C_ox/C_dep ratio | None | | **Threshold Voltage (V_T)** | Shifted + instability | Fermi-level pinning, fixed charges | Manageable via metal gate | | **Channel Mobility (μ_eff)** | ↓ Degraded 20–50% | Remote phonon scattering, soft optical phonons | Process optimization | | **NBTI / PBTI Reliability** | ↑ Worsened for PMOS / NMOS | High trap density in HfO₂ | Annealing, N incorporation | | **DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering)** | Improved (slight) | Better electrostatic integrity | None | | **Short-Channel Effects (SCE)** | Mitigated | Higher effective channel control | None | | **Gate Capacitance Density** | ↑ Higher per unit area | High κ enables thinner EOT | None | | **Off-State Power (P_off)** | ↓ Drastically reduced | Suppressed I_off | None | | **Static Noise Margin (SRAM)** | ↑ Improved | Lower variability in V_T | Process maturity required | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Evolution from SiO₂ to HKMG Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CMOS Scaling Demand
L_G reduces to sub-22nm] --> B[SiO2 Thickness
Less than 2nm] B --> C[Exponential Tunneling
Leakage Increases] C --> D[Standby Power Crisis
Thermal Runaway] D --> E[Industry Solution
High-K Dielectric Adoption] E --> F1[Material HfO2
kappa equals 22] E --> F2[Material HfSiON
kappa equals 12] E --> F3[Material ZrO2
kappa equals 25] E --> F4[Material La2O3
kappa equals 30] F1 --> G[HKMG Gate Stack
Physical Thickness rises
EOT remains sub-1nm] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H[Reduced Gate Leakage
10 to the power 3 to 10 to the power 5] G --> I[Preserved Drive Current
and Subthreshold Swing] G --> J[Process Challenges
Mobility Penalty and V_T Instability] J --> K[Advanced Engineering
Remote Scattering Mitigation
Metal Gate Work-Function Tuning] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — HKMG MOSFET Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Substrate[Silicon Substrate Region] A1[P-type Si Bulk] --> A2[Inversion Channel Region] A2 --> A3[N-type Source and Drain] end subgraph Stack[Gate Stack Vertical Architecture] B1[Metal Gate Electrode - TiN or TaN] --> B2[High-K Dielectric Layer - HfO2] B2 --> B3[Interfacial Layer IL - SiO2 or SiON] B3 --> A2 end subgraph Spacer[Sidewall and Contact Region] C1[Spacer Nitride Sidewall] --> C2[Source and Drain Contacts] C2 --> A3 end subgraph Params[Critical EOT Parameters] D1[t_HK equals 4nm] --> D2[EOT_HK equals 0.71nm] D3[t_IL equals 0.5nm] --> D4[EOT_IL equals 0.5nm] D2 --> D5[EOT_Total equals 1.21nm] D4 --> D5 end ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram — HKMG Integration (Gate-Last Scheme) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1: Form Dummy Poly-Si Gate] --> B[Step 2: Deposit HfO2 via ALD] B --> C[Step 3: Form Thin SiO2 IL via Chemical Oxidation] C --> D[Step 4: Deposit TiN Metal Gate via PVD] D --> E[Step 5: Anneal for Crystallization Control] E --> F[Step 6: Remove Dummy Gate] F --> G[Step 7: Fill with Replacement Metal Gate] G --> H[Step 8: Final Contact and BEOL Processing] H --> I[Final HKMG MOSFET
Leakage 10 to the power 4 lower] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Diagram — Why HfO₂ Dominates ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Candidate High-K Material] --> B{k Greater than 20} B -- Yes --> C{Band Gap Greater than 5eV} B -- No --> D[Rejected Low-k] C -- Yes --> E{Conduction Band Offset Greater than 1eV} C -- No --> F[Rejected Narrow Gap] E -- Yes --> G{Thermal Stability with Si} E -- No --> H[Rejected Band Offset] G -- Yes --> I[HfO2 Selected as Industry Standard] G -- No --> J[Engineered HfSiO Alternatives] I --> K[Used at 45nm and Beyond] J --> L[Used for Specialized Nodes] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) and explain its significance in high-K dielectric engineering. [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme] **Model Answer:** The Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) is the thickness of a hypothetical SiO₂ layer that would yield the same gate capacitance per unit area as the high-K dielectric film under consideration. Mathematically: $$ EOT = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high\text{-}K}} \cdot t_{phys} = \frac{3.9}{\kappa} \cdot t_{phys} $$ **Significance in MOSFET Scaling:** 1. It allows direct comparison between SiO₂ and high-K stacks in terms of their *electrostatic* performance, independent of physical thickness. 2. It is the *key design parameter* used in ITRS roadmaps for technology node scaling, replacing physical oxide thickness. 3. It quantifies the *electrical thickness* of the gate insulator — the smaller the EOT, the stronger the gate's electrostatic control over the channel. 4. It enables the usage of physically thicker dielectrics (suppressing tunneling) while preserving the capacitive coupling equivalent to a much thinner SiO₂ layer. ### Question 2: List any four essential material properties required for a gate dielectric to be classified as a "high-K" material suitable for advanced CMOS. [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme] **Model Answer:** The four essential properties are: 1. **High Dielectric Constant (κ ≫ 3.9):** Typically κ > 15, enabling low EOT at large physical thickness. 2. **Large Band Gap (E_g > 5 eV):** Essential to maintain low OFF-state leakage and high breakdown field. 3. **Large Conduction and Valence Band Offsets (ΔE_C, ΔE_V > 1 eV) with Silicon:** To maintain a sufficient energy barrier against carrier injection (Schottky emission, Fowler-Nordheim tunneling). 4. **Thermodynamic and Chemical Stability with Si Substrate:** The material should not react with Si at high processing temperatures (~1000 °C) to form undesirable interfacial layers (e.g., silicates). Additional desirable properties include: low interface trap density (D_it < 10¹⁰ cm⁻²·eV⁻¹), high breakdown field, low leakage current, and compatibility with complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor processing. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the physical reasons why SiO₂ as a gate dielectric became unsustainable beyond the 90 nm technology node. With a clear cross-sectional sketch, explain how the introduction of high-K dielectrics resolved the issue. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A MOSFET is designed with HfO₂ (κ = 22) as the gate dielectric. If the Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) requirement is 0.9 nm, determine: (i) the required physical thickness of HfO₂, (ii) the resulting oxide capacitance per unit area, and (iii) the ratio of leakage current density if the tunneling current scales as $\exp(-t_{phys})$. Take SiO₂ reference with t_phys = 0.9 nm. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) The SiO₂ scaling crisis arose due to multiple quantum-mechanical and material limitations: 1. **Direct Tunneling Dominance:** For t_ox < 2 nm, the rectangular potential barrier becomes thin enough for direct (non-Fowler-Nordheim) tunneling of electrons. Leakage current rises *exponentially* with decreasing t_ox, reaching > 100 A/cm² at t_ox = 1 nm, which is unacceptable for low-power operation. 2. **Boron Penetration:** In p⁺ poly-Si gate PMOS devices, boron diffuses through ultra-thin SiO₂ into the channel, causing V_T instability and degradation of NBTI (Negative Bias Temperature Instability) reliability. 3. **Polysilicon Gate Depletion:** For ultra-thin oxides, the poly-Si gate depletion region becomes comparable to t_ox, effectively increasing the EOT and degrading transconductance. 4. **Power Wall:** Static (leakage) power consumption reaches parity with dynamic switching power, violating the classical Dennard scaling power model. **Resolution via High-K Dielectrics:** The introduction of HfO₂ (or HfSiON) with κ ≈ 20–22 enabled a physically thick film (t_phys ≈ 5–6 nm) to deliver an EOT < 1 nm. The exponential dependence of tunneling on t_phys reduced leakage by 2–4 orders of magnitude while preserving C_ox and hence the drive current. The HKMG architecture further eliminated boron penetration and gate depletion effects. ``` Gate-Stack Schematic (Verbal Description): Metal Gate (TiN) │ ▼ [ HfO₂ Layer | t_phys ≈ 5.6 nm | κ = 22 ] │ ▼ [ Interfacial SiO₂ | t ≈ 0.5 nm ] │ ▼ Si Substrate (Channel) ``` #### Model Solution for (b) **(i) Physical Thickness of HfO₂:** $$ t_{phys} = \frac{\kappa_{HK}}{\kappa_{SiO_2}} \cdot EOT = \frac{22}{3.9} \times 0.9 = 5.077 \text{ nm} $$ **(ii) Oxide Capacitance per Unit Area:** $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{HK} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}} = \frac{22 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{5.077 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.836 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 $$ Cross-verification using EOT: $$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{0.9 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.836 \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/m}^2 \quad \checkmark $$ **(iii) Leakage Current Ratio (WKB Estimate):** Using the tunneling probability scaling, the ratio of leakage current densities is approximated as: $$ \frac{J_{HK}}{J_{SiO_2}} \approx \exp\!\left(-K(t_{HK}-t_{ref})\right) $$ For a representative decay constant K ≈ 1.0 nm⁻¹ (typical for direct tunneling): $$ \frac{J_{HK}}{J_{SiO_2}} = \exp\!\left(-1.0 \times (5.077 - 0.9)\right) = \exp(-4.18) \approx 0.0153 $$ The leakage current density is reduced by a factor of approximately **65× (1.53%)** compared to a 0.9 nm SiO₂ dielectric. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common mistake is to assume the leakage current is reduced by a *factor of 22* (i.e., the ratio of dielectric constants). The actual reduction is *exponential* in physical thickness — a much more dramatic reduction. Marks will be deducted if the exponential nature of tunneling is ignored. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the role of the Interfacial Layer (IL) in a High-K Metal Gate (HKMG) stack. With a clear mathematical formulation, show that the total EOT of an HKMG stack is the sum of the EOT contributions of the high-K layer and the IL. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the phenomenon of Remote Phonon Scattering and explain how it degrades carrier mobility in high-K based MOSFETs. What are the engineering strategies adopted to mitigate this effect? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **Role of the Interfacial Layer (IL):** In an HKMG stack, a thin (~0.4–0.7 nm) layer of SiO₂ or SiON, known as the Interfacial Layer (IL), is deliberately formed between the high-K dielectric and the silicon substrate. Its roles are: 1. **Chemical Passivation:** It passivates the silicon surface dangling bonds, reducing interface trap density (D_it) to acceptable levels (~10¹⁰ cm⁻²·eV⁻¹). 2. **Crystallization Barrier:** It prevents the nucleation and grain growth of the high-K layer, maintaining its amorphous phase and ensuring uniform film properties. 3. **Defect Mitigation:** It minimizes the density of oxygen vacancies and other bulk traps in the high-K film. 4. **Quality Anchor:** Without the IL, direct deposition of HfO₂ on Si leads to severe Fermi-level pinning, high D_it, and poor electron mobility. **Mathematical Derivation of Total EOT (Additive Model):** The IL and high-K layers act as *two capacitors in series*: $$ \frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{C_{IL}} + \frac{1}{C_{HK}} $$ Expressing each capacitance as: $$ C_{IL} = \frac{\kappa_{IL}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{IL}}, \quad C_{HK} = \frac{\kappa_{HK}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{HK}} $$ The equivalent oxide thickness of the entire stack is defined such that: $$ C_{total} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT_{total}} $$ Substituting and simplifying: $$ \frac{EOT_{total}}{\kappa_{SiO_2}} = \frac{t_{IL}}{\kappa_{IL}} + \frac{t_{HK}}{\kappa_{HK}} $$ Multiplying both sides by κ_SiO₂: $$ EOT_{total} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{IL}} \cdot t_{IL} + \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{HK}} \cdot t_{HK} = EOT_{IL} + EOT_{HK} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The total EOT is the *arithmetic sum* of the EOTs of the individual layers. This simple additive property is the foundation of HKMG stack design and is heavily tested in KTU examinations. #### Model Solution for (b) **Remote Phonon Scattering (RPS) — Physical Mechanism:** In conventional SiO₂-based MOSFETs, the gate insulator is a high-frequency optical-phonon material with energy ℏω_SiO₂ ≈ 63 meV. The optical phonon modes are *localized* within the SiO₂ film, and their evanescent tail decays rapidly into the channel — hence, they cause minimal carrier scattering. In contrast, **high-K materials such as HfO₂ possess low-energy optical phonon modes** (ℏω_HK ≈ 12–20 meV) due to the heavier atomic mass of Hf/Zr and the more polarizable metal-oxygen bonds. These *soft* optical phonons: 1. Have **longer wavelengths** and decay more gradually into the silicon channel. 2. Extend several nanometers into the inversion layer, reaching the centroid of the carrier distribution. 3. **Couple strongly with channel electrons and holes**, scattering them and reducing mobility. The mobility degradation due to RPS is typically modeled as: $$ \frac{1}{\mu_{eff}} = \frac{1}{\mu_{phonon}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{RPS}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{Coulomb}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{SR}} $$ where μ_RPS can reduce the effective mobility by 20–50% relative to the universal Si/SiO₂ mobility curve. **Engineering Strategies to Mitigate RPS:** 1. **Strain Engineering:** Application of bi-axial or uni-axial strain (SiGe source/drain, contact etch stop layer) to enhance carrier transport velocity. 2. **Higher Channel Doping Profile Optimization:** Designing retrograde or halo doping to keep carriers away from the interface. 3. **Capping Layer Engineering:** Introducing La₂O₃, Al₂O₃, or Dy₂O₃ capping layers that shift the V_T and partially screen the remote phonon field. 4. **Aluminum Incorporation:** Forming HfAlO alloys, which raise the average optical phonon energy and reduce the RPS coefficient. 5. **Channel Orientation Engineering:** Use of (100) surface orientation for NMOS to minimize surface roughness scattering complementing RPS. 6. **High-K Material Selection:** Substituting HfO₂ with La₂O₃ or Y₂O₃-based dielectrics, which possess higher phonon energies. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often *omit the additive EOT relation* in part (a) and instead give only a verbal description — losing 3–4 marks. The derivation showing that $EOT_{total} = EOT_{IL} + EOT_{HK}$ must be explicitly demonstrated for full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Module 1** - ✅ **EOT Definition:** $EOT = \frac{3.9}{\kappa} \cdot t_{phys}$ — the single most important formula in this topic. - ✅ **Why High-K:** SiO₂ < 2 nm causes exponential tunneling leakage, which high-K resolves by physical thickness scaling. - ✅ **Tunneling Exponent:** Direct tunneling probability scales as $\exp(-t_{phys})$ — thicker physical layer exponentially reduces leakage. - ✅ **Industry Material:** HfO₂ (κ ≈ 22) is the dominant choice; alternatives include HfSiON, ZrO₂, La₂O₃, Al₂O₃. - ✅ **First Adoption:** Intel's 45 nm logic process (2007) was the first high-volume HKMG production. - ✅ **Additive EOT Rule:** $EOT_{total} = EOT_{HK} + EOT_{IL}$ — capacitors in series. - ✅ **Drive Current Preserved:** At constant EOT, $C_{ox}$ and $I_{D,sat}$ remain unchanged when switching from SiO₂ to high-K. - ✅ **Mobility Penalty:** Remote Phonon Scattering from low-energy Hf-O vibrational modes reduces μ_eff by 20–50%. - ✅ **Threshold Voltage Instability:** Fermi-level pinning by HfO₂ requires dual metal gate work-function tuning (TiN, TaN, TiAlC). - ✅ **Reliability Concerns:** PBTI (NMOS) and NBTI (PMOS) worsen with high-K — mitigated by N incorporation, post-deposition anneal. - ✅ **Equivalent Capacitance:** HKMG stack = series of two dielectrics (IL + HK), so $C_{total} = \left(1/C_{IL} + 1/C_{HK}\right)^{-1}$. - ✅ **Process Routes:** Gate-First vs. Gate-Last (Replacement Metal Gate) — the latter dominates for advanced nodes. - ✅ **Band Offset Constraint:** ΔE_C > 1 eV is required to suppress Schottky emission of electrons into the high-K layer. - ✅ **Interfacial Layer Necessity:** A thin IL is essential for D_it passivation, even though it adds to total EOT. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical EOT problems, *always* show the working formula explicitly. In descriptive questions, *always* anchor your answer to the underlying physical mechanism (tunneling, scattering, band offsets) rather than simply stating outcomes. This 2-pronged approach is the hallmark of a high-scoring KTU answer script.
